option_b: The buyer gives the seller a mortgage and note as part of the purchase price of the property
option_c: A land trust holds title to the property while the buyer makes periodic installment payments to the seller
option_d: The seller uses the purchase money obtained from the buyer's mortgage loan to repay the seller's outstanding loan balance
feedback: With a purchase money mortgage, the borrower gives a mortgage and note to the seller to finance some or all of the purchase price of the property. The seller in this case is said to "take back" a note, or to "carry paper," on the property

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What is the most significant test of whether an item has become a fixture?
option_d: Adaptability of the item to the property use
feedback: The primary test is the parties’ intent, supported by attachment and adaptation factors.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A tenant in a commercial building installed a large sign, which was anchored. This would be considered a/an:
feedback: Trade fixtures remain the tenant’s property and must be removed before lease end.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following items is most likely considered to be personal property?
feedback: Above-ground pools are movable and therefore personal property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Each of the following is a freehold estate except which one?
option_c: A life estate pur autre vie
option_d: A lease for 50 years on a hunting lodge
feedback: Leasehold estates, even long ones, are not freehold estates.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is an example of a leasehold estate?
option_c: Fee simple subject to a condition subsequent
feedback: Leaseholds are non-freehold estates, and an estate for years is a fixed-term leasehold.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: During the term of the lease, the lessor holds what interest?
feedback: The landlord retains a reversionary interest until the lease ends.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When the lessee dies which of the following leasehold estates comes to an end?
feedback: Tenancy at will ends upon death of either party.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The type of survey system that references degrees is known as:
feedback: Metes and bounds use compass bearings and distances.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Joint tenancy has all the characteristics except which of the following?
option_b: Joint tenants have equal rights of possession.
option_c: Heirs of joint tenants retain a survivorship interest.
option_d: Joint tenants receive their interest by the same document.
feedback: In joint tenancy, surviving owners, not heirs, get the deceased’s interest.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which type of business entity must generate at least 75% of the income from real estate:
feedback: REITs must meet this threshold to maintain tax advantages.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Sally and Mickey plan to buy a home together but want to will their property to their own children from previous marriages. How should they hold title?
feedback: Tenants in common allows each owner to will their share separately.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Barry is borrowing 300,000 at 5% for 30 years. The loan constant for this loan is 5.37. What will Barry have to pay for principal and interest each month?
feedback: $300,000/1,000 is 300 units times the loan constant of 5.37 = $1,611

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: An artificial legal entity, created by state laws, which may own property in severalty, is known as a:
feedback: A corporation is a legal “person” that can own property independently.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Ralph sold his house to Oscar, who did not record the deed, but took residency there. Ralph then sold the same property to Larry, who reviewed the county recorder's records, but did not examine the property. Ralph gave Larry a deed, which Larry recorded. Which of the following would be true concerning title to the property?
option_b: Larry and Oscar are co-owners of the property.
option_c: Larry now owns the property, because he recorded his deed first.
option_d: Larry has recourse against Oscar for failure to record.
feedback: In situations like this, the first person to possess or record gets the property. Possession of the property provides constructive notice, so Oscar maintains possession even though he wasn't the first to record.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What is a will written and executed entirely in the handwriting of the testator (person creating the will)?
feedback: A holographic will is defined as a will written and executed entirely in the handwriting of the testator (person making the will). Typically, these days, a will is typed and exists as a digital document/printout, but holographic wills are hand-written (they are rare, and only some states recognize them as legitimate).

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A riparian owner owns land which borders on:
feedback: Riparian Rights are water rights in regard to a flowing body of water like a river or a stream. Littoral rights are in regards to bodies of water that do not flow, such as a lake or ocean.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A project that is "turn key" is said to be:
feedback: "Turnkey project" is term used for a property that is ready for occupancy, ready to be put into immediate use. It is equipped with, or includes, everything needed to operate it.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: In order to avoid successor liability in the buying of a business, which of the following should be filed?
feedback: The buyer or lessee of an existing business might have "successor liability" to the Board of Equalization for an unpaid sales tax. The problem might be avoided by obtaining a certificate of clearance.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: In which of the following situations would property always be reassessed?
option_d: When a property has been rehabilitated or remodeled
feedback: Property is always reassessed upon sale. This reassessment usually results in a supplemental tax bill. The payment of any increase in property tax due to the reassessment and any resulting supplemental tax bill would then become the responsibility of the buyer.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Property taxes are described as "ad valorem" taxes. "Ad valorem" most nearly means:
feedback: By definition, property tax is assessed according to value, so it's an "ad valorem" tax.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following would be an example of "boot," for income tax purposes?
option_a: A decrease in real estate property taxes
option_c: Debt relief from a mortgage in a 1031 exchange
feedback: The term "boot" is not used in the Internal Revenue Code or the Regulations, but is commonly used in discussing the tax consequences of a Section 1031 tax-deferred exchange. For example, if a property worth $150,000 is exchanged for a property worth $145,000 plus $5000 cash, the cash is considered the boot. Receipt of boot doesn't disqualify an exchange, but it may result in a taxable gain.

subject: A4 Appraisal and Valuation
question_text: Expensive house appreciated less than neighbors → principle of:
feedback: Regression = value of expensive property pulled down by neighbors.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: All investors desire their investments to increase in value. However,
option_a: the degree of return is inversely related to the degree of risk
option_b: the more the investor stands to gain, the greater the risk that the investor may lose
option_c: investments requiring intense management have lesser returns
option_d: the more liquid an investment is, the greater the chances are that the investment will not appreciate
feedback: The general rule in investments is that the safer the investment, the more slowly it gains in value. The more you want it to gain, and the more quickly, the more you must risk losing it. Reward in investing corresponds directly to the degree of risk

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Two of the rewards that investments offer are
feedback: The basic financial rewards of an investment include income, appreciation in asset value, positive borrowing leverage, and tax benefits

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following items can be deducted for income tax purposes on real estate which is held as personal residence:
option_b: Losses which occur if the property is sold
option_d: Expenses of repair, maintenance, and care
feedback: Allowable deductions for personal residence include interest, taxes, and some uninsured casualty losses. If the owner of a condominium pays interest on a mortgage for his share of common areas, he can deduct that interest on his income tax return.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Destruction of subject property terminates the agency relationship by:
option_a: Consent of the agent and principal
feedback: Loss of the property ends the agency automatically by law.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: An investor invests in fifteen diversified bond funds. This is an example of an investment in
feedback: A debt investment is one in which an investor buys a debt instrument. Examples are bonds, notes, mortgages, and bond mutual funds. The basic reward comes in the form of interest. Debt investments are usually riskier than money investments and less risky than stocks or real estate

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: A real estate investment can take a long period of time to sell. For the investor, this means that real estate is
feedback: Real estate is by nature more difficult to convert to cash than many other types of investment. Compare the selling of a home with the ease of drawing money out of a bank account or selling a stock

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Compared to a stock portfolio, a real estate investment would be considered
feedback: Real estate tends to require a high degree of investor involvement in management of the investment. Even raw land requires some degree of maintenance to preserve its value. Improved properties often require extensive management, including repairs, maintenance, onsite leasing, tenant relations, security, and fiscal management

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Six investors purchase a shopping center. One investor manages the tenants and another handles the marketing and leasing. Two investors manage accounting and finance, and the remaining two run the management office. This is a possible example of
feedback: A general partnership is a syndicate in which all members participate equally in managing the investment and in the profits or losses it generates

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Taxable income produced by an income property is
option_a: gross income minus expenses plus land and building depreciation
option_b: gross income minus expenses minus land and building depreciation
option_c: gross income minus building depreciation plus land depreciation
option_d: gross income minus expenses minus building depreciation
feedback: Taxable income from investment real estate is the gross income received minus any expenses, deductions or exclusions that current tax law allows. Depreciation of land is not allowed

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: As a general rule, in deriving taxable income on an investment property, it is legal to
option_a: deduct principal and interest payments from income
option_d: deduct principal and interest payments from income and capital gain
feedback: Taxable income is net operating income minus all allowable deductions. The interest portion of debt service is allowed and is deducted from Net Operating Income as interest expense. Principal repayment is not an allowable deduction on investment properties. Capital gain derivation does not involve interest or principal payments

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Which of the following is true of the tax treatment of a principal residence?
option_a: The owner may deduct the property's interest and principal from ordinary income
option_b: The owner may depreciate the property and deduct depreciation expenses
option_c: The owner can deduct any capital gain when the property is sold
option_d: The owner may be able to exclude capital gain from taxable income when the property is sold
feedback: When real estate, whether non-income or income, is sold, a taxable event occurs. If the sale proceeds exceed the original cost of the investment, subject to some adjustments, there is a capital gain that is subject to tax. Tax law provides an exclusion of $250,000 for an individual taxpayer and $500,000 for married taxpayers filing jointly. The exclusion of gain from sale of a residence can be claimed every two years, provided the taxpayer:  1. owned the property for at least two years during the five years preceding the date of sale;  2. used the property as principal residence for a total of two years during that five-year period;  3. has waited two years since the last use of the exclusion for any sale

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: An investment property seller pays $14,000 in closing costs. These costs
option_a: may be deducted from personal income
option_b: may be deducted from the property's income
option_c: may be deducted from the sale price for gains tax purposes
option_d: may be deducted from the adjusted basis for gains tax purposes
feedback: Selling costs include such expenses as brokerage commissions, relevant advertising, legal fees, seller-paid points and other closing costs. These costs are deducted from the selling price to get the amount realized, which is reduced by the adjusted basis to calculate gain on sale for gains tax purposes

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A broker accidentally deposited the client’s earnest money into his or her personal checking account. The broker is guilty of
feedback: Depositing client funds into a personal account is grounds for the revocation or suspension of a real estate license

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A broker used a client’s earnest money to purchase an ocean cruise. The broker will be found guilty of
feedback: Using or spending funds for personal gain or for other uses, not intended for the client’s purpose, is grounds for revocation or suspension of a real estate license

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Voluntary alienation refers to:
feedback: Voluntary alienation is the transfer of real property (or some other asset) through a person's intentional actions and of their own free will.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: According to income tax laws, which of the following is true about depreciation of land?
option_a: The ACRS method of depreciation can be used when depreciating land
option_c: Land is considered to be 25% of the total value depreciated
option_d: Land has a residual value but improvements do not
feedback: An Income property owner is allowed to take Depreciation Deductions on his property, but in order to qualify for this type of Deduction, he must have made some kind of improvement or improvements to the property in question. [The Depreciation Deduction is based on the cost of the improvements that were made. The LAND does not depreciate]

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When a real estate broker speaks of a "tax shelter," they are referring to:
feedback: When a real estate broker speaks of a "tax shelter," they are referring to a means of shielding a person from income taxes.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Under federal income tax law, the "basis" of real property is the property's:
option_b: purchase price minus any existing assumed loans.
feedback: The "cost basis" of a property is the purchase price or investment amount, including commissions and other expenses. Cost basis is used to determine capital gains and capital losses for tax purposes.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: In order for an agreement for a transfer of real property to be binding on the buyer and seller it must:
feedback: There must be some type of offer and acceptance by the parties for the contract to be binding. [For Example, the transfer of real property by contract must have an offer and acceptance to be binding.]

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following deeds contains no expressed or implied warranties?
feedback: A Quitclaim Deed has NO warranties. As opposed to a Grant Deed which has two implied warranties: Number 1: That the Grantor has not already conveyed title to another person; Number 2: That the Estate itself is free from any undisclosed Encumbrances. A Quitclaim Deed Simply conveys the present interest, rights, and title of the Grantor; with no Covenants.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Legally speaking, an "attachment" is a(n):
feedback: An attachment is a legal process used to designate specific property owned by a debtor to be transferred to a creditor to cover the cost of the debt. Attachments are considered liens against property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The "marginal tax rate" is defined as:
option_c: The tax rate which is applied to the last dollar earned
option_d: The tax rate which is applied to the next dollar earned
feedback: Marginal Tax Rate is the tax rate that is applied to the next dollar of taxable earned income.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The cost basis of a single family residence can be adjusted for which of the following?
feedback: An example of an adjusted cost basis would be the addition of a patio on the personal residence [DO NOT factor in mortgage payments in this calculation.]

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The purchaser of a sale-leaseback transaction would be least concerned with the:
option_d: depreciated book value of the building.
feedback: In a sale-leaseback arrangement the seller becomes a tenant, allowing the seller to deduct all of his future rent payments as business expenditures. The buyer in a sale-leaseback agreement is generally not concerned with the seller's book value because they're not getting a loan from a financial institution.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is not subject to taxation?
feedback: Intangible personal property is not subject to taxation (although profits made from selling intellectual property are taxable). Real property and tangible personal property are subject to taxation.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Subordination clause in a mortgage or trust deed:
option_d: Prohibits new loans before original paid
feedback: Subordination sets priority among multiple liens in case of default or foreclosure.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Capital gain tax is figured by multiplying one's tax bracket times
option_a: the sum of the beginning basis plus gain
option_b: the difference between net sale proceeds and adjusted basis
option_c: the sum of net sale proceeds and capital gain
option_d: the difference between net sale proceeds and capital gain
feedback: The IRS defines gain on the sale of a home as amount realized from the sale minus the adjusted basis of the home sold. The amount realized, also known as net proceeds from sale, is expressed by the formula: sale price - cost of sale = amount realized

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When the owner of a property believes that her property has been over-assessed by the county assessor, and if an appeal to the assessor's office has been denied, she should contact the:
feedback: If a Buyer or any other Property Owner felt that his property has been over-assessed he should first file an appeal with the assessor's office. If that appeal doesn't result in an adjustment, he can make an appeal to the Assessment Appeals Board.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which is personal property?
feedback: Harvested crops are movable and considered personal property.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Cash flow is a measure of how much pre-tax or after-tax cash an investment property generates. To derive cash flow it is therefore necessary to exclude
feedback: Cash flow is the difference between the amount of actual cash flowing into the investment as revenue and out of the investment for expenses, debt service, and all other items. Cash flow concerns cash items only, and therefore excludes depreciation (cost recovery), which is not a cash expense

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Title to personal property is transferred by:
feedback: Bill of Sale are for personal property, NOT real property. A bill of sale is a document that transfers ownership of goods from one person to another. It is used in situations where the former owner retains possession of the goods. Bills of sale may be used in a wide variety of transactions: people can sell their goods, exchange them, give them as gifts or mortgage them to get a loan. They can only be used: - to transfer ownership of goods that people already own; - to transfer ownership of moveable tangible goods; and - by individuals and unincorporated businesses.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: All of the following are sufficient to convey an interest in real property, except:
feedback: Bills of sale are a written instrument that conveys title to personal property, such as furniture purchased as a part of a home sale. They are not used for real property. This is not the same as a lease, which is personal property that conveys an interest in real property. NOTE - This question was asking you for which one would NOT convey interest in real property. KEYWORD, EXCEPT:

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What is the meaning of a quiet title action:
option_a: Court action to remove a cloud on the title
option_b: Purchasing property through a dummy transaction
option_d: An action to quiet a noisy tenant
feedback: A quiet title action is an action by a court to remove a cloud or claim that has been placed on title to property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following would be used to clear a defect from the title records?
feedback: A suit to quiet title is used to correct mistakes or disputes in the property title, thereby “quieting” other claims.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: To alienate title to property, one:
feedback: Alienation of Title is the conveyance of title. The opposite is Acquisition of Title (To gain possession of title)

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When a governmental body takes private real property for necessary public use, certain legal processes must be followed. Such a taking is an exercise of the right of:
feedback: Eminent domain is a legal strategy that allows a federal or local government to seize private property for public use. The seizing authority must pay fair market value for the property seized.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A written summary of the history of all conveyances and legal proceedings affecting a specific parcel of real estate is called a(n):
feedback: An "abstract of title" is a written summary that traces every change of ownership and claim against a property (such as mortgages, liens, and easements). In some cases, the abstract goes back to the last change of title, in others to the first recorded owner. It is part of the title report required by virtually all lenders.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The list of previous owners of conveyance from whom the present real estate owner derives his or her title is known as the:
feedback: The "chain" links together the successive owners of a property from the most recent to the original recorded title holder. The chain of title is generally verified by deeds, judgments in lawsuits over title, affidavits, and any other documents which show ownership changes.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A deed to convey property would be SIGNED by:
feedback: The "grantor" is the one conveying the property, so he or she signs the deed. An easy way to remember is that when a title ends with an "or" it is the giver, or the givOR if that helps you remember. A title that ends with "ee" such as a "grantee" is the receiver because they recEEve the item.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Involuntary methods of conveying property include all of the following EXCEPT:
feedback: Quitclaim deeds are a form of voluntary conveyance of a property without any warranties. Adverse possession, condemnation, and escheat are all forms of involuntary conveyance of a property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following statements is incorrect?
option_a: A deed conveys a future interest in real estate if so specified by the grantee.
option_b: A deed conveys a present interest in real estate.
option_c: A will conveys no interest in real estate until it is probated.
option_d: A will conveys no interest in real estate until after the death of the testator.
feedback: The "grantee" is the person buying the property and he has no right to convey anything until the transfer is complete. At that point, he becomes the new owner and thus the "grantor" of any present or future interest he may wish to assign.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: One way investors measure the yield of an investment is by
option_a: dividing net operating income by cash flow
option_b: multiplying the investor's required yield times after-tax cash flow
option_c: dividing cash flow by the investor's equity
option_d: multiplying cash flow times the price paid for the property
feedback: cash flow ÷ equity = return on equity (ROE)

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Title to real property is conveyed by deed when:
option_a: signed by the grantee and delivered to the grantor.
option_d: delivered to and accepted by the grantee.
feedback: Conveying title is different than recording and must be both signed and delivered by the grantor and accepted by the grantee.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which is true about restrictive covenants?
option_a: They are voidable by successive owners.
option_b: They are placed by government agencies in the public record.
option_c: They are placed by private parties in a deed.
option_d: They are placed by government agencies in a deed.
feedback: Restrictive covenants are most commonly associated with subdivisions and community management associations and are intended to maintain consistency within the neighborhood. While viewed as a benefit by most, they do limit the owner's use of the property and are binding on future owners. They are usually put on by the grantor or the developer.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following rights of ownership in real property do individuals normally not have?
feedback: Eminent domain is a legal strategy that allows a federal or local government to seize private property for public use. The seizing authority must pay fair market value for the property seized. This is not a right an individual would have. The other government rights are police power, taxation and escheat. A easy way to remember the government powers is to remember the name Pete. Pete stands for Police power, Escheat, Taxation and Eminent domain.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The two requirements which must be observed when governmental units exercise the power of eminent domain are:
option_a: The proposed use must be public, and just compensation must be paid to the owner.
option_b: The proposed use must be practical, and just compensation must be paid to the owner.
option_c: The proposed use must be both practical and public.
option_d: The inconvenience to the owner must not be greater than the benefit to the public, and the government must justify the taking.
feedback: Eminent Domain is when a property is taken by the government because it is necessary for public use. The owner of the private property taken receives just compensation through a process called condemnation. Eminent Domain is not held by individuals, it is one of the government powers. The other government powers you need to know for the exam are police power, taxation and escheat.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When a dam broke, the flow of water cut away a strip of land from the riverbank. This would be known as:
feedback: Avulsion is an abrupt change in the course of a stream that forms the boundary between two parcels of land resulting in the loss of part of the land. [For example, if a dam breaks and the rushing water washed away a strip of land, this would constitute a Loss of Title by Avulsion.] Accretion is an increase by natural growth or addition. Alluvion is the increase of land when waterborne soil is gradually deposited. Reversion refers to when somebody loses title and title "reverts" back to another party.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The main purpose of a deed is to:
option_a: Evidence of the change in title or transfer of an interest in real property
option_b: Provide a written instrument that can be recorded
option_c: Identify the parties involved in real property transfer
option_d: Provide evidence of the terms of a real property transaction
feedback: Deeds provide evidence of the transfer of title of real property from a grantor to a grantee. Deed requires the grantor must be legally competent, it must be delivered and accepted and contain a granting or action clause. A deed does NOT need to be acknowledged to be valid

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A deed:
option_a: can only be recorded in one county.
option_b: will not give the grantee the usual protection of recording laws if it is a quitclaim deed.
option_c: does not have to be recorded to transfer title.
option_d: gives actual notice of its contents, when recorded.
feedback: It is not necessary to record a deed for it to effectively transfer title. Deeds are considered officially executed when signed by the grantor.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A quitclaim deed to a parcel of real property conveys only the present interest, right and title of the:
feedback: The "grantor" is the one conveying the property, so they sign the deed. An easy way to remember is that when a title ends with an "or" it is the giver, or the givOR if that helps you remember. A title that ends with "ee" such as a "grantee" is the receiver because they recEEve the item.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A valid deed must contain:
feedback: Deeds provide evidence of the transfer of title of real property from a grantor to a grantee. For a deed to be valid, it must meet the following requirements: 1. The grantor must be legally competent 2. The deed must be delivered and accepted 3. The deed must contain a granting or action clause. In most states, a deed does NOT need to be acknowledged to be valid.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following would cause a grant deed to be invalid at its inception?
option_d: It was dated on a legal holiday
feedback: Deeds provide evidence of the transfer of title of real property from a grantor to a grantee. To successfully convey a property, a deed must have a grantor that is legally competent, the deed must be delivered and accepted, and it must contain a granting or action clause. A deed does NOT need to be acknowledged or recorded to be valid.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: In order for a grant deed to be effective in transferring title to property, it must:
feedback: Deeds provide evidence of the transfer of title of real property from a grantor to a grantee. To successfully convey a property, a deed must have a grantor that is legally competent, the deed must be delivered and accepted, and it must contain a granting or action clause. A deed does NOT need to be acknowledged or recorded to be valid.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When a judgment is duly recorded, subsequent buyers and innocent purchasers who are ignorant of the recording have received:
feedback: Constructive Notice attaches once a document is recorded. Once recorded, subsequent buyers will be deemed to have received "Constructive Notice" regarding the document and its effect on the property in question. This could also be taken by taking possession. Actual Notice is when an individual has first-hand knowledge of something. [For example, if a buyer has knowledge that someone has taken possession of the property that is for sale.]

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: FNMA was created under Title-II primarily for:
option_b: Buying Title-II loans to keep market liquid
feedback: FNMA buys Title-II loans to provide liquidity, not directly lend funds.

subject: A4 Appraisal and Valuation
question_text: Approach requiring separate land/improvement values:
feedback: Cost approach requires separate land value.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Conservation of capital refers to
option_a: preserving, through low-risk investments, the original amount invested
option_b: increasing the value of the original investment over an amount of time
option_c: investing only in low cost and safe investment entities
option_d: getting conservative advice from your financial planner
feedback: With any investment, one wants the original investment to grow, without losing it. This idea is called conservation of capital

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Which of the following statements describes the role of an asset manager?
option_a: The asset manager’s role is to achieve the investment objectives of the owners
option_b: The asset manager’s role is to manages the day-to-day operations
option_c: The asset manager’s role is to maintain the property’s condition
option_d: The asset manager’s role is to file the investor's tax return
feedback: The role of an asset manager is to achieve the investment objectives of the owners as opposed to managing day-to-day operations

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: An acknowledgment may be taken by a notary public who is:
option_a: the grantor in a deed being acknowledged.
option_b: the mortgagee in a mortgage being acknowledged.
option_c: an employee of a corporation who has executed an instrument being acknowledged, if the notary is not personally interested.
option_d: the grantee in a deed which is being acknowledged.
feedback: Acknowledgement is done by a notary. A notary is a public figure authorized to attest to the signing of documents, such as deeds or mortgages. The notary public certifies that they have witnessed the signing of the document by also signing the document and affixing his or her official seal (simply said, they are who they say they are). The acknowledgment may be taken by a notary public who is an employee of the grantor's corporation, but only if the notary public has NO interest in the transaction.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A recorded legal document that gives constructive notice that an action affecting a certain property has been filed in court is called:
feedback: Lis pendens is Latin for "litigation pending," which will help you to remember that while a lis pendens isn't a lien, it is notice that litigation (a possible lien) is pending. The other terms listed deal with deeds (habendum clause and general warranty deed), or mortgages (estoppel certificate).

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: All of the following may be added to the original cost basis of a property to arrive at an adjusted basis for federal income tax purposes, except:
feedback: According to the IRS, "Your adjusted basis is generally your cost in acquiring your home plus the cost of any capital improvements you made, less casualty loss amounts and other decreases." An example would be the addition of a concrete patio on the personal residence. Mortgage payments are not included under the IRS's definition. NOTE - This question asks "All of the following.... EXCEPT" which means it's looking for a false answer, and it's false that mortgage payments may be added to the original cost basis for tax purposes.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Robert often refers to tax shelters when discussing properties. Robert is primarily referring to:
feedback: Tax Shelters pertain to Income Taxes. It is a general term used to include any property or other investment which gives the owner certain income tax advantages, such as deductions for property taxes, mortgage interest or depreciation.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: For federal income tax purposes, a taxpayer could adjust the cost basis of their personal residence for which of the following items?
option_b: The addition of a concrete patio
feedback: Adjusted Cost Basis is the net cost of an asset after adjusting for various tax-related items. An example of an adjusted cost basis would be the cost related to adding a concrete patio to a personal residence. Mortgage payments are not factored into this calculation.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: As one of the qualifications for a depreciation deduction for income tax purposes, the real property must be:
feedback: An Income property owner is allowed to take depreciation deductions on his property, but in order to qualify for this type of deduction, he must have made some kind of improvement or improvements to the property in question. [The depreciation deduction is based on the cost of the improvements that were made. The LAND does not depreciate]

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Sam owned a triplex valued at $150,000 with an adjusted basis of $70,000. King owned a duplex valued at $145,000. Both properties were owned free and clear. They exchanged their properties, with King giving Sam $5,000 in cash. For federal income tax purposes:
option_a: both will be taxed on the difference between the value and the basis.
feedback: The term "boot" is not used in the Internal Revenue Code or the Regulations, but is commonly used in discussing the tax consequences of a Section 1031 tax-deferred exchange. For example, if a property worth $150,000 is exchanged for a property worth $145,000 plus $5000 cash, the cash is considered the boot. Receipt of boot doesn't disqualify an exchange, but it may result in a taxable gain. NOTE - The adjusted basis is irrelevant in regards to whether or not the cash results in a taxable gain for Sam.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The buyer of a commercial property under a sale/leaseback arrangement would be least concerned with:
option_a: the depreciated book value of the building.
option_b: the credit rating of the seller.
option_c: the location of the property in the general community.
feedback: In a sale-leaseback transaction, the seller becomes a tenant of the new buyer, which allows the seller to deduct all of his future rent payments as business expenses. In these types of arrangements, the buyer is generally not concerned with the book value- instead, they are concerned with the income generated by the business of the seller.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Which of the following statements describes the general rule of investing?
option_a: The safer the investment, the slower the gain
option_b: The larger the company, the safer the investment
option_c: The less the cost per share, the safer the investment
option_d: The riskier the investment, the greater the gain
feedback: The general rule in investments is that the safer the investment, the more slowly it gains in value. The more you want it to gain, and the more quickly, the more you must risk losing it

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Which of the following statements describes the primary aim of fair housing laws?
option_a: To provide housing assistance to individuals with disabilities
option_b: To provide an equal housing opportunity to all regardless of financial qualifications
option_c: To provide an equal housing opportunity without impediments of discrimination
option_d: To provide equal employment opportunities to all regardless of race or gender
feedback: The aim of these fair housing laws, or equal opportunity housing laws, is to give all people in the country an equal opportunity to live wherever they wish, provided they can afford to do so, without impediments of discrimination in the purchase, sale, rental, or financing of property

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Which of the following statements describes the purpose of a home inspection?
option_a: To identify the areas that must be repaired by the seller
option_b: To give the buyers information about the property
option_c: To confirm that the seller disclosure statement is true and correct
option_d: To provide the seller with more information about the property before selling
feedback: The home inspection gives the buyer more detailed information about the overall condition of the home prior to purchase.  Home inspections should include looking for common environmental issues that can affect the property and the residents of the property

subject: A4 Appraisal and Valuation
question_text: $90k house in $22k–$30k neighborhood suffers:
feedback: Surrounded by cheaper homes = economic obsolescence.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Investment risks include
option_b: changes in the value of money as an exchange medium
option_c: changes in the office staff of the company you have invested in
feedback: Changes in the value of money as an exchange medium, such as through inflation, may decrease the practical value of your invested resource

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Which of the following occurs when an investor is required to add to the investment just to keep it in place?
feedback: An invested asset may fail to appreciate as expected. It may even decline in value. Perhaps even worse, the investor may be called on to add to the investment just to maintain a required level of equity in the investment.   The loss of value of a an asset that is partially financed is called negative leverage

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Investing in an income-producing property would NOT include which of the following?
option_b: A retail store in a strip mall
option_d: An office building in a downtown location
feedback: Non-income property is a residential property used as the investor’s primary residence. Income property owned specifically for the investment rewards include multi-family residential properties, retail stores, industrial properties, and office buildings

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Investing in real estate is always subject to which of the following risk factor?
feedback: Capital put into real estate is always subject to the full range of risk factors: market changes, income shortfalls, negative leverage, tax law changes, and poor overall return

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Which of the following investment entities can comprise a real estate syndicate?
feedback: A real estate syndicate is a group of investors who combine resources to buy, develop, and/or operate a property.  A general partnership is a syndicate in which all members participate equally in managing the investment and in the profits or losses it generates

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Taxable income from investment real estate is
option_a: total income less expenses and cost of vacancies
option_c: gross income minus expenses, deductions and exclusions per tax law
feedback: Taxable income from investment real estate is the gross income received minus any expenses, deductions or exclusions that current tax law allows. Depreciation is also considered a deductible expense.  Taxable income from real estate is added to the investor's other income and taxed at the investor's marginal tax rate

subject: A4 Appraisal and Valuation
question_text: Alicia and Joe built two homes at the same time… value of Joe’s property is way higher. One reason could be because Alicia’s property experienced:
feedback: Functional obsolescence occurs from poor design. Economic/external would affect both properties equally.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Section 1031 (Starker) Exchanges are a way to defer taxes on the sale of investment property.  Tax on any gain is deferred until
option_a: the property is sold and not exchanged
option_b: any mortgage on the property is paid in full
option_c: owners qualify for regular tax exemptions
option_d: ten years after the 1031 Exchange closing date
feedback: An investor can sometimes defer the reporting of gain or loss, and, hence, taxation of gain, by participating in an exchange of like-kind assets. The legislation that deals with like-kind exchanges is contained in Section 1031 of the IRS code. Tax on gain is deferred until the investment or business property is sold and not exchanged

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Appreciation is the increase in the value of an asset over time.  For residential property, a simple formula to estimate total appreciation is
option_a: current value plus or minus the price at the time the property is ultimately sold
option_b: current value minus original purchase price
option_c: original purchase price plus cost of improvements
option_d: total cost of improvements over time of ownership minus current value
feedback: Appreciation is the increase in value of an asset over time. A simple way to estimate appreciation on a primary residence is to subtract the price originally paid from the estimated current market value

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Taxable income is
option_a: current market value minus depreciation and expenses for the current tax year
option_b: original purchase price minus any loss on the market value for the current tax year
option_c: net operating income minus all allowable deductions
feedback: The owner's tax liability on taxable income from the property is based on taxable income rather than cash flow, or income received minus any expense deductions allowed by current tax law (including depreciation)

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: What is the total appreciation of a property that was purchased ten years ago for $300,000 and has a current market value of $325,000?
feedback: A simple way to estimate appreciation on a primary residence is to subtract the price originally paid from the estimated current market value: Current value - original price = total appreciation

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: The interest on a home equity loan is deductible only if the loan proceeds were used to
option_a: pay off credit card debt and/or any outstanding debt on which interest was due
option_b: buy, build or make big improvements to the home that secures the loan
option_c: purchase a second or vacation home
option_d: assist family members with the purchase of a home
feedback: The interest on a home equity loan may be deducted only if the loan is used to “buy, build or substantially improve” the home that secures the loan. Principal payments on a loan are not deductible

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following terms is defined as “equal, undivided interest jointly owned by husband and wife”?
feedback: The definition of tenancy by the entirety is equal, undivided interest jointly owned by husband and wife

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Which of the following is the primary tax benefit available to the owner of a non-income property?
option_a: The annual deduction for the full mortgage payment
option_b: The portion of annual mortgage payment that goes to repay the principal
option_c: The depreciation amounts available for non-income properties
option_d: The annual deduction for mortgage interest
feedback: The primary tax benefit available to the owner of a non-income property is the annual deduction for mortgage interest, assuming the owner itemizes deductions. The portion of mortgage payments applied to principal must be subtracted to determine the amount paid for interest. Principal repayment is not deductible

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: If married taxpayers filing jointly qualified for the gains tax exclusion, what would be the amount of capital gains due on their principal residence if the original purchase price was $300,000 and the recent sale price was $425,000?
feedback: Tax law provides an exclusion of $250,000 for an individual taxpayer and $500,000 for married taxpayers filing jointly

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Which of the following expenses would NOT be considered in calculating the adjusted basis of a property for tax purposes?
feedback: The beginning basis is increased or decreased by certain types of expenditures made while the property is owned. Basis is increased by the cost of capital improvements made to the property

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Tax law provides for exclusions for individual and married taxpayers for capital gains purposes.  Those exclusion amounts are
feedback: Tax law provides an exclusion of $250,000 for an individual taxpayer and $500,000 for married taxpayers filing jointly

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Which of the following is required for a capital gains tax exclusion for individuals and married taxpayers?
option_a: The taxpayer must own the property for at least one of the last three years prior to the date of sale
option_b: The taxpayer must use the property as a principal residence for at least two years during the five years preceding the date of sale
option_c: The taxpayer must not occupy the property
option_d: The taxpayer must own and occupy the property for a period of 12 months and sell it at a loss  from the original purchase price
feedback: The exclusion of gain from sale of a residence can be claimed every two years, provided the taxpayer: 1. owned the property for at least two years during the five years preceding the date of sale; 2. used the property as principal residence for a total of two years during that five-year period; 3. has waited two years since the last use of the exclusion for any sale

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Owner’s right to reclaim mortgaged property after sheriff sale:
feedback: Redemption allows defaulted owners to reclaim property by paying owed amounts.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Cash flow is defined as
option_a: income minus expenses and debt service
option_b: the total sum of all income flowing into the investment
option_c: the total monthly income from the investment offset by 2/3 of the monthly expenses
option_d: the total monthly income from the investment offset by ½ of the monthly expenses
feedback: Investors are generally concerned with cash flow which is defined as the difference between the amount of actual cash flowing into the investment as revenue and out of the investment for expenses, debt service and all other items

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Taxable income is net operating income minus all allowable deductions.  Those allowable deductions include all of the following with the exception of
feedback: Taxable income is net operating income minus all allowable deductions. Cost recovery expense is allowed as a deduction, while allowances for reserves and payments on loan principal payback are not allowed

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Net operating income (NOI) is defined as
option_a: the income remaining after payment of all expenses
option_b: gross income minus expenses before debt service
option_c: gross income minus all debt service and depreciation
option_d: income from rents and tenant portions of payment for utilities and taxes
feedback: NOI is defined as the gross income minus expenses before debt service

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: A real estate syndicate is defined as
option_a: a group of two or more people whose purpose is to invest in real estate to generate income
option_b: a closed group of people who own real estate
option_c: a system whereby real estate investors can exchange information
option_d: similar to an MLS, a compilation of listings that are available for investment
feedback: A real estate syndicate is a group of investors who combine resources to buy, develop, and/or operate a property

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: A Real Estate Mortgage Investment Conduit (REMIC) is
option_a: a group of mortgage brokers whose goal is to reach more potential clients
option_b: an office within any of the large banking institutions that focuses only on private mortgage investors
option_c: a form of partnership created to hold a pool of mortgages secured by real property
option_d: a group of potential purchasers of real estate who individually do not qualify for a mortgage
feedback: A REMIC is a kind of partnership entity formed to hold a fixed pool of mortgages that are secured by real property. The entity issues two kinds of interest. Holders of residual interests are treated, for tax purposes, as partners. Holders of regular interests are regarded as owning debt instruments

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Passive investment activities are defined as
option_a: those in which the investor does not actively participate
option_b: investment activity that requires the investor to take a role in the management of the company
option_c: investments that require no monitoring, but report activity on an annual basis
option_d: those which have no active investors
feedback: Passive activities are business activities in which the taxpayer does not materially participate, including interests in limited partnerships and rental activities; losses from such activities can be used to offset income from other passive activities

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: When contemplating an investment, all of the following are important risk factors to consider EXCEPT
option_b: changes in the value of money
feedback: Investment risks come from a variety of general sources, including the market, business operations, the value of money, and changes in the interest rate

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: When an investment property generates less income than the amount the investor has to pay a lender to finance the investment, the investor suffers from
feedback: Leveraging is borrowing funds in order to make an investment that is larger than your own resource permits you to do directly. In negative leverage situations, your cost of borrowing funds to make the investment becomes greater than the income the investment returns to you

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: An individual buys a small office building as an investment and participates actively in the management and operation of the building. This is an example of
feedback: Individuals, corporations or other investor entities may invest as active investors in a property by buying it directly and taking responsibility for managing and operating the property. This is called a direct investment

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Which of the following best describes a Real Estate Investment Trust?
option_a: Investors are partners in an entity that sells pools of mortgages secured by real property
option_b: Investors are general partners who pool cash resources to buy, develop and operate a property
option_c: Owners of an income property convey title in the property to a land trust as a tax shield
option_d: Investors own shares in a trust that receives 75% of its income from real estate investments
feedback: A trust must receive at least 75% of its income from real estate to qualify as a REIT

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Cost recovery is allowed as a federal tax deduction on
feedback: Cost recovery, or depreciation, allows the owner of income property to deduct a portion of the property's value from gross income each year over the life of the asset. Principal residences cannot be depreciated (unless portions are treated as a home office.) Further, land cannot be depreciated

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: A homeowner paid $285,000 for a house three years ago.  The house sells today for $339,000. How much has the property appreciated?
feedback: Appreciation as a per cent can be estimated by (1) subtracting the estimated current market value from the price originally paid (339,000 - 285,000 = 54,000) and (2) dividing the result by the original price (54,000 / 285,000 = . 19 or 19%)

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: The primary tax benefit in owning a non-income property such as a residence is
option_c: a deduction for costs of operating the property
feedback: Deducting mortgage interest is a significant tax advantage of owning one's residence. Depreciation and cost-deductions do not apply to a non-income producing residence. Appreciation is a benefit, but not a tax benefit

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: A homeowner bought a house five years ago for $250,000.  Since then, the homeowner has spent $6,000 to pave the driveway and has added a central heating/air-conditioning system at a cost of $10,000.  What is the homeowner's adjusted basis if the house is sold today?
feedback: The beginning basis is the cost of acquiring a property. Basis is increased by capital improvements and decreased by depreciation. The basic formula for adjusted basis is Beginning basis ($250,000) + capital improvements ($6,000 + $10,000) - depreciation ($0) = adjusted basis ($266,000)

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: A homeowner sold her house and had net proceeds of $191,000.  Her adjusted basis in the home was $176,000. She immediately bought another house for $200,000.  What was her capital gain?
feedback: Gain on sale. The gain on sale of a primary residence is represented by the basic formula:  amount realized (net sales proceeds) - adjusted basis = gain on sale.  $191,000 - $176,000 = $15,000

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: After three years of owner-occupancy, a young homeowner sells his principal residence for a gain of $150,000, and the next month buys another principal residence that costs more than the adjusted sale price of the old home.  Which of the following is true of the treatment of the tax on gain?
option_b: It must be paid in the year of the sale
option_c: The homeowner may choose to pay it or defer it
option_d: Tax is due on the difference between the cost of the new home and adjusted basis of the old one
feedback: There is no taxable gain because the gain falls under the exclusion limit of $250,000. Tax law provides an exclusion of $250,000 for an individual taxpayer and $500,000 for married taxpayers filing jointly on the gain from sale of a principal residence. The seller must have owned the property for at least two years during the five years preceding the date of sale; used the property as principal residence for a total of two years during that five-year period; and have waited two years since the last use of the exclusion for any sale

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Which of the following are limitations on the exclusion of capital gain on the sale of a house?
option_a: The seller is 55 years old, the property is used as a principal residence, and the seller's previously claimed exclusions do not exceed $125,000
option_b: The property is owner-occupied and the seller has never claimed an exclusion previously
option_c: The seller must have owned it for two years of the previous five, used it as a principal residence for two years during that period, and not claimed an exclusion in the previous two years
option_d: The seller must have owned and occupied it for five years and not claimed an exclusion in the previous year
feedback: Tax law provides an exclusion of $250,000 for an individual taxpayer and $500,000 for married taxpayers filing jointly. Exclusion of gain from sale of a residence can be claimed every two years, provided the taxpayer:  1. owned the property for at least two years during the five years preceding the date of sale; 2. used the property as principal residence for a total of two years during that five-year period; 3. has waited two years since the last use of the exclusion for any sale

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Bill Holdfast owns a small retail property that he inherited from his father.  There are no mortgages or interest expenses connected with the property.  Bill takes an annual cost recovery expense of $5,000. The property has a monthly gross income of $1,500 and monthly operating expenses of $500. Bill's taxable income from this property will be taxed at a rate of 24%.  What is the tax liability for the year?
feedback: Annual gross operating income ($1,500 x 12 = $18,000) - annual operating expenses ($500 x 12 = $6,000) = annual net operating income ( $12,000); annual net operating income ($12,000) - cost recovery expense ($5,000) = taxable income ($7,000); taxable income ($7,000) x tax rate (24%) = tax liability ($1,680)

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Lender “discounting” refers to
option_d: Difference between nominal and effective interest
feedback: Discounting means the lender disburses less than the note’s face value.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Carol buys “subject to” VA loan; liability
feedback: The original borrower (Greg) remains primarily liable; “subject to” does not transfer liability.

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Market changes to seller’s market
feedback: When demand exceeds supply, prices rise.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The abbreviation POB stands for
feedback: POB stands for "point of beginning."

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: FHA financing – buyer would NOT
feedback: Buyers do not apply to a local FHA office; the lender handles FHA appraisal.

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: The purpose of the closing event is to
option_a: confirm that the buyer has fulfilled all contract requirements prior to title transfer immediately after closing
option_b: ensure that the seller has marketable title before monies are transferred
option_c: conclude the process for loan approval
option_d: exchange legal title for the sale price
feedback: The closing process consists of buyer and seller verifying that each has fulfilled the terms of the sale contract. If they have, then the mortgage loan, if any, is closed, all expenses are apportioned and paid, the consideration is exchanged for the title, final documents are signed, and arrangements are made to record the transaction according to local laws

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: A buyer's financing arrangements are often concluded at closing, because
option_a: lenders do not fund loans unless title is being transferred
option_b: the lender wants to ensure proper handling of the collateral for the loan
option_c: the loan term must coincide with title transfer
option_d: the deed will be held as collateral for the loan
feedback: A lender is concerned about the quality of the collateral a borrower is providing in return for the mortgage loan. The collateral would be endangered by defects in the title, by liens that would take precedence over the mortgage lien, such as a tax lien, and by physical damage to the property which is not repaired. Consequently, the lender typically requires a survey; a property inspection; hazard insurance; a title insurance policy; a reserve account for taxes and insurance; and possibly, private mortgage insurance. In some cases the lender may also require a certificate of occupancy verifying that any new construction performed complies with local building codes

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: The Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act prescribes closing procedures that must be followed whenever
option_a: a first, second, or third mortgage lien is involved
option_b: the loan is to be sold to the FNMA
option_c: the buyer pays all cash for the property
option_d: the property is a residential complex in excess of four units
feedback: RESPA specifies certain closing procedures when a purchase is being financed by a "federally-related" mortgage loan, which includes loans made by a federally-insured lender; loans insured or guaranteed by the VA or FHA, loans administered by HUD, and loans intended to be sold to FNMA, FHLMC, or GNMA

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: A sale contract stipulates that a buyer is to pay the seller's title insurance expenses. This practice is not customary in the area. In this case,
option_a: the buyer and seller must amend the contract before closing
option_b: the contract is voidable, since the seller must pay the expense
option_c: the buyer may pay or not pay the expense, at his or her option
option_d: the buyer must pay the expense
feedback: Explicit agreements in the sale contract supercede traditional expense-payment responsibilities, which can vary from state to state

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: A prorated expense on the settlement statement is
option_a: a debit to the buyer and seller
option_b: a credit to the buyer and seller
option_c: a debit and credit to the buyer and seller
option_d: a debit to one party and a credit to the other
feedback: A prorated item is treated as a debit to one party and a credit to the other party for the same amount

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: The amount a buyer owes at closing is equal to
option_a: the excess of the buyer's debits over the buyer's credits
option_b: the excess of the buyer's credits over the buyer's debits
option_c: the excess of the seller's debits over the seller's credits
option_d: the excess of the seller's credits over the seller's debits
feedback: The excess of the buyer's debits over the buyer's credits is the amount the buyer must bring to the closing

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: Which of the following are examples of closing items not prorated between buyer and seller?
option_d: Condominium assessments and special assessment payments
feedback: Inspection fees are usually paid in full by the seller

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: Which of the following items are paid in arrears?
feedback: At the time of closing, the seller has incurred certain expenses that have not been billed or paid at the time of closing and that the buyer will have to pay later. Typical items are real estate taxes, utility bills and interest on an assumed mortgage

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: Which of the following items are paid in advance?
feedback: At the time of closing, the seller has paid some items in advance that cover a period of time that goes beyond the closing date. In effect, the seller has prepaid some of the buyer's expenses, and the buyer must reimburse the seller. Hazard and flood insurance premiums are typical items. By the same token, the seller of a rental property may have received rent or rental deposits in advance, and must reimburse the buyer for the part that belongs to the buyer

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: If a sale contract indicates that the day of closing is "the seller's day," this means that
option_a: the seller must pay prorated expenses inclusive of the day of closing
option_b: the seller does not own the property on the day of closing
option_c: the seller may elect the proration method on the day of closing
option_d: the seller must pay the buyer's portion of prorated expenses instead of the seller's portion
feedback: It is customary in most states that the seller owns the property up to and including the day of closing unless stated otherwise in the contract. Thus the closing day is apportioned to the seller in computing prorations

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: Documentary stamps are used to
option_a: document the procedures employed to close a transaction
option_b: document the payment of a transfer tax
option_c: certify that a transaction was recorded
option_d: mail closing documents to principal parties after closing
feedback: Most states impose a transfer tax when real estate is conveyed. The tax is usually paid when the deed is recorded, often in the form of documentary stamps purchased from the recorder where the deed is recorded. The stamps must be attached to deeds and conveyances before they are recorded

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: How many acres are contained in the SW1/4 of the SW1/2 of Section 16?
feedback: 640 divided by 4 divided by 2 = 80

subject: A3 Financing
question_text: Assumed loan > seller’s basis
feedback: Excess loan assumed over basis is treated as down payment for tax purposes.

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: What is the Internal Revenue Service's Form 1099-S?
option_a: The buyer's and seller's capital gain form
option_b: A form that summarizes and reports transaction data from a closing
option_c: A form brokers must submit if the buyer or seller is a foreigner
option_d: A lender's tax form showing that a loan was funded for a buyer
feedback: The person reporting files a Form 1099-S information return with the Internal Revenue Service. This form includes the names and addresses of the parties to the transaction and information about the sale proceeds

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: Assume a seller at closing must pay transfer taxes at the rate of $1.00 for every $500 of purchase price, or fraction thereof. If the sale price is $345,600, how much tax must the seller pay?
feedback: Methods of stating the transfer tax due include quoting the tax as a percentage of the taxable consideration and as a dollar rate per $100.00 of total selling price. If the number of 100's is not a whole number, it must be rounded up to the next 100. For instance, if a property sells for $345,600 and the tax rate is $1.00 per $500.00 "or any fraction thereof," the tax stamps will cost:  345,600 ÷ 500.00 = 691.20. 691.20 rounded up = 692. 692 x $1.00 = $692

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: If a seller paid $488 for transfer taxes at closing, and the rate was $1.00 for every $400 or fraction thereof of the sale price, what was the sale price?

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: The closing is the culmination of the real estate transaction.  During the closing, which of the following items is addressed and completed?
option_a: The payment of the purchase price, other charges, and disbursement of the loan proceeds
option_b: The review of the Loan Estimate and Closing Disclosure forms
option_c: The renegotiation of the terms of the sales contract
option_d: The assessment of the credit report and credit score
feedback: During the closing, the buyer pays the purchase price and receives the title to the purchased real estate. At the same time, the buyer completes financing arrangements, and the buyer and seller pay all required taxes, fees, and charges

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: The primary parties at the closing are normally the
option_a: title officer and title company attorney
option_b: president of the brokerage firm and the real estate agent
option_c: title company representative and lender representative
option_d: buyers, sellers, and a closing agent
feedback: The primary parties at the closing are normally buyer, seller, and a closing agent or escrow officer. Other parties who might be present include the title officer, attorneys, brokers or agents, and the lender's representative. It is not necessary for any of these parties to attend the meeting. The closing agent can complete the transaction, provided all documents have been duly executed in advance

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: When a closing occurs in escrow, the escrow agent has the authority to
option_a: sign all the documents on behalf of the principals in the transaction
option_b: disburse funds and release documents to the appropriate parties
option_c: inspect the property on behalf of the lender
option_d: close the transaction before the loan has been approved
feedback: If the closing occurs "in escrow" rather than face-to-face, the principal parties deposit funds and documents with the appointed escrow agent, and the escrow agent disburses funds and releases documents to the appropriate parties when all the conditions of the escrow have been met

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: A final walk-through of the purchased property should be scheduled and completed by the buyer
option_b: as close to the closing date as possible
option_d: as soon as the offer is accepted
feedback: The buyer should inspect the property to make certain that the property is in the condition in which the seller states that it is and that any repairs or other required actions have been performed. A final inspection, called a buyer's walk-through, should be conducted as close to the closing date as possible

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: Before transferring the title to the property from the seller to the buyer,
option_a: the title company closing the loan must receive the loan proceeds from the lender
option_b: all appliances must be checked and determined to be in working order
option_c: the sellers must vacate the property
option_d: the title company closing the loan must order an appraisal of the property
feedback: The buyer’s loan must be approved prior to scheduling the closing.  The loan approval is a contract contingency that must be met prior to scheduling the closing

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: An affidavit of title is given to the buyer by the seller at closing.  This document indicates that
option_a: the seller will repair any damages to the property
option_b: the buyer will be responsible for any hidden liens
option_c: the seller has not incurred any new liens that would affect title
option_d: the title commitment that has been issued, and the property is ready to close
feedback: The seller may be asked to execute an affidavit of title stating that, since the date of the original title search, the seller has incurred no new liens, judgments, unpaid bills for repairs or improvements, no unrecorded deeds or contracts, no bankruptcies or divorces that would affect title or any other defects the seller is aware of

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: At the closing, which of the following parties will be expected to sign documents unless pre-signed prior to closing?
option_b: The real estate agents for both sellers and buyers
option_c: The sellers, buyers, attorneys for sellers and buyers, title closer, and any lenders
option_d: The powers of attorney for sellers, buyers, lenders, and title company
feedback: The sellers and buyers will sign documents, either transferring title to the property or finalizing the mortgage loan to purchase the property.  There may be several other documents that must be signed by the sellers and buyers

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Littoral rights" are what?
option_a: Rights that govern lakefront or oceanfront property
option_b: Rights that govern flowing water like rivers
option_c: The right to the water beneath a property
option_d: The right to collect rainwater on a property
feedback: Littoral rights are rights that govern lakefront or oceanfront property. Riparian rights have to do with flowing water like rivers (remember, L = littoral = lake, R = riparian = river). Percolating water rights have to do with the rights to the water below a property, and the doctrine of prior appropriation is a legal doctrine governing water usage in some states.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Legal descriptions that are used in purchase contracts and deeds must be accurate. All of the following would be considered a good and accurate legal description EXCEPT which?
option_a: The section, township, and range where the property is located
option_b: The property address with full zip code
option_d: A description using compass degrees and a point of beginning
feedback: Street addresses are not used as legal descriptions but are often included as a property reference. Lots and blocks, metes and bounds, and the government survey system are all acceptable legal descriptions.

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: RESPA refers to the
feedback: RESPA is the Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Douglas, a broker, has entered into a listing agreement with a seller. Oscar is a different broker who has been working with the buyer and leams of the property through the MLS. Who would Oscar represent?
feedback: Because Oscar, the second cooperating broker in this case, has been working with the buyer, he would represent the buyer as a buyer's agent.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What type of a listing agreement allows the owner to appoint an exclusive agent to sell his property, but retains the right to sell the property himself without paying a commission?
feedback: An exclusive right to sell listing gives the broker his or her commission regardless of who actually sells the property, even if it is the owner. Exclusive agency allows the seller to appoint an exclusive agent, but retains the right to sell the property himself. Open listings mean that if the owner or any other broker or salesperson produces the buyer, the broker will lose his or her commission.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The position of trust assumed by the broker as an agent for the client is described most accurately as a:
feedback: A fiduciary is a person who holds a legal or ethical relationship of trust with one or more other parties (person or group of persons). Typically, a fiduciary takes care of money or other assets for another person.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: An agent becomes the agent of the seller when both the seller and the agent sign a(n):
feedback: The listing agreement is the employment contract that creates the agency relationship between the seller and the listing agent.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: If a borrower must pay $6,000 for points on a $150,000 loan, how many points is the lender charging for this loan?
feedback:
One discount point costs 1% of your mortgage amount. 1% of $150,000 = $1,500, and $6,000 ÷ $1,500 = 4.

Mortgage points, also known as discount points, are fees paid directly to the lender at closing in exchange for a reduced interest rate. This is also called “buying down the rate,” which can lower your monthly mortgage payments.

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: TRID refers to the
feedback: The TILA RESPA Integrated Disclosure Rule is informally referred to by those involved in the real estate industry as TRID

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: Which of the following booklets must a lender deliver to the potential borrower within three business days of receiving the loan application?
feedback: This is the booklet, provided and distributed by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), that lenders must give to the borrower when they apply for a mortgage loan

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: In his will a man left his farm both to his daughter and his son. The daughter is to have a ¾ interest and the son is to have ¼ interest. How will the son and daughter hold title?
feedback: The daughter and son would hold title as tenants in common because of unequal shares.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Broker Emile sells his client's home for $800,000 with the help of a cooperating broker. The parties have agreed that the 6% commission would be evenly split between the two brokers. Emile has a 140 80 split with his firm and the cooperating broker has a 50-50 commission split agreement with his firm. How much will Emile earn?
feedback: First multiply the gross sale price of $800,000 x 0.06 to get $48,000. This is the total commission, which will need to be divided by 2 since it will be split evenly between the two brokers/firms. That gives us $24,000 for Emile's side of the transaction. Emile gets to keep 40% of the $24,000. So: 0.40 x $24,000 = $9,600.

subject: A6 Closing and Settlem
question_text: The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) requires lenders to use which of the following two forms?
