option_c: If the broker holds a South Carolina license
option_d: If the buyer and seller have legally established blood or marital ties
feedback: A broker may receive compensation from both the buyer and the seller if both parties give their informed consent to the dual compensation.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Julia Gomez is a licensee who represents the Randolphs (sellers). The Randolphs do not wish to have a subagency or disclosed dual agency relationship for the transaction. The Lomans are prospective buyers who ask Julia to show them the Randolphs' house. May Julia show the property to the Lomans under these circumstances?
option_a: Yes, but Julia must inform the Lomans, in writing, that she represents the Randolphs' best interests.
option_b: Yes, as long as the Lomans agree, in writing, to not having any representation.
option_c: Yes, as long as the Lomans first enter a buyer representation agreement with another licensee.
option_d: No, because the Randolphs oppose a disclosed dual agency or subagency relationship and Julia cannot show the property to the Lomans without engendering such a relationship.
feedback: In this situation, the Lomans must agree, in writing, to no representation before Julia can show them the Randolphs' property.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Armen is a buyer who has marked "self representation" on the agency disclosure form. What obligations, if any, does the agent have to Armen?
option_a: The agent has obligations of fairness, honesty, and the provision of accurate information
option_b: None, because Armen has determined to represent his own interests
option_c: The agent has obligations of confidentiality and accountability
option_d: The agent has obligations of care and concern
feedback: Although Armen is representing his own interests, an agent still owes him fairness, honesty, and accurate information during the transaction. These are the same obligations an agent would have toward any customer, and are separate from the full fiduciary duties owed to a client.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following would not be subject to property tax?
option_b: Possessory interests of lessees in tax-exempt public property, such as leases on oil and gas properties
option_c: Vacant land located in an unincorporated area of the county
option_d: Mobile homes properly installed on a permanent foundation
feedback: Intangible personal property is not subject to taxation. Real property and tangible personal property are subject to taxation.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: To what extent is confidentiality guaranteed under South Carolina law?
option_a: The only circumstances in which a client's confidential information may be divulged is in the case of a disclosed dual agency relationship.
option_b: The only circumstances in which an agent can divulge a client's confidential information is when required by law.
option_c: Client confidentiality must be preserved indefinitely and may not be violated under any circumstances.
option_d: Confidential information about a client may only be divulged if the broker's relationship with the client is limited.
feedback: The only time confidential information about a client may be divulged is when required by law.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Tia Rouse is a salesperson whose company has just received a listing on a two bedroom, two bath bungalow. Tia's sister, Maria, would like to make an offer on the bungalow. What should Tia do?
option_a: Tell the seller that the prospective buyer is related to the agent
option_b: Represent Maria in the transaction; family relationship need not be disclosed to the seller as long as Maria signs an agency agreement
option_c: Counsel Maria to make an offer on a house not listed with Tia's company
option_d: Refer Maria to another licensee for representation
feedback: As long as Tia tells the seller that she and Maria are related, Tia may represent Maria in the sale.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: To whom does the South Carolina Residential Landlord-Tenant Act apply?
option_a: Only to a purchaser renting until closing on a property
option_d: Only to residential rental property managers
feedback: The South Carolina Residential-Tenant Act governs only those who manage residential rental property and does not apply to commercial property or individuals renting until closing.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Andrew is a 66-year-old widower on a fixed income who owns a house appraised at $220,000. Property taxes have surged in recent years, and Andrew would like to find some tax relief to lower his costs. If he can't get some assistance, he may have to sell his house. What help might be available to Andrew?
option_a: Since he is over 65, Andrew qualifies for a $50,000 reduction on tax-assessed value.
option_b: Once he reaches 67, the legal age for retirement, he will be eligible for a $50,000 reduction in the tax-appraised value.
option_c: None, unless he is either 100% disabled or completely blind; he should sell his house and buy a less expensive home.
option_d: Since he is over 65, Andrew is eligible for a $50,000 reduction in tax-appraised value.
feedback: The South Carolina Homestead Act offers a $50,000 reduction in tax-appraised value to anyone who is over the age of 65, 100 percent disabled, or totally blind and who requests the help. Assessments and appraisals can differ in value, so it's important to use the correct value.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Each time share that is purchased by a client must be registered with which South Carolina entity?
feedback: The South Carolina Real Estate Commission requires registration of each time-share sold.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: The South Carolina Coastal Zone Management Act governs all of the following in impacted areas EXCEPT which one?
option_a: The height of a two-story home
option_c: The addition of a golf course
feedback: The South Carolina Coastal Zone Management Act requires permits concerning the length of a dock, the addition of a pool, and the construction of a golf course but not the height of a house.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Which of the following is true with respect to real property taxation by the federal government?
option_a: It may impose ad valorem property taxes and capital gain tax
option_b: It may not impose property taxes nor tax liens
option_c: There are no federal ad valorem taxes on real property
option_d: It may impose ad valorem tax, but not capital gain tax
feedback: There are no federal taxes on real property. The Constitution of the United States specifically prohibits such taxes. The federal government does, however, tax income derived from real property and gains realized on the sale of real property

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: According to law, states
option_a: may not levy real estate taxes
option_c: may delegate taxing authority to county governments
option_d: may prevent federal taxation of real estate within their respective jurisdictions, if properly legislated
feedback: States may legally levy taxes on real property, but most delegate this power to counties, cities, townships and local taxing districts

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: The role of local tax districts is to
option_a: levy income, sales, and property taxes to meet their budget requirements
option_b: manage their budgeted portion of real estate tax revenues levied and distributed by the state
option_c: impose property taxes for specific municipal services
option_d: place tax liens on its facilities
feedback: County and local governments establish tax districts to collect funds for providing specific services

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A special tax district might be created to
option_a: construct and manage a park district
option_b: create a two-mile extension of county sewer facilities
option_c: establish and maintain a public library
feedback: A special assessment is a tax levied against specific properties that will benefit from a public improvement. Common examples are assessments for sidewalks, water service and sewers. Special assessments are based on the cost of the improvement and apportioned on a pro rata basis among benefiting properties according to the value that each parcel will receive from the improvement

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Ad valorem taxes are based on
feedback: General property taxes are levied on an ad valorem basis, meaning that they are based on the assessed value of the property. Assessed value is determined according to state law, usually by a county or township assessor or appraiser

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: The ad valorem tax base of a municipal jurisdiction is equal to
option_a: the jurisdiction's annual budget times the tax rate
option_b: the total of all assessed values of properties minus exemptions
option_c: the total amount of ad valorem taxes required by the budget
option_d: the municipality's budget multiplied times the millage rate
feedback: The tax base of an area is the total of the appraised or assessed values of all real property within the area's boundaries, excluding partially or totally exempt properties:  tax base = assessed values - exemptions

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: As part of the assessment process, many taxing entities utilize equalization boards in order to
option_a: adjust millage rates within the district to ensure fairness
option_b: modify the tax rate from one neighborhood to the next
option_c: ensure that property owners have nearly equal tax bills
option_d: smooth out wide discrepancies of assessed values within the district
feedback: Some taxing bodies recognize that local assessments can lead to unfairly high or low values for properties in certain areas. Therefore the jurisdiction may establish equalization factors to level out the unevenness of valuations

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The seller conveyed a quitclaim deed to the buyer. Upon receipt of the deed, the buyer may be certain that:
option_a: The buyer now owns the property subject to certain claims of the seller.
option_c: There are no encumbrances against the property.
option_d: All of the seller's interests in the property belong to the buyer.
feedback: It's important to remember that quit claim deeds have no guarantees beyond the signer's declaration that he or she is giving up all claims to the title. Accordingly, they are generally used to clear up minor issues and almost never as the primary form of transferring ownership.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is a means of transferring title from one party to another?
feedback: Deeds are the written form by which land is transferred from one owner to another. In order to be legal, they must be executed, delivered by the current owner and accepted by the new owner.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When a company furnishes materials for the construction of a house and is subsequently not paid, it may file a(n):
feedback: A mechanic's lien is the first, and usually most cost-effective, step for a person providing labor and/or materials to a homeowner to recover monies owed--in large part because of the pressure it puts on the homeowner to settle quickly and without costly court involvement.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What is the opposite of "alienation?"
feedback: Alienation of title is the conveyance of title, so the opposite is the acquisition of title.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Recorded title to a parcel of real property was vested in Jill Martinez, a single woman. After her marriage to Josh Cohen, she executed a deed to the property only in the name of Jill Cohen, a married woman. The discrepancy in the grantor's name is:
option_a: not important as long as the property is adequately described.
option_b: a defect which could cause separate property of both spouses to become joint tenancy property.
option_c: cured after the deed is on freehold for one year.
option_d: a defect which may cause a cloud on the title.
feedback: A "cloud on title" means there is something affecting the title that may prevent its transfer. For example, if a single woman gets married and she purchased real property prior to marriage, then executed the grant deed in her married name, a discrepancy would arise regarding the grantor's name. This might constitute enough of a defect to result in a cloud on the title.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Title to real property can be acquired by an individual by all of the following methods except:
feedback: Escheat is a reversion of property to the state in the absence of an individual owner. It usually occurs when a property owner dies intestate (without a will), and without heirs. An individual CANNOT acquire property through Escheat.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: "Intestate succession" occurs when:
option_a: Not all heirs are named in a formal will.
option_b: The deceased leaves only a holographic will.
feedback: Intestate succession occurs when someone dies without leaving a will, or leaving an invalid will so that the property of the estate passes by the laws of succession rather than by the direction of the deceased.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following deeds would least likely contain implied covenants by the grantor?
feedback: A Quitclaim Deed is a deed operating as a release; intended to pass any title, interest, or claim which the grantor may have in the property, but not containing any warranty of a valid interest or title in the grantor.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is not essential to a valid deed?
option_c: It must be acknowledged by a notary or public official
feedback: Deeds provide evidence of the transfer of title of real property from a grantor to a grantee. The deed requires the grantor to be legally competent; it must be delivered and accepted, and it contains a granting or action clause. In most states, a deed does NOT need to be acknowledged by a notary or public official to be valid.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A lis pendens:
option_a: only affects title to real property if the owner is not a party to the lawsuit.
option_b: can only be removed from the public records by a court order.
option_c: can be recorded no matter what the type of lawsuit is.
option_d: may affect title to real property based on the results of the lawsuit.
feedback: A "lis pendens" is a legal document which gives notice that an action or legal proceeding (mechanic's lien filing, foreclosure) is pending in the courts that could affect the title to the designated property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: An order from a court to a designated official to sell property in order to pay a judgment is called:
feedback: A court order instructing an officer of the court to carry out the decision of the court, such as the selling of foreclosed property or the removal of a tenant at sufferance.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: In making the decision of whether or not to issue a title policy, the title insurance company would be most concerned with those documents that appear within the:
feedback: The chain of title is the history of all of the documents that transfer title to a parcel of real property, starting with the earliest existing document and ending with the most recent. Note that the chain of title only tracks documents that transfer title to a property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A buyer would want which of the following removed from a preliminary title report?
option_b: An appurtenant easement over adjacent property.
feedback: A preliminary title report is issued early in the transaction to reveal all matters that presently affect the title, such as liens or judgments on the property or easements and covenants. A buyer would not be pleased to see a mechanic's lien on the report and would most likely want that taken care of.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A standard title insurance policy insures against:
option_b: A recorded deed in the chain of title that was not properly delivered
option_c: Losses sustained by improved property only
option_d: Claims of persons in possession of the property
feedback: Standard policy of title insurance protects against forgery and improper delivery, however, it does not include a survey or on-site inspection.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: There are several differences between an extended coverage policy of title insurance and a standard coverage policy of title insurance. Which of the following is insured under the extended policy, but not under the standard policy?
option_b: The results of a forged deed in the chain title
option_c: The lack of capacity of one of the parties to any transaction involving title to the land
option_d: The possibility that some improvements on the insured property are located on adjoining land
feedback: Extended policy covers improvements on adjoining land.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Although a title insurance policy does cover the risk of loss for many reasons, which of the following risks is not covered in a title insurance policy?
option_a: An unpaid city tax not shown in the policy
option_b: A change in zoning ordinance, regulation or plan
option_c: A document in the series of records is forged
option_d: Failure of husband or wife to sign the deed
feedback: Title insurance generally doesn't cover changes in zoning or city regulations.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which type of policy will cover everything?
feedback: Title insurance policy is a policy that protects a buyer against errors or omissions or defects in the title of the property. No title insurance policy covers everything.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A title company could make a title search by searching the records of the:
option_a: County Recorder's, Federal Land and County Clerk's office
feedback: A title search is a review of all recorded documents affecting a specific piece of property to determine the present condition of title.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: An agreement wherein one party agrees to reimburse the other party for damages suffered in the event of a clearly defined risk, in exchange for payment of monetary consideration, is commonly known as:
feedback: Insurance is the promise of reimbursement for damages for the promise to pay. With respect to Fire Insurance, the policy should allow the insured to neither gain nor lose in the event of claim.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The IRS (Internal Revenue Service) would define marginal tax rate as:
option_a: The tax rate which is used for the next dollar of taxable income
option_b: The difference between the present tax rate and the past tax rate
option_c: None of the other options are correct
option_d: The tax rate used for your state income taxes
feedback: Marginal Tax Rate is the tax rate that is applied to the next dollar of taxable earned income.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A written instrument which passes title of property, such as furniture purchased as a part of a home sale, from seller to buyer would be a:
feedback: Bills of Sale are written instruments which passes title of personal property, such as furniture purchased as a part of a home sale, from seller to buyer. They are not used for real property. A Bill of Sale transfers title to personal property in much the same way that a deed transfers title to real property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: An apartment house was purchased for $180,000. At the time of purchase, it had a fair market value of $200,000, and an assessed value of $140,000. The basis of the property for federal income tax purposes would be:
feedback: Basis is the value placed upon property for purposes of capital gains and depreciation. The basis upon purchase of real property for investment purposes is generally the cost referred to as the original cost basis, so in the case the purchase price of $180,000 is the property's basis for tax purposes.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following would alienate title to property:
option_d: Securing an ALTA policy of title insurance
feedback: Conveying the title will alienate (transfer from one owner to another) the title to property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What item will appear as a debit on the buyer's closing statement?
option_c: Homeowner's insurance that had been prepaid by the seller
option_d: None of the other options are correct
feedback: The price is always a credit for the seller and a debit for the buyer. NOTE: Any seller-prepaid homeowners insurance will show up as a credit to the seller only. If the buyer's purchase is financed, a new homeowner's policy would need to be written and obtained by the buyer and could show up in the closing statement as homeowner's insurance prepaid by the buyer.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Larry owned an income property with an adjusted cost basis of $150,000 and a fair market value of $200,000. He exchanged the property for another income property, which has a fair market value of $210,000. Both properties had no loans against them and no adjustment was made for the difference in value. For federal income tax purposes, the new property will have a basis for Larry of:
feedback: In a tax deferred exchange transaction, the cost basis of the property transferred becomes the cost basis of the property which is acquired or received.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: One who is authorized to perform certain acts for another under a power of attorney would be a:
option_a: None of the other options are correct
feedback: A type of agency relationship where one person holds a POWER OF ATTORNEY allowing him to execute legal documents on behalf of another. Decisions made by the attorney in fact are binding on the principal.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A homeowner receives a tax bill that she feels is outrageous. This taxpayer may
option_a: appeal to adjust the millage rate
option_b: appeal to adjust the district's budget
option_c: appeal to adjust the assessed valuation
feedback: Property owners may object to the assessed value of their property, but not to the tax rate. An owner usually has a certain period to protest after receiving notice of the assessed value. According to local law, a property owner must present evidence that the assessor made an error to a review board or appeal board

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: The purpose of a homestead tax exemption is
option_a: to exempt qualified property owners from ad valorem taxation
option_b: to offer an amount of tax relief on an owner's principal residence
option_d: to exempt owners of principal residences who rent their properties
feedback: Some states exempt a portion of the value of the homestead from judgments to protect families against eviction by creditors

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A title insurance policy will protect a buyer from financial losses that are caused by:
option_a: an encumbrance to which the policy has taken an exception.
option_b: a recorded easement to which the policy has taken an exception.
option_d: an unrecorded easement to which the policy has not taken an exception.
feedback: Title insurance is provided to ensure that the buyer is obtaining clean and marketable title. A title insurance policy insures against losses due to defects or problems not identified by the title search or examination, such as an unrecorded easement for which no exception was taken because it was not discovered during the title search. Allowable exceptions to title, including any easements or liens against the property that are repaid in full when the property is sold (such as a current mortgage) are generally not covered by the title insurance policy, but allow the policy to be issued. Title exceptions that are not permitted, like ongoing property disputes or outstanding tax liens, usually must be resolved before the title insurance will be issued.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When private property is abandoned, the state may acquire title to that property under the right of:
feedback: Escheat is a reversion of property to the state in the absence of an individual owner. Usually occurs when a property owner dies intestate (without a will), and without heirs. An individual CANNOT acquire property through Escheat.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following documents is usually prepared when a real estate buyer also purchases a seller's riding lawn mower?
feedback: A bill of sale is a written document that serves as evidence of the transfer of title to personal property. Personal property is generally movable property, an example of a bill of sale would be a lawn mower.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The donation of private land for public use is called:
feedback: Dedication is the application of privately-owned land to the public for no consideration, with the intent that the land will be accepted and used for public purposes. A landowner may dedicate the entire fee simple interest, or an easement such as a public right-of-way across his property. Lands thus dedicated are normally taxed at a preferential rate.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A mechanic's lien would be properly classified as a(n):
feedback: Mechanic's lien is a specific lien a lien affecting or attaching only to a certain, specific parcel of land or piece of property. A mechanic's lien is a security interest in the title to property for the benefit of those who have supplied labor or materials that improve the property.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A millage rate is derived by
option_a: dividing the tax requirement by the tax base
option_b: multiplying the tax base times the tax requirement
option_c: adding an inflation factor to the prior year's tax rate
option_d: dividing the tax base by the tax requirement
feedback: tax requirement ÷ tax base = tax rate (millage rate)

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A homeowner's total tax bill is derived by
option_a: dividing the tax requirement by the tax base
option_b: multiplying each district's tax rate times the assessed value of the property
option_c: multiplying each district's tax rate times the taxable value of the property
option_d: averaging the tax rate for each tax district, and multiplying the average tax rate times the assessed value
feedback: Each property owner's tax bill is determined by multiplying the tax rate for each taxing district times the taxable value of the property. Taxable value is the assessed value after all exemptions and adjustments have been taken into account

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The covenant in a deed which states that the grantor is the owner and has the right to convey the title is called:
feedback: Another outgrowth of the feudal system, "seisin" derives from the French meaning to "sit upon or own" and gives owners the right to sell or transfer property at will.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following instruments proves a marketable title?
feedback: A general warranty deed stipulates that the ownership being conveyed is free of any hidden liens, encumbrances or other claims. Further, it states that if some unknown encumbrance should arise in the future, the person conveying the deed will make appropriate compensation. (This guarantee, however, is no substitute for title insurance.)

subject: null
question_text: null
feedback: null

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The title to real estate passes when a valid deed is:
feedback: Fundamentally, real estate transactions only involve two parties--the buyer and the seller. All that's necessary to create a legal sale is for one party to make an offer the other accepts. Recording, escrow, real estate licensees, mortgage companies and the like facilitate and support the transaction process but are not requirements of a legal sale.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A special warranty deed differs from a general warranty deed in that the grantor's covenant in the special warranty deed:
option_a: protects all subsequent owners of the property.
option_b: applies only to a definite limited time.
option_c: is implied and is not written in full.
option_d: covers the time back to the original title.
feedback: Under a special warranty deed, an owner transfers property guaranteeing the quality of title only during the period of his or her ownership, leaving subsequent buyers vulnerable to prior claims. The more common deed in most states is the general warranty, because it establishes the ownership trail and validity of title going back to the original recorded ownership (for example, the purchase of Manhattan Island and all subsequent divisions, subdivisions and resales).

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A unique characteristic of a special assessment tax is that
option_a: it only applies to properties which will benefit from the public improvement
option_b: the equalization board discounts levies for properties not affected by the public improvement
option_c: more valuable properties which stand to benefit will pay proportionately more taxes
option_d: it creates an involuntary junior lien on the property
feedback: A special assessment is a tax levied against specific properties that will benefit from a public improvement

subject: A5 Real Estate Math
question_text: If a home appraised for $93,000, and Mr. Nickel, the borrower, puts 20% down, what would his loan amount be?
feedback: To arrive at the loan amount, take the $93,000 appraisal amount and multiply that by 80% (loan needed) for a total loan amount of $74,400.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A summary of the recorded history of matters that affect the title to a specific parcel of real property is called a(n):
feedback: Abstracts give an at-a-glance history of all the recorded documents associated with a property, including changes in title, liens, mortgages taken out and paid off, transfers through death and so forth. Chain of title is a history of ownership only.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is an example of involuntary alienation?
option_a: letting another person plant crops on an unused portion of a piece of land
option_b: giving a piece of land to the zoo
option_c: having a piece of land sold for delinquent taxes
option_d: selling a property to pay off debts
feedback: In legal terms "alienation" means transferring, which can be voluntary--as in the case of a sale--or "involuntary," which means the property is taken from the owner without his consent. In addition to enforcing liens, involuntary alienation can also occur through eminent domain, adverse possession and other circumstances.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When an owner sues the government to get them to pay for taking his property, this is known as:
feedback: Inverse condemnation is a term used in the law to describe a situation in which the government takes private property but fails to pay the just compensation required by the Constitution. In order to be compensated, the owner must then sue the government. In such cases the owner is the plaintiff and that is why the action is called inverse. The order of parties is reversed, as compared to direct condemnation where the government is the plaintiff who sues a defendant-owner to take his property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A tax bill derived by calculating the cost-per-front-foot, is a:
feedback: Special assessments are generally for repayment to the government for the cost of installing utilities, or curbs and gutters. Each owner is billed their share of the expense calculated by their proportion of the benefit to their property. The amount is normally based on the number of feet along the front of your lot, hence the term front foot fee. On a typical house, this fee can vary. The bill is paid annually, and when the home is sold, the expense transfers to the new homeowner.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Emblements are:
feedback: Emblements are annual crops, produced by cultivation, which legally belong to a tenant with an implied right of harvest. They are treated as the tenant's personal property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What type of title insurance policy covers everything?
option_d: No title insurance policy covers everything
feedback: Be careful of absolute words such as words like ALL, EVERYTHING, ALWAYS or NEVER- these words rarely apply to real estate concepts.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A broker shows a prospective buyer a home. The buyer makes an offer and the seller accepts. The broker, buyer, and seller then go to the Reliable Escrow Company where the buyer makes out a personal check for a $1,000 deposit payable to Reliable Escrow Company and hands it to the escrow officer. Which of the following is correct?
option_a: The broker has to keep a record of all checks deposited into his personal bank account
option_b: No record is required to be kept since the check was made out directly the escrow company and was not delivered by the broker
option_c: The broker has to keep a record of the check in his record of all trust funds received and not placed in his trust account
option_d: The broker can keep a digital record of the check on his phone only, as long as the governing real estate body knows where his phone is kept
feedback: Brokers are generally required to keep records of all financial dealings related to real estate transactions they are brokering.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Roberto owns an apartment building and wants to exchange his property in a 1031 tax deferred exchange in order to defer paying taxes in the year of the exchange. He could exchange his apartment building for:
option_a: a personal residence with an existing
feedback: null

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is required for a valid deed?
option_c: Eviction on any day of the week except on Sundays
option_d: Use of the phrase to have and to hold
feedback: Property deeds are used to convey real property from a grantor (seller) to a grantee (buyer). For a deed to be legally operative, several essentials must exist including the identification of the grantor and grantee, and an adequate legal description of the property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The unadjusted basis of a taxpayer's residence would be its:
feedback: For federal income tax purposes in determining gain or loss on real property, the term "unadjusted basis" most nearly means "original cost". NOTE - This question asks about "unadjusted basis", not "adjusted basis". Sometimes you may also see unadjusted basis simply referred to as "basis"

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is treated as a credit on the seller's closing statement?
option_a: none of the other options are correct
feedback: If a seller has already paid for a period of property tax that must be reimbursed by the buyer, it is a credit on the seller's closing statement. Prepaid premiums, such as insurance premiums paid in advance by the seller, are not typically treated as a credit on the seller's closing statement because these payments are specific to the seller’s policy and coverage period. When a property is sold, the buyer will usually obtain their own insurance policy effective as of the closing date. Therefore, any prepaid insurance premiums would not be credited to the seller in the transaction. Prepaid rents from tenants are actually credited to the buyer, not the seller, on a closing statement. If tenants have paid rent in advance for a period that extends past the closing date, the buyer is entitled to this rent, as they will be the property owner during that time.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: An installment sale represents a tax advantage because:
option_b: it defers payment of capital gains.
feedback: An installment sale allows the taxpayer to postpone the receipt and reporting of income tax to future years when his/her other income may be lower. Thus, the seller of a parcel of land in a land installment contract can avoid paying the entire tax on the gain in the year of sale.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Boot refers to:
feedback: The term "boot" is not used in the Internal Revenue Code or the Regulations, but is commonly used in discussing the tax consequences of a Section 1031 tax-deferred exchange. For example, if a property worth $150,000 is exchanged for a property worth $145,000 plus $5000 cash, the cash is considered the boot. Receipt of boot doesn't disqualify an exchange, but it may result in a taxable gain.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Personal income taxes are structured using rates that are:
feedback: Progressive means the more you make, the more you pay. It might also be termed "graduated."

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The selling price on a buyer's closing statement is treated as:
option_c: None of the other options are correct
feedback: A closing statement is a detailed cash accounting of a real estate transaction prepared by a broker, escrow officer, attorney or other persons designated to process the mechanics of the sales, showing all cash received, all charges and credits made and all cash paid out in the transaction. Selling price on the buyer's closing statement is treated as a debit to the buyer.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: To determine property taxes on a residence, an assessor will:
option_a: total all land and improvements and assess them together, then multiply each by the different tax rates for land and improvements.
option_b: assess land and improvements separately, then multiply the total by one tax rate.
option_c: total all land and improvements and assess them together, then multiply by one tax rate.
option_d: assess land and improvements separately, then multiply each by the different tax rates for land and improvements.
feedback: Land and improvements are assessed separately, then added together and multiplied by one tax rate.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Under the federal Lead-Based Paint Hazard Reduction Act, which statement is TRUE?
option_a: All residential housing built prior to 1978 must be tested for the presence of lead-based paint before being listed for sale or rent.
option_b: A lead hazard pamphlet must be distributed to all prospective buyers but not to tenants.
option_c: Purchasers of housing built before 1978 must be given five days to test the property for the presence of lead-based paint.
option_d: A disclosure statement must be included with all sales contracts and leases involving residential properties built prior to 1978.
feedback: Although no remediation of the property is required, a lead-based paint warning disclosure statement must be completed and signed by the parties and a copy included with any sales contract or lease for a residential property built before 1978. In the disclosure statement, buyers and renters acknowledge receiving a HUD booklet about protecting their families from lead-based paint hazards. The pamphlet "Protect Your Family From Lead in the Home" must be provided to lessees as well as to buyers. The length of time given the buyer to inspect the property is listed in the lead-based paint disclosure provided by the seller.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: All of the following are examples of private controls of real property EXCEPT:
option_b: a requirement to make monthly payments to maintain common elements within the complex.
option_c: a condition stating the property may not be used to sell alcohol.
option_d: criteria that establish the color of house paint that can be used.
feedback: Certificates of occupancy are public control created by zoning laws. Deed conditions, Owner association or cooperative fees and covenants on paint color are all private controls.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: An example of involuntary alienation of property is
feedback: Condemnation is the operation of a government to take private property for public use and is an involuntary transfer of the property. Property inherited or given as a gift transfers voluntarily. A quitclaim deed voluntarily transfers the interest a grantor may have in a property to a grantee with no covenants or warranties.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: General real estate property taxes levied for the operation of the government are called:
feedback: The general real estate property taxes, the "ad valorem" tax, creates a specific lien on a particular property. The word "general" in this question denotes that this tax is different from a special assessment tax (which is levied on real estate to fund a public improvement that also benefits the property being taxed). Ad Valorem means "according to value", and an ad valorem tax is a tax based on the assessed value of an item. The most common ad valorem taxes are property taxes levied on real estate.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The construction of a family room, additional bedroom, and extra bath has been completed on the owner's home. Before the addition can be used, which of the following is TRUE?
option_a: The bath must be inspected by the plumbing inspector.
option_b: A certificate of occupancy must be issued.
option_c: The municipality must issue a building permit.
option_d: The municipality must issue a conditional-use permit.
feedback: A certificate of occupancy grants permission to occupy a completed building structure after it has been inspected. A building inspector may inspect the plumbing during the actual building process before completion. A building permit is issued before construction begins. A conditional use permit does not relate to actual building construction but permits use of a property for a use related to, but not permitted by, zoning ordinances.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: An annual crop that is considered personal property is called a(n)
feedback: Annually cultivated crops such as fruit, vegetables, and grains are known as emblements and are considered personal property. A fixture is an item that was once personal property, but that has been attached or affixed to real estate and has become real property. A fixture is tangible and transfers with the property unless detached from the property prior to its sale. An appurtenance is anything that attaches to and runs with the land such as improvements, rights and fixtures.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A "chain of title" is:
option_a: the list of all parties who have ever owned real estate in a county.
option_b: an involuntary conveyance of title by statutory rules of descent.
option_c: a chronology of successive owners of record of a parcel of real estate.
option_d: the bundle of rights linked to the recorded title to a parcel.
feedback: A "chain of title" refers to the series of owners of record relating to a specific property, dating back to the original grant of title from the state to a private party. It is a chronological list (as shown in title records).

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A competent and disinterested person who is authorized by another person to act in his or her place and sign a contract of sale is called:
feedback: An attorney-in-fact is the person named in a power of attorney document. An attorney-in-fact is a fiduciary of the person they are representing, so they must remain "disinterested" - acting in an objective manner without any hidden motivation or prospect of gain. For example, a person who made a secret deal to sign a contract contrary to his client's best interests in exchange for an under-the-table payment would not be "disinterested."

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When a prospective buyer makes a written purchase offer that the seller accepts, then the:
option_a: Buyer receives legal title to the property.
option_b: Seller grants the buyer ownership rights.
option_c: Buyer receives equitable title to the property.
option_d: Buyer may take possession of the real estate.
feedback: "Equitable title" means that the prospective buyer has obtained the right to acquire ownership of a property currently owned and occupied by another.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A person agrees to sell a property for $500,000. The buyer gives the seller $150 as valuable consideration for a six-month option. Which of the following statements is true?
option_a: The buyer must have at least 5% down as valuable consideration
option_b: The seller cannot accept money for the option
option_c: The $150 is valuable consideration if the seller accepts it
option_d: The buyer must have at least 20% down
feedback: "Valuable consideration" is a necessary component of all contracts. It is the benefit one party receives in exchange for granting benefit to the other. Generally it is money in any amount both parties agree to, though it can take other forms such as personal property, work or refraining from an act. If the seller of a home accepts $150 to keep an option to buy open on the property, it will be considered the "consideration" necessary to form a valid contract.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What is required if personal property is used as consideration in the purchase of real property?
option_b: A current appraisal of the personal property
feedback: A bill of sale is a document that transfers ownership of goods (personal property) from one person to another.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The primary purpose of a deed is to:
feedback: A deed is the instrument by which ownership of a property is transferred from one person to another, while a title is evidence of that ownership. A title is evidence of ownership, a deed is evidence of the transfer.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A tax certificate
option_a: certifies to tax collectors that a property owner has paid all ad valorem taxes on the property for the calendar year
option_b: entitles its holder to apply for a tax deed after a certain period
option_c: exempts its holder from paying taxes on the particular property referenced by the certificate
option_d: waives a property owner's rights of redemption in a foreclosure
feedback: After a period of time specified by law, the holder of the tax certificate on a property may then apply for a tax deed

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A borrower defaulted on his home mortgage loan payments, and the lender obtained a court order to foreclose on the property. At the foreclosure sale, the property sold for $164,000; the unpaid balance on the loan at the time of foreclosure was $178,000. What must the lender do to recover the $14,000 that the borrower still owes?
feedback: A deficiency judgment is an unsecured money judgment against a borrower whose mortgage foreclosure sale did not produce sufficient funds to pay the underlying promissory note, or loan, in full.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following may appear on a closing statement as a credit to the buyer?
option_a: Property taxes after the closing date of March 31st until June 30th
option_b: Property taxes after November 15th to the date of closing
option_d: The balance of an assumed loan
feedback: A loan assumption is when a buyer takes over the seller’s loan. Since the buyer does not need to bring that loan amount to closing, it appears as a credit to the buyer.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The best way to discover a flaw in the recorded title to a piece of real property is by:
feedback: A title search ensures the seller has the legal right to sell and checks for liens or encumbrances.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: If a developer wants to build a commercial building closer to the street than is permitted by the local zoning ordinance because the shape of the lot makes a standard setback impossible, the developer should seek a
feedback: A variance allows deviation from zoning rules when strict compliance is impractical.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Joe conveys property to Vivian by delivering a deed. The deed contains six covenants. This is MOST likely a:
feedback: A warranty deed includes six covenants that guarantee clear title and the right to transfer.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following statements concerning the addition or subtraction of land is correct?
option_a: Accretion is the gradual deposit of soil through natural causes
option_b: Accretion is the gradual loss of soil through natural causes
option_c: Erosion is the natural wear and tear of property improvements
option_d: Erosion is the gradual deposit of soil due to ocean tides
feedback: Accretion refers to gradual deposits of soil by natural forces, increasing land area.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The right of alienation is defined as:
option_b: the right to make a will
option_c: the right to transfer an interest in real property
option_d: the right to execute and deliver a deed
feedback: Alienation is the owner’s right to transfer property, whether by sale, gift, or will.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: An equitable right of redemption
option_a: allows a holder of a tax certificate to redeem it for a tax deed
option_b: gives a delinquent taxpayer a grace period prior to the tax sale to pay property taxes
option_c: gives a holder of a tax deed the right to acquire the property named in the tax certificate
option_d: gives a delinquent taxpayer a grace period after the tax sale to pay property taxes
feedback: If the taxpayer can redeem the property by paying the delinquent taxes and any other charges before the tax sale occurs, this right is known as an equitable right of redemption

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Three years after signing a five-year lease, business A outgrew its rental space. Business A would like business B to take on the responsibility of the lease for the remaining two years. Business B can do this through:
feedback: An assignment transfers the rights and obligations of a lease to another party, with landlord consent.

subject: A5 Real Estate Math
question_text: Jones sold his home for $17,200. If this represents 9% more than what he paid for it, the cost of the home was most nearly:
feedback:
Selling price = cost + 9% or 109%
 $17,200 divided by 1.09 = $15,779
 Closest answer is $15,800

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Real estate taxes on property that you own or hold for investment can be imposed by which of the following taxing entities?
option_d: The county and local levels of government
feedback: Real estate property taxes are imposed by "taxing entities" or "taxing districts" at county and local levels of government.  School districts and other infrastructure municipalities can also levy real property taxes

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following best describes earnest money?
option_a: The consideration for the sale of the property
option_c: The money provided by the purchaser at the time of entering into a contract
option_d: The money deposited by the purchaser with the broker or seller to pay for the expenses of examining the title
feedback: Earnest money is a good faith deposit showing the buyer’s intent to complete the purchase.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following could not be real property?
feedback: A mortgage is a personal obligation, not real property. Mineral rights, easements, and fixtures are real property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The covenant in a deed that states that the grantor is the owner and has the right to convey the title is called the:
feedback: The covenant of seisin guarantees the grantor holds title and has the right to transfer it.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Unless there is a deed restriction that states otherwise, the holder of a life estate may do all of the following to their estate except:
feedback: A life estate ends at death and cannot be willed to heirs, though it can be rented, sold, or mortgaged.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A property tax bill might include taxes due for such services as
option_b: a new grocery store to be built near a residential neighborhood
feedback: County and local governments establish tax districts to collect funds for providing specific services. The major tax district in most areas is the school district. Other important tax districts are those for fire protection, community colleges, and parks

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When a claim is settled by a title insurance company, the company acquires all rights and claims of the insured against any other person who is responsible for the loss. This is known as what?
feedback: Subrogation means the title insurer takes over the insured’s legal rights to pursue recovery from a responsible third party.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When a buyer assumes a mortgage, prepaid insurance and tax reserves will appear on a typical closing statement as a:
option_a: credit to the buyer and a debit to the seller
option_d: debit to the buyer and a credit to the seller
feedback: The seller has already paid these, so the buyer must reimburse the seller (debit to buyer, credit to seller).

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: On a settlement statement, prorations for real estate taxes paid in arrears are shown as a:
option_a: debit to the seller and a credit to the buyer
option_b: credit to both the seller and the buyer
option_c: debit to both the seller and the buyer
option_d: credit to the seller and a debit to the buyer
feedback: Taxes paid in arrears are owed by the seller up to the closing date, so they appear as a debit to the seller and credit to the buyer.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Transferring a property's title over to another person can also be called:
feedback: In property law, alienation is the voluntary act of an owner of some property transferring, or otherwise disposing, of the property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The sudden tearing away or removal of land by action of water is:
feedback: In real property law, avulsion refers to a sudden loss or addition to land, which results from the action of water. It differs from erosion, which describes a gradual loss or addition to land resulting from the action of water. NOTE - Avulsion is SUDDEN, while erosion is GRADUAL. Accretion the process of growth or increase.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: The assessed value of real property is determined by the
option_a: real market value of the property
option_d: county or township assessors or appraisers
feedback: General property taxes are levied on an ad valorem basis, meaning that they are based on the assessed value of the property. Assessed value is determined according to state law, usually by a county or township assessor or appraiser

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A defect or a cloud on title to property may be cured by:
option_a: bringing an action to register the title.
option_b: obtaining proof of satisfaction from all appropriate parties.
option_c: paying cash for the property at the settlement.
option_d: bringing an action to repudiate the title.
feedback: In United States property law, a cloud on title or title defect refers to any irregularity in the chain of title for a property that would give a reasonable person pause before accepting a conveyance of title. It can be cured by obtaining proof of satisfaction between the affected parties, or by a "quiet title" order.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: If Joe dies without having made a will, it is said to be:
feedback: Intestacy is the condition of the estate of a person who dies without having made a valid will or other binding declaration. Alternatively this may also apply where a will or declaration has been made, but only applies to part of the estate; the remaining estate forms the "intestate estate".

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What do liens and easements have in common?
option_b: Both must be on public record to be valid
option_c: Neither can be done without the consent of the owner
option_d: Both are money claims against the property
feedback: Liens are, of course, serious in that they indicate the owner has failed to pay a debt secured directly or indirectly by the property. Easements, on the other hand, are a practical necessity for many residential properties. Both, however, result in an encumbrance on the affected property.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When the grantor does not wish to convey certain property rights, he or she:
option_a: may note the exceptions in the deed of conveyance.
option_b: must note the exceptions in a separate document.
option_c: may not do so, since the deed conveys the entire premises.
option_d: must convey the entire premises and have the grantee reconvey the rights to be retained by the grantor.
feedback: An Exception in Deed is a statement in a deed that reserves certain rights for the grantor- for example, if the grantor wants to sell a property but keep the mineral rights in the land.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: On a settlement statement, the selling price is ALWAYS:
feedback: On a closing statement for a real estate sale, a debit is an item that is charged to a party. The selling price is the point of reference for all debits and credits on a closing statement, and it is always a debit (debt to be paid) to the buyer.

subject: A5 Real Estate Math
question_text: If a home appraised for $93,000, and Mr. Nickel, the borrower, puts 20% down, what would his loan amount be?
feedback: To arrive at the loan amount, take the $93,000 appraisal amount and multiply that by 80% (loan needed) for a total loan amount of $74,400.

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Equalization factors are used to level out uneven valuations in certain areas due to high versus low value ranges.  An example of an equalization process is
option_a: re-assessment for properties in the area whose current values are not in line with nearby areas
option_b: an equalization board may multiply each assessed value in that county by a factor of 110%.
option_c: raising the assessed values across the state based upon the assessed value of the county
option_d: re-calculate the assessment process for each county on an annual basis
feedback: The jurisdiction may establish equalization factors to level out the unevenness of valuations. For instance, if assessed values of properties in one county are consistently ten percent below the average for other counties, an equalization board may multiply each assessed value in that county by a factor of 110% to raise them to the average level for the state

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Appeals of the assessed valuation may also be filed due to which of the following reasons?
option_a: The tax assessor’s information is incorrect
option_b: The owner of the property is new to the neighborhood
option_c: The owner is unable to pay the taxes because of a high assessment
option_d: The property should be assessed lower because it failed to sell when listed
feedback: According to local law, a property owner must present evidence that the assessor made an error to a review board or appeal board. Typical evidence would include market data on comparable properties that sold recently, or evidence that neighboring properties had less appreciation or even declined in assessed value in comparison with the protestor's property

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Which of the following must occur in order to qualify for a property exemption?
option_a: The property owner must reside in the property
option_b: The assessed value of the property must be above $500,000
option_c: The market value of the property must be above $500,000
option_d: The property must be larger than 1000 square feet
feedback: A property owner generally qualifies for a homestead exemption by meeting two criteria: is head of a family, resides in the property for a required length of time

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Some governmental properties are exempt from real property taxation.  Another exempted property is
option_a: the campaign office of a candidate
option_b: the rental space for a commercial location
option_d: a wedding chapel owned by a private citizen
feedback: Most states exempt certain types of property from property taxes. Certain classes of property owner may also be exempted or have a reduced liability. Exempted are: Government-owned properties such as real properties owned by federal, state, and local governments, Properties owned by non-profit organizations such as real properties owned by churches and non-profit from organizations

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: For a tax district to determine the tax rate, the tax district must begin by
option_a: issuing the tax bills to jurisdiction property owners
option_b: determining funding requirements to provide services
feedback: The derivation of a tax rate, or millage rate, begins with the taxing body determining its funding requirements to provide services for the year. This requirement is formalized in the annual budget

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Some taxing districts place a cap on the millage rate for property taxes.  If the taxing district decides to place a cap on the tax rate, which of the following will apply?
option_a: The taxing district must limit their budget requirements
option_b: The taxing district must increase the tax rate to meet the budgetary requirements
option_c: The taxing district must reassess the residential properties in the area
option_d: The taxing district must avoid using the equalization factor
feedback: Some states, counties or other taxing districts place limitations or caps on the absolute millage rate or the annual increase in millage for property taxes. In such situations, taxing bodies are forced to limit their budget requirements, unless there has been a sufficient increase in tax base to produce the required funds without raising the millage rate

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Each property owner's tax bill is determined by multiplying the tax rate for each taxing district times the ____________ of the property
feedback: Each property owner's tax bill is determined by multiplying the tax rate for each taxing district times the taxable value of the property. Taxable value is the assessed value after all exemptions, and adjustments have been taken into account

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: If the tax rate is .04 and the assessed valuation is $40,000, what is the amount of the total tax bill?

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A special assessment is a tax levied against certain properties to benefit public improvement.  For example,
option_b: trees for the property owner’s yard
feedback: A special assessment is a tax levied against specific properties that will benefit from a public improvement. Common examples are assessments for sidewalks, water service, and sewers

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: If the total cost of the improvement for which a special assessment has been levied is $250,000 and 50 properties will receive equal benefit from the project, what is the amount of the assessment that will be charged to each property?
feedback: The special assessment is based on the total cost of the improvement and is divided among the properties that will benefit from the improvement. Therefore, the calculation is as follows: $250,000 divided by 50 = $5000

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: If the assessment tax is not paid, the tax-collecting agency may enforce the tax lien in several different ways, depending upon the law.  Which of the following is an example of lien enforcement?
option_b: A tax deed which conveys title when a property is sold & conveyed for non-payment
option_c: Repossession of the property by the taxing authority
option_d: Forgiveness of the debt due to the inability to pay
feedback: The buyer of a tax certificate agrees to pay the taxes due. After a period of time specified by law, the holder of the tax certificate on a property may then apply for a tax deed

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A property has an assessed valuation of $200,000.  The tax rate is 4%.  The owner qualifies for a homestead exemption that will reduce the assessed valuation by $10,000.  What is the amount of the tax bill?

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: In states where legally enacted, a ____________ gives a defaulting taxpayer an opportunity to buy back the property and reclaim the title
feedback: There is usually a legally-prescribed redemption period during which the defaulted taxpayer has the right to buy back the property and reclaim title. If the taxpayer can redeem the property by paying the delinquent taxes and any other charges before the tax sale occurs, this right is known as an equitable right of redemption. If the taxpayer can redeem the property after the tax sale, this right is known as a statutory right of redemption

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: If the defaulting owner does not pay the taxes and fees due during the redemption period, which of the following will apply?
option_a: The person who purchased the property at the tax sale may then apply for a tax deed
option_b: The defaulting owner will be given an automatic extension to apply for a loan
option_c: The defaulting owner’s debt will be forgiven and will not result in the loss of the property
option_d: The defaulting owner has an unlimited period of time in which to redeem the property
feedback: If the defaulted taxpayer does not redeem the property within the allotted time, the state issues the tax deed to convey title to the person who purchased the property at the tax sale

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Which of the following can legally levy real property taxes?
feedback: Real estate property taxes are imposed by "taxing entities" or "taxing districts" at county and local levels of government. States may legally levy taxes on real property, but most delegate this power to counties, cities, townships and local taxing districts. The federal government does, however, tax income derived from real property and gains realized on the sale of real property

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Certain classes of property owner and types of property are exempted or immune from real property taxation in many areas.  The protected categories usually include
option_b: properties owned by a government agency
option_c: properties that comply with the Americans with Disabilities Act
feedback: Certain types of property are exempt from property taxes. Certain classes of property owner may also be exempted or have a reduced liability. Exemptions include government-owned properties, real properties owned by churches, and properties owned by other non-profit organizations

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: What is the purpose of an equalization factor in ad valorem taxation?
option_a: It modifies a local tax rate to bring it into conformity with statutory tax rates
option_b: It changes the assessed value of an individual property to make it reflect the assessed values of other properties in the same neighborhood
option_c: It adjusts assessments in a locality to make them more consistent with an average level for the state or other higher level jurisdiction
option_d: It adjusts the amount of the homestead exemption in a certain area to make it proportionally equivalent to the average homestead exemption in other areas
feedback: Equalization factors level out the unevenness of valuations for groups of properties. For instance, if assessed values of properties in one county are consistently ten percent below the average for other counties, an equalization board may multiply each assessed value in that county by a factor of 110% to raise them to the average level for the state

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: To qualify for a homestead exemption, a property owner generally must
option_b: have a house on the property
feedback: A property owner generally qualifies for a homestead exemption by meeting two criteria:  •is head of a family  •resides on the property for a required length of time. Some states also allow a single person to claim the homestead exemption

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: What is a tax levy?
option_a: The total of the assessed values of all real properties within a taxing jurisdiction
option_b: The sum of all the taxes a local taxing jurisdiction collects in a tax year
option_c: The action a taxing jurisdiction takes to impose a tax
option_d: The number of dollars per thousand  of assessed value a property owner must pay in property taxes
feedback: Taxing entities generate the annual revenues they require by levying taxes on the tax base. The tax rate, or millage rate, determines how much of a tax levy the tax base will receive. The tax rate for each taxing entity is calculated by dividing the amount of revenue required by the tax base. This rate is then applied to the taxable value of each individual real property to determine its tax levy

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A homeowner receives a tax bill that includes an amount for the library district, taxed at $1.00 per $1,000, and the fire protection district, taxed at $2.00 per $1,000.  How much does the taxpayer have to pay for these two items if the property's taxable value is $470,000?

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A town is replacing a sidewalk that serves five homes.  The length of the sidewalk is 200 feet.   Mary's property has 38 feet of front footage.  If the cost of the project to be paid by a special assessment is $7,000, what will Mary's assessment be?
feedback: $7000 (total cost) / 200 (total length) x 38 (Mary's portion) = $1,330

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: What is a tax deed?
option_a: A conveyance instrument for a property that is sold to enforce a tax lien
option_b: A document recorded in title records showing that property taxes have been paid
option_c: A notice to a homeowner that a tax lien has been entered against the property
option_d: A document that gives a municipal authority the power to collect an individual tax bill
feedback: A tax deed is a legal instrument for conveying title when a property is sold for non-payment of taxes. The application for a tax deed causes the taxing agency to institute a tax sale or tax foreclosure

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: The right of a property owner to redeem his or her property after a tax sale is called
option_b: an equitable right to acquire title
feedback: If the taxpayer can redeem the property after the tax sale, this right is known as a statutory right of redemption. In this case, the taxpayer must pay the amount paid by the winning bidder at the tax sale, plus any charges, additional taxes, or interest that may have accumulated

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Property managers have a _______ relationship with the property owner
feedback: The management agreement creates an agency relationship between the manager and the principal. This is a fiduciary relationship and, in general, the agent is charged with producing the greatest possible net return on the owner's investment while safeguarding the value of the investment for the owner/investor

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Effective gross income is defined as
option_b: the total of all rents and revenues generated by a property
option_c: potential gross income minus debt service and reserves
option_d: revenue from all sources minus losses from uncollected rents, vacancies, and evictions
feedback: The total of scheduled rents plus revenues from such sources as vending services, storage charges, late fees, utilities, and contracts is the potential gross income. Subtracting losses caused by uncollected rents, vacancies and evictions gives effective gross income

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: The efficiency of marketing activities can be measured in terms of
option_a: cost per tenant prospect generated per lease
option_b: number of ads produced per marketing dollar
option_c: dollars expended per square foot of vacant space
option_d: percentage of reserves expended on marketing
feedback: The efficiency of marketing activities can be judged in terms of how many prospects per completed lease they generate. The lower the cost per prospect per lease, the more effective and efficient the program

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Why does a manager need to keep tenants happy?
option_a: Happy tenants make fewer demands for services
option_b: Managers are contractually required to please tenants
option_c: Unhappy tenants make the owner look bad
option_d: High tenant turnover increases expenses and reduces profits
feedback: Happy tenants remain in a rented space longer than unhappy tenants. High tenant turnover adds to increased advertising and redecorating expenses

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: What are the three kinds of maintenance a manager has to carry out for a managed property?
feedback: The foremost maintenance objective is generally to preserve the value of the physical asset for the owner over the long term. Three general types of maintenance are required to keep a property in serviceable condition: routine (day-to-day), preventive (scheduled), and corrective (repairs and replacements)

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: The Americans with Disabilities Act requires property managers to
option_a: ensure that disabled employees have the same level of access to facilities that all employees have
option_c: remove all existing barriers to the free movement of disabled persons within the property, regardless of the cost
option_d: remodel the ground floor of the property in accordance with ADA standards if it was built before 1978
feedback: Employers with at least fifteen employees must follow nondiscriminatory employment and hiring practices and make reasonable accommodations to enable disabled employees to perform essential functions of their jobs. Modifications to the physical components of the building may be necessary to provide the required access to tenants and their customers. Existing barriers must be removed when the removal is "readily achievable," that is, when cost is not prohibitive

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Trust funds to be handled by a property manager are likely to include all of the following except
option_b: cash for the management firm’s operating expenses
option_d: capital contributions from the property owner
feedback: Managers are responsible for proper handling of monies belonging to other parties (trust funds) that come into the manager's hands in the course of doing business. For property managers, such trust funds include rents collected from tenants, security deposits, and capital contributions from the property owner. Funds belonging to the management firm must not be commingled with those of the client

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: What kind of agency is commonly created by a management agreement?
feedback: Property managers are usually considered to be general agents empowered to perform some or all of the ongoing tasks and duties of operating the property, including the authority to enter into contracts

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: The rights, duties, and liabilities of the landlord and manager are
option_a: apportioned under the terms of the management contract
option_d: regulated by the Universal Landlord Tenant Relations Act
feedback: Both the manager and the landlord have rights, duties and liabilities under the terms of the management contract. How these are apportioned should be clearly stated in the agreement

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Which of the following describes a gross lease?
option_a: The tenant pays a base rent plus some or all of the operating expenses
option_b: The tenant pays a fixed rent, and the landlord pays all operating expenses
option_c: The tenant pays a base rent plus an amount based on income generated in the leased space
option_d: The tenant pays a rent that increases at specified times over the lease term
feedback: In a gross lease, the tenant pays an established, fixed rent, and the landlord pays all property operating expenses, such as taxes, insurance, utilities, and other services. This is the arrangement commonly used in residential leases. If the tenant pays some of the operating expenses, the lease is a form of net lease

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: If an apartment contains a refrigerator that is not included in the lease,
option_a: the lessee is required to buy it from the landlord
option_b: the landlord is required to remove it
option_c: the lease is invalidated because of an incomplete property description
option_d: the property manager does not have to maintain it
feedback: The lease should set forth items that are excluded or included in the leased property. For instance, a residential lease may include built-in appliances such as dishwashers but exclude freestanding ones, such as refrigerators. At issue for the landlord is the cost of maintenance. If a refrigerator is not included, it does not have to be maintained by the property manager

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: A basic responsibility of a landlord is to
option_a: provide leased space at market rental rates
option_c: keep the rental space freshly painted
option_d: refrain from entering the leased space at any time during the lease term
feedback: The landlord (by way of the property manager), is expected to deliver a property that is habitable. This means keeping heating, plumbing, cooling, and electrical systems in good repair as well as maintaining serviceability of floors, stairways, railings, roofs, and windows

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: How does a constructive eviction occur?
option_a: A landlord obtains a court order to force the tenant to vacate the leased premises
option_b: A court officer forcibly removes the tenant from the premises
option_c: A tenant declares a landlord in default and vacates the leased premises
option_d: A landlord declares a tenant in default and takes possession of the leased premises
feedback: A constructive eviction occurs when a tenant vacates the leased premises and declares the lease void, claiming that the landlord's actions have made the premises unfit for the purpose described in the lease. The tenant must prove that it was the landlord's actions that were responsible and may be able to recover damages

subject: B6 Governmental Controls
question_text: Among the essential elements of a management plan is consideration of
option_a: the competitive market for the property
feedback: In preparing a management plan, a manager must consider the owner's objectives, including financial goals; the competitive market for the property, both local and regional, depending on the property type; and the features of the particular property.
