option_a: the crime statistics for the neighborhood where the property is located
option_c: all material facts that might affect the clients‘ interest in the property transaction
option_d: the demographic data for a given area
feedback: An agent has the duty to inform the client of all material facts, reports, and rumors that might affect the client's interests in the property transaction

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A contract for deed is also called
feedback: A contract for deed is also called a land contract, an installment sale, a conditional sales contract, and an agreement for deed

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Upon the death of the owner, a life estate passes to
option_a: the original owner or other named person
feedback: Upon the death of the owner or other named individual, the estate passes to the original owner or another named party

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following life estates is created by someone other than the owner?
feedback: A legal life estate is created by state law as opposed to being created by a property owner's agreement

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is a right provided to a life tenant?
feedback: The life tenant holds the rights of possession during a life estate and reverts to the original owner or to another designated individual upon death

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: "Quiet enjoyment and possession" means:
option_a: Right to tell neighbors to be quiet
option_c: Possession without disturbance from owner of paramount title
feedback: Tenant has the right to enjoy property without interference from superior title holders.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A contract in which all parties have fulfilled their promises is a(n):
feedback: An executed contract has been fully performed by all parties.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Homestead, dower and curtesy, and elective share are examples of
option_c: an estate created by an owner's agreement
feedback: The major forms of legal life estate are the homestead, dower and curtesy, and elective share

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When a single individual or entity owns a fee or life estate in a real property, the type of ownership is
feedback: If a single party owns the fee or life estate, the ownership is a tenancy in severalty

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Three people have identical rights but unequal shares in a property, share an indivisible interest, and may sell or transfer their interest without consent of the others. This type of ownership is
feedback: Tenancy in common, co-tenants enjoy an individually owned, undivided interest; any ownership share possible; no survivorship

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The "four unities" required to create a joint tenancy include which of the following conditions?
option_a: Parties must acquire respective interests at the same time
option_b: Parties must be legally married at the time of acquiring interest
option_d: Parties must have joint financial resources
feedback: All owners must acquire the property at the same time, use the same deed, acquire equal interests, and share in equal rights of possession

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Unlike tenants in common, joint tenants
option_a: own distinct portions of the physical property
option_b: cannot will their interest to a party outside the tenancy
option_c: may own unequal shares of the property
option_d: cannot sell their interest to outside parties
feedback: The survivorship feature of joint tenancy presents an advantage to tenancy in common, in that interests pass without probate proceedings. On the other hand, joint tenants relinquish any ability to will their interest to parties outside of the tenancy

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: In a community property state, a basic distinction is made between
option_a: property acquired together and property acquired separately over the duration of the marriage
option_b: property owned privately versus property owned by the state
option_c: property acquired during a marriage and property already owned by each party at the time of marriage
option_d: property acquired during the marriage and property acquired after the marriage
feedback: Community property law distinguishes real and personal property into categories of separate and community property. Separate property belongs to one spouse; community property belongs to both spouses equally

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Who are the essential parties involved in an estate in trust?
feedback: In an estate in trust, a fee owner-- the grantor or trustor-- transfers legal title to a fiduciary-- the trustee-- who holds and manages the estate for the benefit of another party, the beneficiary

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The distinguishing features of a condominium estate are
option_a: ownership of a share in an association that owns one's apartment
option_b: tenancy in common interest in airspace and common areas of the property
option_c: fee simple ownership of the airspace in a unit and an undivided share of the entire property's common areas
option_d: fee simple ownership of a pro rata share of the entire property
feedback: Multi-unit residential or commercial properties. It combines ownership of a fee simple interest in the airspace within a unit with ownership of an undivided share, as a tenant in common

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Who owns the property in a time-share estate?
option_a: Ownership is shared by the developer and the broker
option_b: The property is owned by tenants in common or by a freehold owner who leases on a time-share basis
option_c: A real estate investment trust holds a fee simple estate
option_d: A general partner holds a fee simple interest and interval estates are owned by limited partners
feedback: Timeshare ownership is a fee or leasehold interest in a property whose owners or tenants agree to use the property on a periodic, non-overlapping basis

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is true of a cooperative?
option_a: A cooperative may hold an owner liable for the unpaid operating expenses of other owners
option_b: The owners have a fee simple interest in the airspace of their respective apartments
option_c: Owners may retain their apartments even if they sell their stock in the cooperative
option_d: The proprietary lease is guaranteed to have a fixed rate of rent over the life of the lease term
feedback: Since the corporation owns an undivided interest in the cooperative property, debts and financial obligations apply to the property as a whole, not to individual units as in a condominium

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: One difference between a cooperative estate and a condominium estate is that
option_a: a default by a coop owner may cause a foreclosure on the entire property instead of just a single unit, as with a condominium
option_b: the condominium owner must pay expenses as well as rent
option_c: the coop owner owns stock and a freehold real estate interest whereas the condominium owner simply owns real estate
option_d: the condominium owner owns the common elements and the airspace whereas the coop owner only owns the apartment
feedback: Should the cooperative corporation fail to meet its obligations, creditors and mortgagees may foreclose on the entire property. A completed foreclosure would terminate the shareholders' proprietary lease, and bankrupt the owning corporation. Compare this situation with that of a condominium, in which an individual's failure to pay endangers only that individual's unit, not the entire property

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A unique feature of a land trust is that
option_a: the trustee controls both the trustor and the beneficiary
option_b: the trustee takes ownership of both land and improvements
option_c: the identity of the beneficiary may not be identified
option_d: the properties in the trust are probated in the states where the properties are located
feedback: Beneficiary identity not on record - public records do not identify the beneficiary; the beneficiary owns and enjoys the property in secrecy

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Ownership in severalty is synonymous with which of the following terms?
feedback: If a single party owns the fee or life estate, the ownership is a tenancy in severalty. Synonyms are sole ownership, ownership in severalty, and estate in severalty

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The most common form of co-ownership when the owners are not married is the
feedback: The tenancy in common, also known as the estate in common, is the most common form of co-ownership when the owners are not married

subject: B3 Agency
question_text: The parties to the agency relationship are identified as the
feedback: The parties to the relationship are the principal (a client), the agent (a broker), and the customer (a third party)

subject: B3 Agency
question_text: The “third party” in an agency relationship is known as the
feedback: The parties to the relationship are the principal (a client), the agent (a broker), and the customer (a third party)

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is a defining characteristic of tenancy in common?
feedback: two or more owners, identical rights, interests individually owned, electable ownership shares, no survivorship, no unity of time.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Joint tenants may only convey their interests to outside parties as
feedback: In a joint tenancy, two or more persons collectively own a property as if they were a single person. Rights and interests are indivisible and equal: each has a shared interest in the whole property which cannot be divided up. Joint tenants may only convey their interests to outside parties as tenant-in-common interests. One cannot convey a joint tenant interest

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: To create a joint tenancy, all owners must
option_a: occupy the property at the same time
option_c: own equal shares in the property
option_d: hold separate titles to their individual interests
feedback: Joint tenants own equal shares in the property, without exception. If there are four co-tenants, each owns 25% of the property. If there are ten co-tenants, each owns 10%

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A ____________ is a legal avenue for an owner who wants to dispose of his or her interest against the wishes of other co-owners
feedback: A partition suit is a legal avenue for an owner who wants to dispose of his or her interest against the wishes of other co-owners. The suit petitions the court to divide, or partition, the property physically, according to the owner's respective rights and interests

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Community property consists of property
option_a: owned by either spouse at the time of the marriage
option_b: acquired by either spouse through inheritance during the marriage
option_d: acquired by either party during the marriage
feedback: Community property law distinguishes real and personal property into categories of separate and community property. Separate property belongs to one spouse; community property belongs to both spouses equally. Community property consists of all property earned or acquired by either party during the marriage

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: In an estate in trust, the fee owner is known as the
feedback: In an estate in trust, a fee owner-- the grantor or trustor-- transfers legal title to a fiduciary-- the trustee-- who holds and manages the estate for the benefit of another party, the beneficiary

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The distinguishing features of the land trust are all of the following EXCEPT
option_c: the beneficiary is not identified on public records
option_d: the term of the land trust is limited
feedback: beneficiary controls property, beneficiary controls trustee, beneficiary identity not on record, limited term.

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The unique aspect of the condominium is its _________________ interest in the airspace contained within the outer walls, floors, and ceiling of the building unit
feedback: The unique aspect of the condominium is its fee simple interest in the airspace contained within the outer walls, floors, and ceiling of the building unit. This airspace may include internal walls, which are not essential to the structural support of the building

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The common elements in a condominium include all of the following EXCEPT
feedback: Common elements are all portions of the property that are necessary for the existence, operation, and maintenance of the condominium units. Common elements include the land (if not leased); structural components of the building, such as exterior windows, roof, and foundation; physical operating systems supporting all units, such as plumbing, power, communications installations, and central air conditioning; recreational facilities; building and ground areas used non-exclusively, such as stairways, elevators, hallways, and laundry rooms

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Condominium properties are created by executing and recording a
option_a: management agreement and an owner’s association
option_b: condominium declaration and a master deed
option_d: legal description and a proprietary lease
feedback: Condominium properties are created by executing and recording a condominium declaration and a master deed

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following statements regarding condominiums is true?
option_a: The shareholders of a condominium acquire a proprietary lease
option_b: Each condominium unit is individually assessed and taxed
option_c: A lender may foreclose on the entire condominium building if a unit owner defaults
option_d: A condominium unit owner’s interest is personal property
feedback: Condominium units are individually assessed and taxed. The assessment pertains to the value of the exclusive interest in the apartment as well as the unit's pro-rata share of common elements

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following statements regarding cooperatives is true?
option_a: The shareholders of a cooperative acquire a proprietary lease
option_b: Each cooperative unit is individually assessed and taxed
option_c: A lender may foreclose on a single unit if the owner of that unit defaults
option_d: A cooperative unit owner’s interest is real property
feedback: In a cooperative, or co-op, one owns shares in a non-profit corporation or cooperative association, which in turn acquires and owns an apartment building as its principal asset. Along with this stock, the shareholder acquires a proprietary lease to occupy one of the apartment units

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The corporate entity of the cooperative association is the only party in the cooperative with
option_d: an ownership interest with individual units
feedback: The corporate entity of the cooperative association is the only party in the cooperative with a real property interest. The association's interest is an undivided interest in the entire property. There is no ownership interest in individual units, as with a condominium

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which type of ownership is described as a fee or leasehold interest in a property whose owners or tenants agree to use the property on a periodic, non-overlapping basis?
feedback: Time-share ownership is a fee or leasehold interest in a property whose owners or tenants agree to use the property on a periodic, non-overlapping basis. This type of ownership commonly concerns vacation and resort properties

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A freehold time-share is also known as
feedback: In a freehold time-share, or interval ownership estate, tenants in common own undivided interests in the property. Expense prorations and rules governing interval usage are established by separate agreement when the estate is acquired

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The co-op interest is transferred by
option_a: recording the deed in county records
option_b: delivering a deed to the buyer
option_c: assigning both the stock certificates and lease to the buyer
option_d: signing a living trust and transferring interest to the buyer
feedback: The co-op interest is transferred by assigning both the stock certificates and lease to the buyer

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The proprietor-tenant in a cooperative is responsible for the
option_a: unit's pro-rata share of the corporation's expenses
feedback: The co-op lease is called a proprietary lease because the tenant is an owner (proprietor) of the corporation that owns the property. The lease has no stated or fixed rent. Instead, the proprietor-tenant is responsible for the unit's pro-rata share of the corporation's expenses in supporting the cooperative. Unit owners pay monthly assessments

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is a feature of a corporation?
option_c: The corporation may be own real estate in severalty
option_d: The corporation may own real estate by the entirety
feedback: A corporation is a legal entity owned by stockholders.  An elected board of directors oversees the business. Officers and managers conduct day-to-day activities. Officers and directors may be held fully liable for the corporation’s actions, while shareholders are liable only to the extent of the value of their shares. Corporations, like individuals, may own real estate in severalty or as tenants in common

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is true of a tenancy in common?
option_b: The tenants share an indivisible interest
option_c: The tenants must acquire their interests at the same time
option_d: The tenants own equal shares of the estate
feedback: The tenants share an indivisible interest of the common elements

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When a tenant in common dies, what happens to the tenant's interest in the estate?
option_a: It is divided equally among the surviving tenants in common
option_b: The surviving tenants must buy the interest from the deceased tenant's heirs or sell their interests to the heirs
option_d: It passes by probate to the deceased tenant's heirs
feedback: A deceased co-tenant's estate passes by probate to the decedent's heirs and devisees rather than to the other tenants in common. Any number of heirs can share in the ownership of the willed tenancy

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is true of a joint tenancy?
option_a: The tenants can determine the size of the share owned by each tenant
option_b: The size of the tenant's shares is determined by the amount of equity each has invested in the property
option_c: The tenants have an equal and indivisible ownership interest
option_d: There can be no more than two co-owners, and each has a fifty percent interest
feedback: In a joint tenancy, two or more persons collectively own a property as if they were a single person. Rights and interests are indivisible and equal: each has a shared interest in the whole property which cannot be divided up. Joint tenants may only convey their interests to outside parties as tenant-in-common interests. One can not convey a joint tenant interest

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: In contrast to a tenancy in common, in a joint tenancy
option_a: there is a single title to the property
option_b: there are as many titles to the property as there are co-owners
option_c: title is held by a corporate entity
option_d: co-owners who are married hold separate titles
feedback: Whereas tenants in common hold separate title to their individual interests, joint tenants together hold a single title to the property. This is referred to as unity of ownership

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: If a joint tenant sells his or her interest to an outside party,
option_a: the new owner becomes a joint tenant
option_b: the new owner is a tenant in common
option_c: the joint tenancy terminates and all owners become tenants in common
option_d: the joint tenancy terminates and the owners must create a new joint tenancy to include the new owner
feedback: A joint tenant may transfer his or her interest in the property to an outside party, but only as a tenancy in common interest. Whoever acquires the interest co-owns the property as a tenant in common with the other joint tenants

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When a joint tenant dies, what happens to the tenant's interest in the estate?
option_a: It passes to the decedent's heirs, who become joint tenants
option_b: It passes as a tenancy in common to the decedent's heirs
option_c: The joint tenancy terminates and becomes a tenancy in common with the decedent's heirs and the surviving tenants as co-owners
option_d: It passes to the surviving joint tenants
feedback: n most states, joint tenants enjoy rights of survivorship: if a joint tenant dies, all interests and rights pass to the surviving joint tenants free from any claims of creditors or heirs. When only one joint tenant survives, the survivor's interest becomes an estate in severalty, and the joint tenancy is terminated

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The most common form of co-ownership when the owners are not married is
feedback: The tenancy in common, also known as the estate in common, is the most common form of co-ownership when the owners are not married

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: If a co-owner in a joint tenancy or tenancy in common wants to dispose of his or her interest against the wishes of the other co-owners, the co-owner can
option_a: sue in court for a termination of the tenancy and partition of the property
option_b: deed the property to himself or herself as a tenancy in severalty
option_c: create a trust with himself or herself as sole beneficiary
option_d: file a foreclosure suit to force the other owners to sell their interests
feedback: A partition suit is a legal avenue for an owner who wants to dispose of his or her interest against the wishes of other co-owners. The suit petitions the court to divide, or partition, the property physically, according to the owner's respective rights and interests. If this is not reasonably feasible, the court may order the property sold, whereupon the interests are liquidated and distributed proportionately

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Two individuals can own a house as tenants by the entireties if
option_a: they so elect at the time of acquiring title
feedback: Tenancy by the entireties is a form of ownership traditionally reserved for husband and wife

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: One of the benefits offered by ownership in a tenancy by the entireties is
option_a: the estate is subject to foreclosure only for jointly incurred debts
option_b: the estate may be terminated only by mutual agreement
option_c: the estate is not subject to foreclosure
option_d: a co-owner may transfer his or her fractional interest without the consent of other co-owners
feedback: No foreclosure for individual debts. The estate is subject to foreclosure only for jointly incurred debts

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When an estate is held in a trust, who holds legal title?
feedback: In an estate in trust, a fee owner-- the grantor or trustor-- transfers legal title to a fiduciary-- the trustee-- who holds and manages the estate for the benefit of another party, the beneficiary

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: When real property is held in a land trust, who controls the property?
option_d: The property manager appointed by the trustee
feedback: Beneficiary controls property - this includes occupancy and control of rents and sale proceeds

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A condominium owner can
option_a: sell or mortgage the condominium unit without hindrance from individual owners of neighboring units
option_b: sell the interest in the physical unit separately from the interest in the common elements
option_c: exclude non-owners from the unit owner's pro rata portion of the common elements
option_d: exclusively possess and use those portions of the common areas structurally or functionally necessary for the operation of the unit
feedback: Condominium units can be individually sold, mortgaged, or otherwise encumbered without interference from other unit owners

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The owner of a cooperative owns
option_a: shares in a corporation or association and a proprietary lease in a physical unit
option_b: a fee simple interest in a physical unit plus a tenancy in common in common elements
option_c: a tenancy in common in a physical unit and the common areas
option_d: a ground lease in the physical unit's pro rata share of land and a proprietary lease in the unit
feedback: In a cooperative, or co-op, one owns shares in a non-profit corporation or cooperative association, which in turn acquires and owns an apartment building as its principal asset. Along with this stock, the shareholder acquires a proprietary lease to occupy one of the apartment units

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: In a cooperative, real property is owned only by
option_b: the individual unit owners and the cooperative association
option_d: the corporate entity of the cooperative association
feedback: The corporate entity of the cooperative association is the only party in the cooperative with a real property interest. The association's interest is an undivided interest in the entire property. There is no ownership interest in individual units, as with a condominium

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: In a time-share freehold, owners acquire
option_a: undivided interests as tenants in common
option_b: a tenancy from period-to-period for a portion of a year
option_c: a pro rata share of a leased fee
option_d: a tenancy in severalty for a portion of a year
feedback: In a freehold time-share, or interval ownership estate, tenants in common own undivided interests in the property. Expense prorations and rules governing interval usage are established by separate agreement when the estate is acquired

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What is the principal purpose underlying legal descriptions of real property?
option_a: To create a consistent, unchanging standard for locating the property
option_b: To eliminate all possible boundary disputes
option_d: To eliminate cumbersome metes and bounds descriptions
feedback: A legal description of real property is one which accurately locates and identifies the boundaries of the subject parcel to a degree acceptable by courts of law in the state where the property is located. Legal description provides accuracy and consistency over time

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is a distinctive feature of metes and bounds descriptions?
option_a: They use meridians and base lines
option_b: They identify an enclosed area, beginning and ending at the same point
option_c: They use lot and block numbers.as the street address
option_d: They incorporate elevation into the descriptions
feedback: A metes and bounds description always identifies an enclosed area by starting at an origination point, called "point of beginning," or POB, and returning to the POB at the end of the description

subject: B3 Agency
question_text: In most instances, real estate brokerage is based on which of the following types of agency?
feedback: In most instances, real estate brokerage is based on a special agency. The principal hires a licensed broker to procure a ready, willing, and able buyer or seller

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A certain legal description contains the phrase "...southeasterly along Happ Road to the stone landmark..." What kind of description is this?
option_a: Plat survey plat of survey is
feedback: A metes and bounds description identities the point of beginning and describes the distance and direction from that point to the first monument, and thence to subsequent monuments and back to the beginning to define the property's enclosed perimeter

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What are the approximate dimensions of a township in the rectangular survey system?
feedback: A township is a square with six miles on each side. Its area is 36 square miles

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The area running north and south between meridians is a
feedback: The north-south area between consecutive meridians is called a range

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The area running east and west between base lines is a
feedback: The east-west area between two parallels is called a tier, or a township strip

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: How many sections are there in a township?
feedback: The rectangular survey system divides a township into thirty-six sections

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A section contains how many acres?
feedback: Each side of a section is one mile in length. Thus the area of a section is one square mile, or 640 acres

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: If a parcel does not have a lot and block number and is too irregular to be described as a fraction of a section, the legal description
option_b: will include a metes and bounds description
option_c: will use an estimate of the sectional fraction
option_d: will create a special reference number
feedback: For an irregular shape, such as a triangle, the full description has to include a metes and bounds or lot and block description

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The legal description of a parcel in a subdivision that has been recorded with lot and block numbers on a plat of survey is
option_a: the lot and block number, with section, township and meridian references
feedback: The subdivision plat map is recorded in the county where the subdivision is located. The recorded lot and block numbers of a subdivision parcel, along with its section, township and meridian reference, become the property's legal description

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A datum is a reference point used for legal descriptions of
option_c: properties located above or below the earth's surface
feedback: To describe property located above or below the earth's surface, a surveyor must know the property's elevation. Standard elevation reference points, called "datums," have been established throughout the country

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A description of a parcel of property which accurately locates and identifies the boundaries of the subject parcel to a degree acceptable by courts of law is known as
feedback: A legal description of real property is one which accurately locates and identifies the boundaries of the subject parcel to a degree acceptable by courts of law in the state where the property is located

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Methods employed in legal descriptions facilitate all the following EXCEPT
feedback: Legal descriptions facilitate transfers of ownership, prevent boundary disputes and assist in resolving problems with encroachments and chain of title

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following activities requires a legal description?
option_a: Creating a valid deed of conveyance
feedback: A legal description is required for: public recording, creating a valid deed of conveyance or lease, completing mortgage documents, executing and recording other legal documents

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A legal description provides a basis for
option_a: court rulings on nonconforming use and variances
option_b: court rulings on encroachments and easements
feedback: A legal description provides a basis for court rulings on encroachments and easements

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following legal description method is generally used in the East Coast states?
feedback: Since the metes and bounds method preceded the inception of the rectangular survey system, the older East Coast states generally employ metes and bounds descriptions. States in the Midwest and West predominantly use the rectangular survey system. Some states combine methods

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The north-south, longitudinal lines on the survey grid are called
feedback: The north-south, longitudinal lines on the survey grid are meridians. The principal meridian is the single designated meridian for identifying townships in the principal meridian's geographical "jurisdiction." There are 37 principal meridians in the national survey

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The southeast corner of every township is always identified as which section?
feedback: The sections in a township are numbered sequentially starting with Section 1 in the northeast corner, proceeding east to west across the top row, continuing from west to east across the next lower row, and so on, alternately, ending with Section 36 in the southeast corner

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: How many acres are contained in the SW1/4 and the SW1/2 of Section 16?

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Standard elevation reference points, called ___________, have been established throughout the country
feedback: To describe property located above or below the earth's surface, such as the air rights of a condominium, a surveyor must know the property's elevation. Standard elevation reference points, called datums, have been established throughout the country

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Surveyors have identified local elevation markers, called ____________, to provide reference elevations for nearby properties
feedback: In many cases, it is impractical for a surveyor to rely on a single datum for an entire surveying area. To simplify matters, surveyors have identified local elevation markers, called benchmarks, to provide reference elevations for nearby properties. Once a benchmark is registered, it provides a valid reference point for surveying other elevations in the immediate area

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is used in surveyed subdivisions?
feedback: The recorded plat method, or lot and block system is used in surveyed subdivisions

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A legal description of a property is one which
option_a: accurately locates and identifies the boundaries of the property and distinguishes it from all other properties
option_b: accurately identifies the boundaries of the property and describes the location and dimensions of any improvements on the property
option_c: is accepted by a licensed surveyor as suitable for inclusion in a survey of the property
option_d: written by an attorney licensed to practice real estate law in the state in which the property is located
feedback: A legal description of real property is one which accurately locates and identifies the boundaries of the subject parcel to a degree acceptable by courts of law in the state where the property is located. The general criterion for a legal description is that it alone provides sufficient data for a surveyor to locate the parcel. A legal description identifies the property as unique and distinct from all other properties

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What method of legal description was the first one used in America?
feedback: A metes and bounds description identifies the point of beginning and describes the distance and direction from that point to the first monument, and thence to subsequent monuments and back to the beginning to define the property's enclosed perimeter

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A certain property has the following general features: Which of the following statements would provide a (partial) metes and bounds description of the property?
option_a: Commencing at A, thence south 0 degrees east to B, thence north 45 degrees west to C, thence north 0 degrees west to D, thence north 90 degrees east to the point of beginning
option_b: Commencing at A, thence 90 degrees south to B, thence west 45 degrees north to C,  then east to the point of beginning
option_c: Commencing at A, thence south 90 degrees west to B, thence north 45 degrees west to C, thence north 0 degrees east to D, thence 90 degrees east to the point of beginning
option_d: Commencing at A, thence south 0 degrees east to B, thence west 45 degrees north to C, thence north 0 degrees east to D, thence east 0 degrees north to the point of beginning
feedback: The metes and bounds descriptions begin and end at the same point.  Only option A will get you back to the POB

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: The fundamental components of the metes and bounds system are
option_b: boundaries, distances, and a base line
feedback: A metes and bounds description identifies the boundaries of a parcel of real estate using reference points, distances, and angles. The description always identifies an enclosed area by starting at an origination point, called point of beginning, or POB, and returning to the POB at the end of the description

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A legal description in the rectangular survey system will also include a metes and bounds or lot and block description if the property
option_b: is more than six miles from a standard parallel
feedback: For an irregular shape, such as a triangle, the full description has to include a metes and bounds or lot and block description

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following best describes a meridian?
option_a: An east-west line that corresponds to a latitude on a rectangular survey grid
option_b: An east-west latitudinal  line 6 miles in length used to describe a township’s north or south border
option_c: A north-south longitudinal line on a rectangular survey grid
option_d: A north-south longitudinal line 6 miles in length used to describe a township’s east or west border
feedback: The north-south, longitudinal lines on a rectangular survey grid are meridians

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What is the purpose of a principal meridian and base parallel?
option_a: Used to divide townships into sections
option_c: To establish the distance between all meridians and parallels
option_d: To differentiate a tier from a range
feedback: The principal meridian is the single designated meridian for identifying townships in the principal meridian's geographical "jurisdiction." The base parallel or base line is the designated line for identifying townships

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: How far apart are parallels?
feedback: Parallels are for identifying townships. Townships are six miles by six miles in size, making parallels six miles apart

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is true?
option_a: A township is the area enclosed by principal meridians and base parallels
option_b: A township’s sections are six miles apart
option_c: There are 36 townships in a section
option_d: There are 36 sections in a township
feedback: A township is a square with six miles on each side. Its area is 36 square miles. Each section within a township is one square mile, making 36 sections in a township

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A range is defined by
option_c: the distance between a principal meridian and a base parallel
feedback: The "vertical" area between consecutive meridians is called a range. A range is identified by its relationship to the principal meridian. All ranges are six miles wide

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A tier is defined by
feedback: The "horizontal" area between two parallels is called a tier, or a township strip. A tier is identified by its relationship to the base parallel. All tiers are six miles wide

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What are the dimensions of a township?
feedback: A township is the area enclosed by the intersection of two consecutive meridians and two consecutive parallels. Since the parallels and meridians are six miles apart, a township is a square with six miles on each side. Its area is therefore 36 square miles

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: Sections of a township are numbered in which of the following directions?
option_a: From the northeast corner toward the west and on to the southeast corner, alternating direction in each row
option_b: From the northwest corner toward the east and on to the southwest corner, alternating direction in each row
option_c: North to south from left to right in each row, starting in the northwest corner
option_d: Clockwise in a spiral beginning in the northeast corner
feedback: The sections in a township are numbered sequentially starting with Section 1 in the northeast corner, proceeding east to west across the top row, continuing from west to east across the next lower row, and so on, alternately, ending with Section 36 in the southeast corner

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: What portion of a section is ten acres?
feedback: The size in acres of a subsection of a township is a fraction of 640 acres, since there are 640 acres in a section. Thus ten acres is 10/640, or 1/64th of a section

subject: A1 Real Property
question_text: A tract of land is 1/32nd of a section.  How many acres does this tract consist of?
feedback: A section is 640 acres. 640 ÷ 32 = 20 acres

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Red River, a navigable stream, forms the boundary between farmer Brown's property and that of a neighbor, Smith. Neighbor Smith allows a canoeist to cross the property to gain access to the river. The water traffic annoys farmer Brown. Does Brown have the right to stop the canoeist from using the river?
option_a: No, since Red River is a navigable stream.
option_b: Yes, since Brown is a riparian owner and owns to the center of the stream.
option_c: Yes, since Brown is a riparian owner and owns the bed of the stream.
option_d: Since Brown is a riparian owner, he owns the stream to the middle and can prevent the canoeist from using his one-half of the stream.
feedback: Riparian owners on navigable streams own to the center line and may restrict access to their half.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What is the best way to determine if there is an encroachment on your property?
feedback: A survey precisely shows boundaries and reveals encroachments.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A zoning law states that no structure on any lot can be constructed within 15 feet of the lot line. One owner obtained a one-time approval from the zoning board to build to a point 10 feet from the lot line. What did the zoning board grant that owner?
feedback: A variance allows deviation from zoning requirements for one property.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: In real estate sales, when a cooperating sub-agent performs acts, the agent is:
option_a: Not responsible since they are with another company.
option_b: Responsible only if they know of the acts.
option_c: Fully responsible for the acts of the sub-agent.
option_d: Partially responsible for the acts of the sub-agent.
feedback: An agent is liable if they are aware of the sub-agents improper acts.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Property owned by James was sold at a tax sale. He may regain full ownership by payment of the taxes, interest, and other charges within a specified time. What is this process called?
feedback: The statutory right of redemption lets an owner reclaim property after a tax sale.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What is usually true of contracts made by minors?
option_a: They are enforceable by all parties.
option_c: They are voidable by the vendee.
option_d: They are voidable by the vendor.
feedback: Minors can enforce contracts, but others cannot enforce against them without their consent.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following would be considered a violation of the Fair Housing Act of 1968?
option_a: A private club owns a building for the use of its members only and refuses to rent a unit to a couple who are not members.
option_b: A man who only owns one home refuses to sell to a man of Asian descent.
option_c: A man who owns four houses refuses to rent to a woman with two children.
option_d: A lender refuses to make a loan to Mr. Jack, an African American male, because Mr. Jack has been unemployed for several months and has a history of unemployment.
feedback: Refusing to rent to a family with children is discrimination based on familial status.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If the principal instructs the broker-in-charge to violate the law, what should the broker-in-charge do?
option_a: Refuse to obey and give the principal the name of another brokerage that would probably do as instructed.
option_d: Proceed very carefully to avoid prosecution.
feedback: The broker must withdraw to avoid participating in illegal acts.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What law allows borrowers who have been denied credit to view the credit report that was used to make the determination?
feedback: The Fair Credit Reporting Act gives the right to review credit reports used in decisions.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Why may a license be suspended or revoked?
option_a: For making unkind remarks about your competition.
option_b: For working for the Buyer and the Seller.
option_c: For fraudulent misrepresentation in a real estate transaction.
option_d: For refusing to join the Board of Realtors.
feedback: Fraud in a real estate transaction is grounds for loss of license.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If a group of competing real estate brokers-in-charge created a "fee schedule" of commission rates that all the brokerages agreed to follow, what law would they be violating?
feedback: Price-fixing among competitors violates the Sherman Antitrust Act.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which action may the South Carolina Real Estate Commission take?
option_a: Suspend, revoke or cancel any license it issues.
option_b: Imprison a violator of the license laws.
option_c: Both fine and imprison violators of license laws.
option_d: Fine a licensee a maximum of $1000.
feedback: The Commission can discipline by suspending, revoking, or canceling licenses.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Wha may cause a licensee to lose their license?
option_a: Buying listed properties as an undisclosed principal.
option_b: Failing to be employed on a full-time basis.
feedback: Failing to disclose when buying property for oneself is prohibited.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The Fair Housing Act has as its primary purpose the:
option_b: Creation of equal but separate housing.
option_c: Creating of minority employment in the United States.
option_d: Creation and assurance of fair housing opportunities for everyone.
feedback: It ensures equal housing opportunities regardless of protected classes.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: When applying for a VA loan the veteran must have a document from the Veteran's Administration stating that the veteran is eligible for the loan. This is called a/an:
feedback: The Certificate of Eligibility proves the veteran qualifies for a VA loan.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Under the law of contracts when something remains to be done, the contract is said to be a/an ____________contract. When all the conditions of the contract have been met, the contract is said to be a/an ____________contract.
feedback: An executory contract is incomplete; executed means fully performed.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: In what type of contract do both parties promise to perform specified acts?
feedback: A bilateral contract contains mutual promises from both sides.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Broker Betty wants to show a property. Betty finds out that a competing brokerage has an exclusive right to sell listing on the property. How should Betty proceed?
option_b: Ask permission from listing agent to show the property.
option_c: Hold off until the listing expires.
option_d: Advise her customer that state law requires her to be a "Buyer's Agent."
feedback: Permission from the listing agent is required to show the property.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following statements is true concerning a "Quitclaim Deed"?
option_a: It carries no warranties or covenants.
option_b: It warrants that there are no covenants except those recorded.
option_c: It warrants against encumbrances, if any, which are known to the grantor.
option_d: It warrants against encumbrances known by the grantee.
feedback: Quitclaim deeds transfer any interest without warranties.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If the owner of a property solicits a buyer and sells the property to that buyer while you have an exclusive agency listing, how much commission do you receive?
option_b: The commission shown in the listing agreement that you would get if you sold the property.
option_c: The split stated in the cooperating agency agreement.
option_d: On half of the stated commission.
feedback: Exclusive agency allows the owner to sell directly without paying commission.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What is the primary purpose of the South Carolina Real Estate Commission?
option_c: Protect citizens in the real estate business.
option_d: Protect the public through police power.
feedback: The Commissions purpose is to protect the public interest.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: George pays Dan a sum of money which gives George the right to buy a specific parcel from Dan, within a specified period, and for a specific price. Dan remains in possession of the property. What has George acquired?
feedback: An option is the right to buy at set terms within a certain time.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A right, benefit, or improvement, that belongs to and becomes part of the real estate is best described as a/an:
feedback: An appurtenance is a right or improvement attached to and passing with the property.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A lawsuit in which am African American man was denied the right to purchase a home because of his race became a landmark civil rights decision because it reaffirmed the 1866 Civil Rights Act. What is the name of this case?
feedback: Jones v. Mayer confirmed no racial discrimination in property sales.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What is the experience requirement to become a broker in South Carolina?
option_a: Six months with an active Associate license
option_b: One year with an active Associate license
option_c: Five of the past seven years with an Associate license
option_d: Three of the past five years with an Associate license
feedback: SC requires 5 years as a licensed salesperson in the last 7 years.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What is an unlicensed administrative employee of a broker-in-charge allowed to do?
option_a: Sell property if it is done under the broker-in-charges direct supervision.
option_b: Sell or negotiate deals if they are done in the office.
option_c: Refer interested customers to the broker-in-charge or other licensed individuals in the office.
option_d: Show property if price is not discussed.
feedback: Unlicensed staff can refer prospects to a licensee but not sell or negotiate.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Once they have completed the 60-hour Unit I Pre-License course, how long do students have to wait before they can take the 30-hour Unit II Advanced Principles course?
option_b: Until they pass the State licensing exam
feedback: Both courses can be taken back-to-back with no wait required.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Where does the South Carolina Real Estate Commission receive its police power to protect the public?
option_c: The Department of Labor Licensing and Regulation
feedback: The state legislature grants the Commissions authority.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Who would not be required to have a real estate license?
option_b: Party selling neighbors home for a fee
option_d: Attorney selling a client's property in connection with other legal duties
feedback: Attorneys acting within their duties are exempt from licensing.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which is not a duty of the South Carolina Real Estate Commission?
option_b: Promulgating regulations governing the real estate business.
feedback: The Commission does not arbitrate disputes between licensees.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Roberts leaves town and a neighbor, Smiley, sells Roberts' home with Roberts' permission and charges no fee. Which choice is true?
option_b: Smiley is guilty of a misdemeanor.
option_c: Smiley should have charged a reasonable fee
option_d: Roberts can be fined for his actions in this matter.
feedback: Selling without compensation and without license does not violate license law.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A party living in an open, notorious, and hostile manner on a parcel of land for the time prescribed by law may, under some circumstances, gain title through what process?
feedback: Adverse possession allows legal title after statutory occupation period.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: When an supervised licensee leaves a brokerage, the broker-in-charge should take what action?
option_a: Allow the license to expire and not renew since they don't work there anymore.
option_b: Give the license to the supervised licensee.
option_d: Return the license to the Real Estate Commission.
feedback: The broker must return the license to the SC Real Estate Commission.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What is the intended benefit of the truth-in-lending portion of the Truth-in-Lending Integrated Disclosure (TRID) legislation?
option_b: It protects borrowers by allowing them to compare the cost of borrowing from various firms.
option_c: It protects borrowers from paying exorbitant interest rates.
option_d: It protects both borrowers and lenders from frivolous lawsuits.
feedback: It allows borrowers to compare loan terms from different lenders.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: How do appraisers normally obtain the square footage of single-family detached homes?
feedback: Exterior measurements are standard for determining square footage.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A building supply company that delivered shingles to a construction site and did not get paid by the builder may file a ______________.
feedback: A mechanics lien protects suppliers for unpaid materials or labor.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Bo owns a large tract of land, and his home is next to the road. Bo sells the back part of his lot to John. At the time of the sale Bo gave John the right to use a 20-foot strip of land across his property for a driveway. This is an _______________.
feedback: Easement appurtenant benefits the back lot and runs with the land.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What do we call any claim, charge, or liability which attaches to and is binding on the real estate is?
feedback: An encumbrance is any burden or limitation on property rights.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Through a condemnation suit the government may acquire private property and convert it to public use under the power of ________________.
feedback: Eminent domain is the power to take property with compensation.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A permissive use that gives no rights to the user because it can be revoked at any time is known as a/an _______________.
feedback: A license is temporary and can be revoked at will.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: All the following are private control of property except which one?
feedback: Zoning ordinances are public (government) controls.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A property use that was legal in the beginning but is now in violation of the current zoning regulations would be a:
feedback: Non-conforming use is grandfathered from before zoning changed.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The document that shows a short history of the title, including the chain of title, would be the ______________.
feedback: An abstract summarizes the chain of title and recorded documents.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: All the following are requirements for a valid deed except which one?
feedback: Only the grantor must sign; grantees signature not required.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What type of deed creates the greatest liability for the grantor?
feedback: General warranty deed guarantees title against all defects forever.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What type of deed provides warranty by a prior owner only after they acquired title?
feedback: Special warranty limits guarantees to the grantors period of ownership.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Title insurance would normally cover all the following except for:
option_d: Changes in land use brought about by zoning
feedback: Zoning changes are not insurable title defects.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What is the covenant in a general warranty deed whereby the grantor promises to compensate the grantee for any loss suffered because of the condition of the title, other than disclosed encumbrances?
feedback: Warranty forever promises to defend title against claims.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: When a grantor gives a document to transfer a possible interest in the property, which clears up defects in the title, the grantee might receive a ________________.
feedback: Quitclaim deeds release any interest, clearing potential title issues.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: When a person dies intestate and with no heirs found, the state has a reversionary interest called ________________.
feedback: Escheat returns property to the state when no heirs exist.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The procedures used to record real estate title documents are governed by ________________.
option_d: Rules of various federal regulatory agencies
feedback: Recording is regulated at the state level.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A recorded deed must have all but which of the following?
feedback: A deed need not list encumbrances to be valid for recording.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Cities and counties incorporate their long-term land usage goals into what types of documents?
feedback: A master plan guides future growth and land use.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The legal premise underlying the definition of littoral rights is that a lake or sea
option_a: is not a navigable body of water
option_b: should be available to the owner for unrestricted use
option_c: is a navigable body of water
option_d: is owned by the abutting owners
feedback: The legal premise underlying the definition of littoral rights is that a lake or sea is a navigable body of water and, therefore, public property owned by the state

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Three unique physical characteristics of land are immobility, indestructibility and
feedback: Land has three unique physical characteristics: immobility, indestructibility, and heterogeneity.  It cannot be moved, destroyed or identical to any other property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Land cannot be homogenous because
option_b: a parcel of land cannot be moved
feedback: Land is non-homogeneous since no two parcels of land are exactly the same. They are inherently different because each parcel has a unique location

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is an example of a trade fixture?
feedback: Trade fixtures, or chattel fixtures, are items of a tenant's personal property that the tenant has temporarily affixed to a landlord's real property in order to conduct business. Examples of trade fixtures include a grocer's food freezers, a merchant's clothes racks, a tavern owner's bar, a dairy's milking machines, and a printer's printing press

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The conversion of real property to personal property effected by detaching it from the real estate is known as
feedback: Severance is the conversion of real property to personal property by detaching it from the real estate, such as by cutting down a tree, detaching a door from a shed, or removing an antenna from a roof

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following statements regarding the conversion of property is true?
option_a: The classification of an item of property as real or personal is not necessarily fixed
option_b: The classification of property can never be changed by the process of conversion
option_c: The conversion of real property to personal property is not possible
option_d: The conversion of personal property to real property is not possible
feedback: The classification of an item of property as real or personal is not necessarily fixed. The classification may be changed by the process of conversion

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Real estate includes such things as fences, streets, buildings, wells, sewers, sidewalks, and piers. Such man-made structures attached to the land are called
feedback: Real estate includes, in addition to land, such things as fences, streets, buildings, wells, sewers, sidewalks, and piers. Such man-made structures attached to the land are called improvements

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The ownership of real estate and the bundle of rights associated with owning the real estate is defined as
feedback: Real property is ownership of real estate and the bundle of rights associated with owning the real estate

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is an example of tangible property?
feedback: All real estate, by its physical nature, is tangible property. Personal property may be tangible or intangible. Boats, jewelry, coins, appliances, computers, and art work are examples of tangible personal property. Stocks, copyrights, bonds, trademarks, patents, franchises, and listing agreements are examples of intangible personal property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The right to ____________ the property includes the right to mortgage the property as collateral for debt
feedback: The right to encumber the property includes the right to mortgage the property as collateral for debt. There may be restrictions to this right, such as a spouse's right to limit the degree to which a homestead may be mortgaged

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Since water is a resource necessary for survival, some states -- particularly those where water is scarce -- have taken the legal position that the state owns and controls all bodies of water. Which of the following applies to property owners that must obtain permits for the use of water?
feedback: Called the Doctrine of Prior Appropriation, this legality requires that property owners obtain permits for use of water. If a proposed usage is reasonable and beneficial, the state will grant a permit which, over time, can attach to the property of the permit holder

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The primary purpose of a listing agreement is to:
option_a: Give broker permission to sell all principal's properties
option_b: Serve as a contract of employment between owner and broker
option_c: Serve as a guide for a sales contract
feedback: Listing agreements define the broker’s employment to find a buyer for the property.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If a body of water is entirely contained within the boundaries of an owner's property,  the owner would have which of the following rights?
feedback: The legal premise underlying the definition of littoral rights is that a lake or sea is a navigable body of water and, therefore, public property owned by the state. By contrast, a body of water entirely contained within the boundaries of an owner's property is not navigable. In such a case, the owner would own the water as well as unrestricted rights of usage

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The primary criterion for distinguishing real property from personal property is
option_a: whether the item is permanently attached to the land or the structure
option_b: the original cost of the item
option_c: whether the item was included in the previous sale of the property
option_d: whether the item will be included in the listing agreement
feedback: The primary criterion for distinguishing real from personal property is whether the item is permanently attached to the land or structures attached to the land

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The act of converting personal property to real property by attaching it to real estate is known as
feedback: Affixing, or attachment, is the act of converting personal property to real property by attaching it to the real estate, such as by assembling a pile of bricks into a barbecue pit, or constructing a boat dock from wood planks

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is the primary regulatory entity for the real estate brokerage business?
feedback: State governments are the primary regulatory entities of the real estate brokerage business. State governments establish real estate license laws and qualifications. In addition, state governments have established real estate commissions to administer license laws and oversee activities of licensees

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The judicial system exerts an influence on real estate ownership and use through decisions based on
feedback: The judicial system exerts an influence on real estate ownership and use through decisions based on case law and common law, as distinguished from statutory law. Case law consists of decisions based on judicial precedent. Common law is the collective body of law deriving from custom and generally accepted practice in society

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: "Real estate" includes such things as
option_a: fences, sidewalks, and piers on a parcel of land
option_b: mobile homes temporarily parked on a parcel of land
option_c: timber that has been cut and is lying on a parcel of land
option_d: business equipment an owner or tenant has placed on a parcel of land
feedback: The legal concept of real estate encompasses the land and all man-made structures that are "permanently" attached to the land. Real estate therefore includes, in addition to land, such things as fences, streets, buildings, wells, sewers, sidewalks and piers. The phrase "permanently attached" refers primarily to one's intention in attaching the item

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: An "improvement" is
option_a: a modification of land to make it more useful
option_b: a modification of a structure to enhance its value
option_c: a structure intended as a permanent attachment to land
option_d: a parcel of land that has been prepared for development
feedback: Real estate therefore includes, in addition to land, such things as fences, streets, buildings, wells, sewers, sidewalks and piers. Such man-made structures attached to the land are called improvements

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The physical limits of land are
option_a: the surface of the earth and infinite space above the surface
option_b: the center of the earth and infinite space above the earth
option_c: the surface of the earth and all water and minerals on or below the surface to the center of the earth
option_d: the surface of the earth and the air rights above the surface to the point defined by local zoning
feedback: Subsurface rights apply to land beneath the surface of the real estate parcel extending from its surface boundaries downward to the center of the earth

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The "bundle of rights" refers to a set of rights
option_a: enjoyed by the owner of a property
option_b: that is inseparable from a parcel of real estate
option_c: guaranteed to citizens by the Declaration of Independence
option_d: specified in a deed or land contract
feedback: This group of rights includes the right to Possess, Use, Transfer, Encumber, and Exclude others from using the property. (Remember: "PUTEE") Transfer rights include the right to sell, rent, donate, assign, or bequeath. The owner may also encumber the item by mortgaging it as collateral for debt

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The rights to possess and use a property are
option_c: limited by what the law allows
option_d: dependent on the type of property involved
feedback: In owning property, one has the right to possess and use it as the law allows

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The right to encumber a property means that the owner can
option_b: use the property as collateral for debt
option_d: assign the bundle of rights to another
feedback: The right to encumber the property essentially means the right to mortgage the property as collateral for debt. There may be restrictions to this right, such as a spouse's right to limit the degree to which a homestead may be mortgaged

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A property owner has the right to transfer water rights
option_a: if the owner has not previously separated the bundle of rights
option_b: only to the owner of an adjoining property
option_c: only if there are water rights attached to the property
feedback: While all rights are transferrable, the owner can only transfer what the owner in fact possesses. A property seller, for example, cannot sell water rights if there are no water rights attached to the property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A lease is an example of a(n)
option_a: transfer of a portion of the bundle of rights
option_b: encroachment on the bundle of rights
option_c: transfer of the complete bundle of rights
feedback: An ordinary lease is a common example of the transfer of a portion of one's bundle of rights

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is included in surface rights?
feedback: Surface rights apply to the real estate contained within the surface boundaries of the parcel. This includes the ground, all natural things affixed to the ground, and all improvements. Surface rights also include water rights

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The right to build a structure on top of an existing structure is an example of
feedback: Air rights apply to the space above the surface boundaries of the parcel, as delineated by imaginary vertical lines extended to infinity

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Water rights include the right to
option_a: extract water from the water table
option_b: divert surface water onto an adjoining property
option_c: prevent a stream or river from flowing onto an adjoining property
option_d: own and use a body of water on the surface of the earth
feedback: Water rights basically concern the rights to own and use water found in lakes, streams, rivers, and the ocean

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Littoral rights concern which of the following bodies of water?
option_a: Lakes entirely contained within the boundaries of an owner's property
feedback: Littoral rights concern properties abutting bodies of water that are not moving, such as lakes and seas. Owners of properties abutting a navigable, non-moving body of water enjoy the littoral right of use, but do not own the water nor the land beneath the water. Ownership extends to the high-water mark of the body of water

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If littoral rights attach to a property, the property extends to
option_a: the high water mark of the body of water
option_b: the low water mark of the body of water
option_c: the center line of the body of water
option_d: the property line of a property that also has littoral rights on the opposite side of the body of water
feedback: Ownership extends to the high-water mark of the body of water

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is true of littoral rights?
option_a: They revert to the state when the property is sold
option_c: They are a personal right belonging to an individual owner, not attaching to the real property
option_d: They transfer with the property when the property is sold
feedback: Littoral rights attach to the property. When the property is sold, the littoral rights transfer with the property to the new owner

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Riparian rights concern which of the following bodies of water?
feedback: Riparian rights concern properties abutting moving water such as streams and rivers. If a property abuts a stream or river, the owner's riparian rights are determined by whether the water is navigable or not navigable. If the property abuts a non-navigable stream, the owner enjoys unrestricted use of the water and owns the land beneath the stream to the stream's midpoint. If the waterway in question is navigable, the waterway is considered to be a public easement. In such a case, the owner's property extends to the water's edge as opposed to the midpoint of the waterway

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Who owns the land under a navigable river?
option_a: Owners of properties that abut the river
option_d: The municipality, if the area is incorporated
feedback: If the waterway in question is navigable, the waterway is considered to be a public easement. In such a case, the owner's property extends to the water's edge as opposed to the midpoint of the waterway. The state owns the land beneath the water

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If a property has riparian rights, the owner has the right to
option_a: pollute the water for a legitimate business purpose
option_c: require downstream property owners to pay a fee for use of the water
option_d: use the water in a way that does not infringe on another's riparian rights
feedback: One's riparian rights to use flowing water are subject to the conditions that: the usage is reasonable and does not infringe on the riparian rights of other owners downstream  the usage does not pollute the water  the usage does not impede or alter the course of the water flow

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If a property abuts a navigable river, where is the boundary of the property?
option_c: At the midpoint of the river
feedback: If the waterway in question is navigable, the waterway is considered to be a public easement. In such a case, the owner's property extends to the water's edge

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What is a "fixture?"
option_a: Any item of personal property located within the boundaries of a parcel of real estate
option_b: An item of personal property that has been converted to real property
option_c: An item of real property temporarily placed on land for the purpose of conducting a business
option_d: An item of personal property that has been left in one location for a period of one year
feedback: A personal property item that has been converted to real property by attachment to real estate is called a fixture. Typical examples are chandeliers, toilets, water pumps, septic tanks, and window shutters

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Even though it is attached to a structure, an item may be considered personal property rather than real property if
option_a: the owner originally intended to remove it after a period of time
option_b: it can be removed without altering the appearance of the structure
option_c: it is unnecessary to the physical integrity of the structure
option_d: the owner installed it at some time after acquiring the real property
feedback: One's original intention can override the test of movability in determining whether an item is a fixture or not. If someone attached an item to real property, yet intended to remove it after a period of time, the article may be deemed personal property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The level of government that is the primary regulator of the real estate business is
feedback: State governments are the primary regulatory entities of the real estate business

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What guarantees the right of private ownership of real estate in the United States?
feedback: The Constitution establishes absolute right of private ownership of real estate

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is the best definition of real estate?
option_c: Land and everything permanently attached to it
option_d: An ownership interest in land and improvements
feedback: Real estate is the land and all things, man made or natural, that are permanently attached to it

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is included in the legal concept of land?
option_a: The surface of the earth and all natural things permanently attached to the earth
option_b: Only the surface of the earth that is delineated by boundaries
option_c: The surface of the earth except for lakes and streams
option_d: Everything above, on and below the surface of the earth
feedback: Land includes minerals beneath the earth's surface, water on or below the earth's surface, and the air above the surface. In addition, land includes all plants attached to the ground or in the ground, such as trees and grass

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What are the three unique physical characteristics of land?
feedback: Land has three unique physical characteristics: immobility, indestructibility, and heterogeneity

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The primary distinction between the legal concepts of land and real estate is that
option_a: real estate includes air above the surface and minerals below the surface
option_d: land does not include man-made structures
feedback: Real estate includes, in addition to land, such things as fences, streets, buildings, wells, sewers, sidewalks and piers. Such manmade structures attached to the land are called improvements

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The primary distinction between the legal concepts of real estate and real property is that
option_a: real property includes ownership of a bundle of rights
option_c: real property is physical, not abstract
feedback: Real property is ownership of real estate and the bundle of rights associated with owning the real estate

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is included in the bundle of rights inherent in ownership?
feedback: In owning property, the owner has the right to transfer ownership of the item (sell, rent, donate, assign, or bequeath)

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is an example of intangible property?
feedback: A stock certificate, contract, or patent would be an example of intangible property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The right to use real property is limited by
option_a: the right of others to use and enjoy their property
option_d: Title 12 of the U.S. Civil Code
feedback: One's right to use may not infringe on the rights of others to use and enjoy their property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Surface rights, air rights and subsurface rights are
feedback: The bundle of real property rights also applies separately to the individual components of real estate: the air, the surface, and the subsurface. An owner can, for example, transfer subsurface rights without transferring air rights. Similarly, an owner can rent air space without encumbering surface or subsurface rights

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following terms refers to the rights of a property that abuts a stream or river?
feedback: Riparian rights concern properties abutting moving water such as streams and rivers

subject: A2 Real Property
