The surviving owner is neither related to the deceased owner nor his creditor. What would explain how the surviving owner acquired the deceased owner's interest in the office building?
feedback: Upon the death of an owner in a joint tenancy, all remaining interests do not pass to the heirs of the deceased owner or according to the will but to the surviving joint tenant or tenants. Reversionary rights and remainder interests refer to the future right to an estate in real property. Adverse possession occurs when a person acquires title to another's property by means of open, hostile, and continuous possession over time.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Property taxes levied on a property owner to pay for installation of sidewalks or sewers are called:
feedback: Special assessments are taxes levied on real estate to fund public improvements. General property taxes are taxes on real or personal property levied by a government taxing authority. Special excise taxes are direct taxes on services performed or income received, such as license fees and sales taxes. Real estate taxes are also known as ad valorem taxes, taxes based on the value of the specific property being taxed.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A property owner who has the legal right to cross over a neighbor's land holds a(n)
feedback: An easement is a right of use or passage. An easement is not an estate in land, as it does not create any right of ownership. An easement is not an exercise of the police power of the state. An easement is not an encroachment, which is an illegal extension of a structure onto another's property.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A license is an example of a(n)
feedback: A license is a personal and temporary right to use another's property. A license can be canceled or revoked at any time. An encroachment occurs when a building or part of a structure extends illegally onto another's property. An appurtenant easement permits the owner of one parcel to use an adjoining neighbor's parcel. A restriction limits the ways property owners may use their property.

subject: A5 Real Estate Math
question_text: The property owner will use this type of insurance coverage to safeguard against theft, vandalism, burglary, illness and accident, and machinery damage
feedback: Many types of insurance allows the shifting of liability away from the owner. Casualty coverage applies for specific risks, such as theft, vandalism, burglary, illness and accident, and machinery damage

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A property owner deeds a parcel of his property to a town "for recreational purposes only." The deed conveys to the town a
feedback: The deed conveys a fee simple defeasible estate which includes a specific condition on the use of the parcel. If the town uses the parcel for any purpose other than for recreational use, the property owner can take possession of the property through legal action. Holder of a leasehold estate has no ownership only possession. A fee simple absolute estate has no restrictions on the use of the property. A life estate is limited in duration to the life of the owner of the estate or to the life of some other designated person.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A tax lien on a property can hinder the sale of the property because it is a(n):
option_c: general lien and will attach to the real and personal property.
option_d: monetary charge that the purchaser must satisfy.
feedback: An encumbrance is a claim, charge, or liability that attaches to real estate. It is a right or interest held by someone else other than the fee owner of the property that affects the title. A tax lien is a specific lien attached to the real property when real estate property taxes are not paid by the owner. It is not an encroachment, which is an illegal extension of a building or structure onto another's property. A purchaser is not required to satisfy a tax lien unless agreed to by the purchaser in the purchase contract.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: In receiving a gift of a parcel of real estate, one of the two new owners was given an undivided 60% share, and the other received an undivided 40% share. They now hold title as
feedback: Tenants in common hold property with undivided fractional interests, and the shares do not have to be equal. In a joint tenancy, each owner holds equal shares and interests to the property. Community property consists of personal or real property acquired by either party in a marriage and belonging to both parties to the marriage. In a cooperative, owners own shares in a corporation, partnership or trust which owns a property, with each owner holding a proprietary lease and the right to occupy the unit.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Sally conveys a life estate to Scott for his lifetime and designates Bill as the person to whom the property will pass when the life estate ends. When Scott dies, what happens to the property?
option_a: The property reverts back to the original property owner.
option_b: The property passes to Scott's heirs.
option_c: Bill automatically becomes the fee simple owner of the property.
option_d: Sally must designate a remainderman for the property.
feedback: The property owner named Bill as the remainderman, the one to whom the property would pass upon the death of Scott. On Scott's death, the life estate ends, and the property automatically passes to Bill. If the property owner does not choose a remainderman when conveying the life estate to Scott, ownership reverts back to the original property owner or to her heirs.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What is the difference between a general lien and a specific lien?
option_a: A specific lien is a lien against a certain parcel of real estate, while a general lien covers all of a debtor’s property.
option_b: A general lien cannot be enforced in court, while a specific lien can.
option_c: A specific lien is held by only one person, while a general lien must be held by two or more people.
option_d: A general lien is a lien against personal property, while a specific lien is a lien against real estate.
feedback: A general lien is against all the property owned by a debtor. This is different from a specific lien, which is against a specific piece of property. With a specific lien, the specific piece of property alone satisfies the debt- the lien does not attach to other property owned by the debtor, as it does with a general lien.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What is the difference between a general lien and a specific lien?
option_a: A general lien affects all of a debtor's property, while a specific lien affects only a certain piece of property.
option_b: A specific lien covers real estate, while a general lien covers personal property.
option_c: A general lien cannot be enforced in court, while a specific lien can be enforced.
option_d: A specific lien is held by one person, while a general lien is held by at least two people.
feedback: A general lien affects all of a debtor's property, both real and personal. A specific lien affects only a particular piece of real or personal property. Both general and specific liens may be enforced in court.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which lien affects all real and personal property of a debtor?
feedback: A general lien attaches to all of a debtor's real and personal property as opposed to a specific lien, which affects only a particular piece of real or personal property. A voluntary lien, such as a mortgage, is placed on a property with the consent of the owner. An involuntary lien is imposed upon a property without the consent of the owner.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: One major difference between joint tenancy and tenancy in common is:
option_a: joint tenancy requires all ownership rights to be acquired at the same time.
option_b: tenants in common have a separate legal title, which allows them to have divided interests.
option_c: tenants in common have survivor rights for each owner.
option_d: joint tenants have equal rights of possession, while tenants in common do not.
feedback: Joint tenancy (which offers the right of survivorship) requires PITT- possession, interest and title must all occur at the same time. Tenants in common owners have equal rights of possession but may have unequal rights of ownership. All owners, regardless of how ownership is taken, share one deed which conveys legal title.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following best describes fee simple absolute estate?
option_a: It is the maximum estate in land.
option_b: The duration is dependent upon the language of the deed.
option_c: It will have the maximum rights unless a deed condition limits.
option_d: It is the maximum estate in land but lasts only for the duration of the property ownership.
feedback: Fee simple absolute estates, which have the largest (maximum) bundle of rights, are typically transferred from one owner to another when title is passed so they continue forever- there is no time limit to the duration of property ownership. A deed condition would create a fee simple defeasible or qualified estate.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: An ownership interest that is based on occupancy during a specified period of time, is a:
feedback: A real property interest with the right to use the facilities for a certain period of time is called a time-share. While a time-share may be sold as a leasehold or freehold interest, time-share intervals are usually shorter intervals of time, typically weekly intervals. A leasehold interest occurs when a tenant leases a property from an owner but usually for an extended longer period of time (such as one year). A condominium owner holds a fee simple absolute ownership to the unit and owns the common elements as tenants in common with the other owners. In a cooperative, tenants own shares in a corporation, partnership or trust that holds title to the building, with tenants having the right to occupy their own units but do not have a deed.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Two friends bought a store building and took title as joint tenants. One woman died testate. The surviving woman now owns the store:
option_a: as a tenant in common with the dead woman's heirs.
option_c: as a joint tenant with rights of survivorship.
feedback: Joint tenancy includes the right to survivorship, which means the property passes to the surviving owner(s) upon the death of one tenant. In this case, the women took title as joint tenants. When one woman died, her share passed to the other owner who now owns the property in severalty.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following about a condominium is FALSE?
option_a: Each unit owner owns the common elements as tenants in common with all other unit owners
option_b: Each owner has a proprietary lease with the association for their own unit
option_c: Each owner receives a separate real estate tax statement
option_d: Each owner must carry their own property insurance
feedback: In a condominium, each owner has fee simple title to the unit while owners of cooperatives have proprietary leases. In a condominium, each owner owns the common elements as tenants in common with all other unit owners. Each owner receives a separate real estate tax statement. Each owner of a condominium unit must carry their own property casualty insurance on the individual unit based upon the condominium declaration. The association carries insurance for the common elements. NOTE - This question asks "Which of the following statements... is FALSE" which means it's looking for a false answer, and it's false that each owner of a condominium has a proprietary lease.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: When a company furnishes materials for the construction of a house, and the company is NOT paid, it may file a
feedback: A mechanic's lien gives security to persons or companies that perform labor or furnish material to improve real property and can be filed when the owner has not fully paid for the work. Lis pendens is a recorded notice of a suit that may affect title to real property. A default judgment is granted when a creditor files for unpaid bills and wins the right to place a general lien on all the debtor's property to collect payment. A deficiency judgment is a personal judgment a lender may file against a borrower for the unpaid balance on a mortgage loan when a foreclosure sale has not produced enough cash to pay the loan balance.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: An owner conveys a life estate to her grandson and stipulates that on her death the estate will pass to her son-in-law. The son-in-law has a(n)
feedback: The owner's death will end the life estate conveyed to her grandson, which will then mean the holder of the remainder interest, the son-in-law, will have fee simple absolute ownership of the property. An estate for years is a leasehold estate that continues for any definite period of time. A legal life estate is a freehold estate created out of a provision.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Because a homeowner failed to pay her real estate property taxes on time, the taxing authority imposed a claim against her property. This claim is known as a(n)
feedback: The claim is a tax lien. Unpaid real estate property taxes are a lien on the property as they are a charge against the property that provides security for a debt or an obligation for payment by the property owner. A deed restriction creates a limitation on the use of a property. An easement is the right of one party to use the real property of another for a specific use. A reversionary interest is the ownership interest belonging to the creator of a life estate when the ownership reverts to the original owner upon the end of the life estate.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Four friends decide to purchase an investment property as co-owners and take title as joint tenants. All of the following are true EXCEPT:
option_a: all four will enjoy equal rights of possession of the property.
option_b: all four will have equal interests in the property.
option_c: all four will acquire their interests at the same time.
option_d: the four friends will each receive a separate deed for their share.
feedback: All concurrent ownership is created by one deed, which will establish either joint tenancy or tenants in common. Additionally, joint tenancy requires that four unities must be present: possession, interest, title, and time (PITT).

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A mechanic's lien would be properly classified as a(n)
feedback: A mechanic's lien is a specific lien as it affects a specific property and only that particular property. It is an involuntary lien placed on a property without the owner's consent. A general lien is the right of a creditor to have all of an owner's property, real and personal, sold to satisfy a debt. An equitable lien arises out of a written contract that shows the intention of the parties to charge a particular property as a security for a debt or obligation.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following liens usually would be given highest priority in disbursing funds from a foreclosure sale?
option_a: A judgment rendered the day before foreclosure
option_b: A mechanic's lien for work started before the mortgage was made
feedback: Unpaid real estate property taxes have first priority and "move to the head of the line" when property is liquidated at a foreclosure sale. They are a statutory lien with priority over liens created by contract, such as mortgages. Unpaid real estate property taxes take priority over mechanic's liens and judgments, no matter when those liens were recorded.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The owner has a large parcel of land surveyed into lots and streets and files a subdivision plat. Each lot can be legally described by use of which of the following?
feedback: The lot and block system is often used to describe property in subdivisions. The rectangular government survey system divides land into rectangles and describes those rectangles with principal meridians and base lines. The metes-and-bounds system outlines the perimeter of a parcel by starting at one point and ending at the same point. A street address is not a legal property description.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Neighbor A uses Neighbor B's driveway to reach A's garage, which is on their property. B's attorney explains that ownership rights of A's real estate includes an easement appurtenant giving them the right to use B's driveway. In this case, B's property is the
feedback: In an appurtenant easement the parcel over which the easement runs is known as the servient tenement, and the neighboring parcel that benefits from the easement is known as the dominant tenement. B's property is the servient tenement, and A's is the dominant tenement. The easement does not create a leasehold estate. A license is a personal privilege with permission from a landowner to enter the land for a specific purpose.

subject: A5 Real Estate Math
question_text: What type of agent is the property manager?
feedback: Property managers are general agents empowered to perform some or all operational duties, including the authority to enter into contracts

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following terms refers to sole ownership by one person?
feedback: Severalty ownership is ownership by one person severed and cut-off from all others. Tenancy in common and tenancy by the entirety are forms of joint ownership. Community property is personal or real property belonging to spouses according to state laws.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A section in the rectangular survey system contains
feedback: The answer is 640 acres per section. Each township contains 36 sections, and each section is 1 square mile, or 640 acres per section.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A seller lists her property with a local broker, and the listing agreement excludes the seller's prized Iris flowers planted in the garden. The broker lists the exclusion in the MLS and notes it on the property brochure. But when a buyer makes an offer on the property, the plants are not mentioned anywhere in the contract. The seller accepts the offer. At closing, who has possession of the Iris plants?
option_a: The buyer, since they are personal property that transfers with the title
option_b: The seller, since personal property must be included to convey
option_c: The seller, since they were excluded in the listing and MLS
option_d: The buyer, since they are improvements to the land
feedback: Landscaping is considered to be an improvement and, once attached, is an appurtenance which transfers with the land via the deed. In this question, the seller excludes the Iris plants on the listing which implies they are planted, or otherwise appurtenant to the property. Since the seller forgot to exclude the plants in the purchase contract, which is what determines the legal status of appurtenant items, the plants would likely be considered appurtenant and would transfer with the property to the buyer.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A tenant's lease has expired, but the tenant has not vacated the premises or negotiated a renewal lease. The landlord has not given the tenant permission to remain in the building. This situation is an example of:
option_c: an estate from period to period.
feedback: Tenancy (estate) at sufferance occurs when a tenant who lawfully possessed real property continues in possession of the premises without the landlord's consent after the lease rights expire, typically from an estate for years. An estate from period to period automatically renews unless notice is given. A tenancy at will permits a tenant to possess property with the landlord's consent for an unspecified term. An estate for years is a leasehold estate that continues for a definite period, whether that period is days, weeks, months, or years and has a definite expiration date.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The principal difference between an estate for years and an estate from period to period is that an
option_a: estate for years is a life estate.
option_b: estate from period to period has no expiration date.
option_c: estate for years cannot be terminated.
option_d: estate from period to period must be in writing.
feedback: An estate from period to period, or periodic tenancy, does not have a specific expiration date, as it automatically renews until notice is given by the tenant or landlord. An estate (tenancy) for years has specific beginning and ending dates. Neither estate is a life estate. Each can be terminated. Both an estate for years and an estate from period to period must be in writing to be enforceable.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Three friends were concurrent owners of a parcel of real estate. One of the friends died, and his interest passed according to his will to become part of his estate. The deceased friend was a
feedback: In a tenancy in common, the property of a deceased owner may pass to the heirs according to the will. If the deceased owner had been a joint tenant, the property would not have passed according to the will because in a joint tenancy the property interests would have transferred directly to the other two friends (co-tenants). The deceased owner was not a tenant by the entirety, a category reserved for married couples. Nor was the deceased owner a severalty owner because that form of ownership requires property to be held by only one person.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A general contractor recently filed suit against a homeowner for nonpayment. The contractor now learns that the homeowner has listed the property for sale with a real estate broker. In this situation, which of the following will the contractor's attorney use to protect the contractor's interest?
feedback: A recorded lis pendens (a notice of action pending) warns any potential purchaser that a property is subject to a pending legal action. This notice would prompt a buyer to consider very carefully whether to proceed, but it would not absolutely prevent it. The contractor may also have the right to use a mechanic's lien to obtain payment. The lis pendens would give notice until the mechanic's lien was in place. An assessment is a special levy for a definite purpose, such as adding curbs or sewers in a neighborhood. A mortgage lien is a voluntary specific lien the borrower signs.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: To create joint tenancy in the ownership of real estate, there must be unities of
option_d: grantees, ownership, claim of right, and possession
feedback: The equal interests of possession, interest, and title must all occur at the same time for joint tenancy to be created. REMEMBER: PITT for the unities of title in joint tenancy.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is TRUE of condominium ownership?
option_a: The association pays the unit income taxes
option_b: The common elements cannot be sold separately
option_c: The association pays the unit real estate taxes
option_d: The ownership of a unit cannot be willed
feedback: Although an individual unit owner owns an interest in the common elements as a tenant in common, they may not be sold separately by a unit owner; unit owners do not have the same right to partition that other tenants in common have. Individual owners pay the real estate taxes for their units. Condominium owners may will or mortgage their fee simple ownership in a condominium.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A purchaser of real estate learns that his ownership rights could continue forever and that no other person claims to be the owner or has any ownership control over the property. This person owns a
feedback: Fee simple is an absolute ownership, an inheritable interest "with no strings attached." A life estate is a non-inheritable freehold estate that ends with the death of the owner or the death of some other designated person. A remainder interest is tied to a life estate. A qualified fee estate exists as long as a determined use of the property continues.

subject: A5 Real Estate Math
question_text: What type of agent is the property manager?
feedback: Property managers are general agents empowered to perform some or all operational duties, including the authority to enter into contracts

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A tenant who holds possession of a landlord's property without a definite lease term but with the consent of the landlord has a
feedback: An estate (tenancy) at will gives the tenant the right to possess property with the landlord's consent for an unspecified term. It continues until it is terminated by either party giving notice. A tenancy at sufferance arises when a tenant continues to possess the property without the landlord's consent after the tenant's rights expire. Tenancy in common is a form of co-ownership in which each owner holds an undivided interest in a property as if each were the sole owner. A holdover tenancy is created when a tenant with an estate for years holds on to a property after the lease term expires when no new lease has been signed.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A parcel of property was purchased by two friends. The deed they received from the seller at closing conveyed the property by name to each of the two friends without further explanation. The friends took title as which of the following?
feedback: The answer is tenants in common. When joint tenancy is not clearly stated in the deed, grantees take title as tenants in common to avoid the results of accidental joint tenancy. Friends are unable to hold community property or be tenants by the entirety as those forms of ownership are available only for married couples.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: When defining ownership estates, the term estate refers to:
option_a: a person's legal rights in the land or property.
option_c: only the possessionary rights of tenants and owners.
option_d: the quantity of land as shown on a plat map for a property.
feedback: With respect to property ownership, the word estate refers to a person's legal use or the bundle of rights in land which, depending upon the type of estate, include the right to possess, transfer, encumber, et cetera. Estates are not tied to the physical quantity or the value of the land itself. A fee simple estate is the highest estate one can hold in land since it has the most or maximum rights to the land.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The type of real estate ownership that is MOST all-inclusive is a
feedback: A fee simple absolute estate is the highest interest in real estate recognized by law. Fee simple ownership is absolute ownership in which the holder is entitled to all rights to the property. A life estate is a freehold estate that lasts as long as the life of the life tenant. A qualified fee estate is limited by specific conditions created by agreement or by law. A reversionary interest is that interest in property that exists when ownership of a life estate reverts back to the original owner at the end of the life estate.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Under which type of lien are both the real property and the personal property of the debtor sold to pay the debt?
feedback: A judgment is a general, involuntary, equitable lien on both real and personal property owned by the debtor. A mechanic's lien is a specific lien against real property filed by a contractor, subcontractor, or supplier when an owner or contractor has not paid for work or supplies for improvements on a property. A real estate tax lien is a statutory lien against real property for nonpayment of taxes. An assessment lien is a special assessment levied on real property to fund public improvements to property.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A married couple owns a farm together, with the right of survivorship. Their ownership is MOST likely
feedback: A married couple, in certain states, may use a special form of co-ownership called tenancy by the entirety. Each spouse has an equal, undivided interest in the property, with the right of survivorship. Severalty ownership occurs when real estate is owned by one person only. Tenancy in common and community property forms of ownership do not include the right of survivorship (in most states).

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: An owner purchased an interest in a house in a resort beach community. The owner is entitled to the right of possession only between July 10 and August 4 of each year. Which type of ownership MOST likely has been purchased?
feedback: The owner holds an interest in property for a stated time each year, sharing the property according to a specified time plan. The owner's interest is a time-share. The owner of a condominium or a cooperative has a right of possession for as long as the ownership is not transferred. An individual does not own property through a trust, as a trust holds title to the real estate.

subject: A4 Appraisal and Valuation
question_text: If demand is increasing, what would be the likely effect on real estate prices in an area where the municipality has declared a moratorium on new construction?
option_b: Prices would continue to follow the trend that preceded the moratorium
feedback: A good example of government influence over the local real estate market is a city government's power to declare a moratorium to stop new construction, regardless of demand. Because demand is increasing but availability is limited, the prices would rise

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A person who has complete control of a parcel of real estate is said to own a
feedback: The highest interest in real estate recognized by law is the fee simple or fee simple absolute estate in which the holder is entitled to all rights of the property. In a fee simple estate, the property may pass to the owner's heirs upon the death of its owner. A tenant's right to possess a property for the term of a lease is called a leasehold estate. A life estate is an interest in real property that ends when the owner dies. A defeasible fee estate is a fee simple estate that can exist as long as a certain use continues or provided that the property is used for a specific purpose.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A property owner conveys a life estate to her uncle and names her son as remainderman. On the death of the uncle, the son will have what type of estate?
feedback: The son holds a remainder interest in the property, and will have a fee simple absolute estate upon the death of the uncle, the life tenant.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The difference between a freehold and leasehold estate is
option_b: the right of survivorship for the tenant.
option_c: ownership versus the right to purchase.
option_d: who is obligated to pay the property expenses.
feedback: Freehold estates are estates of ownership while in a leasehold estate the property owner gives the tenant possession of the property. Tenants may agree to pay all the property expenses or share them with the owner or the owner can pay them all. The right to purchase is not a requirement for leasehold and there is no right of survivorship.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following would be classified as a general lien?
feedback: A judgment lien is not a lien just on specific assets but on the assets of the debtor in general. A judgment applies to both the personal and real property of the debtor. Property tax liens are specific liens that have priority over other liens. A mechanic's lien may be filed against real property by a contractor or supplier for nonpayment of work or supplies. A property tax lien may be filed by a government for the nonpayment of real estate property taxes.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A property owner contracted to have a swimming pool installed... This lien was MOST likely a:
feedback: A mechanic's lien may be filed by a contractor when an owner refuses to pay... It is a specific, involuntary lien.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: When one property is owned in severalty, the:
option_a: property may be owned by more than one person.
option_b: property owner may sell, will, or lease...
option_d: property may be co-owned by spouses.
feedback: Property owned in severalty is owned by one person... Owner may be an individual or corporation.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A landowner divided much of her land... What type of easement will be granted?
feedback: Easement by necessity arises when there is no access without crossing anothers land.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Legal descriptions are used in purchase contracts... EXCEPT:
option_b: property address with full zip code
feedback: Addresses can change and are not legal descriptions. Lots, metes/bounds, and govt survey are valid.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Property owned by three cousins as tenants in common. One dies intestate (with heirs). Interest passes to:
option_b: The other two cousins in joint tenancy
feedback: Interest passes to deceased owner's heirs under intestate succession. Not to other co-owners.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The priority of liens refers to:
option_b: Order liens are paid if property sold
feedback: Priority of liens = order in which claims are paid. General rule: first to record, first in right.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is a lien on real estate?
feedback: A recorded mortgage is a voluntary, specific lien. Easements/covenants/encroachments are encumbrances.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Fixtures EXCEPT:
feedback: Fixtures determined by attachment, adaptation, agreement. A plug-in microwave is personal property.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Normally, priority of general liens is determined by:
feedback: Priority rule = first recorded, first in right. Property taxes/special assessments always come first.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Personal property includes EXCEPT:
feedback: Built-in bookcase = real property. Chairs, emblements, trade fixtures = personal property.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Wooded area with trails between cotton mills and housing =
feedback: Buffer zones separate residential from nonresidential zones.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: 7-story building, zoning later changed to max 6 stories. TRUE?
feedback: Existing building becomes legal nonconforming use.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Purpose of building code is to:
feedback: Building codes ensure structural safety.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Sign restriction: max 3ft, owner wants 9ft revolving sign. To do legally:
feedback: Variance allows prohibited use.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Govt powers EXCEPT:
feedback: Private subdivision controls = deed restrictions, not government powers.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: To determine if location can be retail store, examine:
feedback: Zoning ordinances regulate permitted property uses.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Developer must consider what when planning subdivision?
feedback: Master plan governs land use, utilities, housing, etc.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Eminent domain requires:
feedback: Government must take for public use/purpose.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Lien with highest foreclosure priority?
feedback: Property taxes/special assessments always first.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which tax targets homeowners in particular?
feedback: Real property tax is specific to homeowners.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Grocery store in rezoned area (now residential) is:
feedback: Store existed prior, so its legal nonconforming use.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Municipality establishes development goals in:
feedback: Master plan = comprehensive planning.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which is a property improvement?
feedback: Improvements = physical structures like coop, fence, well.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: "Bundle of rights" refers to rights:
feedback: Bundle of rights = possess, use, transfer, encumber, exclude.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: System of land ownership in U.S. =
feedback: Allodial = absolute ownership by individuals.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Rights of owner along riverbank are:
feedback: Riparian rights = rivers/streams. Littoral = lakes/oceans.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Deed says "subject to an easement." Burdened land is:
feedback: Subject to = encumbered (burdened).

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A non-possessory interest in real property is also called:
feedback: A claim against, limitation on or liability against real estate is called an encumbrance. Encumbrances include liens, deed restrictions, easements, encroachments, and licenses. An encumbrance gives some other entity a non-possessory interest in a property and can restrict the owner's ability to transfer title to the property or lessen its value.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A condominium community offers a swimming pool, tennis courts, and a biking trail. These facilities are MOST likely owned by the:
option_a: unit owners, in the form of proportional divided interests.
option_b: unit owners, in the form of percentage undivided interests.
option_c: corporation in which the unit owners hold stock.
feedback: A condominium is a living space independently sellable and regarded as real estate. Common elements like recreational facilities are owned by all the unit owners as a percentage of undivided interests.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A construction lien is best described as:
feedback: A construction lien, also known as a mechanics lien, is a claim made against a property by a contractor or other professional who has supplied labor or materials. They are considered a specific lien.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The ownership in a condominium is an estate in real property consisting of an undivided interest in common and a separate ownership of space. The estate in the defined space is:
option_a: A fee simple estate with a proprietary lease.
feedback: A fee simple (absolute) is the highest possible ownership interest in real property, and it applies to individual condo units.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The maximum estate that a person may hold in land may be referred to as a(n):
feedback: Fee simple absolute is the highest form of land ownership possible.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following individuals holds a license and is therefore not entitled to a claim of adverse possession?
option_a: a person who has been entering an orchard with permission, and taking apples every year for 30 years.
option_b: a person who has been in possession of the property for 19 years.
option_c: a person who has been parking his car on a neighbor's property for 20 years without permission.
option_d: a person who held property for five years after inheriting it from a parent, who was in adverse possession for ten years.
feedback: A license is a personal right that may be revoked at any time. Entering the orchard to pick apples, with permission, is a license.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: When a company furnishes materials for construction and is not paid, it may file a(n):
feedback: A mechanic's lien is used by contractors/suppliers to recover unpaid labor or materials.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: An unmarried couple owns real estate. Man owns 1/3, woman owns 2/3. The form of ownership is:
feedback: A tenancy in common is a shared tenancy where each holder has a distinct, separately transferable interest.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: An ownership interest that permits multiple purchasers to buy interests in the same real estate is a:
feedback: A timeshare divides ownership or use rights into time periods.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Zoning ordinances may regulate:
feedback: Zoning ordinances regulate use, lot size, placement, height, and density.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A person with complete control over a parcel of real estate owns a:
feedback: Fee simple provides the most control over real estate, while other estates have conditions or time limits.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Neighbors use your driveway via an easement appurtenant. Your property is the:
feedback: The servient tenement is the property burdened by the easement.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: An easement appurtenant is best described as:
option_a: right to use anothers property at any time for whatever reason.
option_b: may be terminated at any time by the holder.
option_d: belongs to the land and follows title.
feedback: An appurtenant easement runs with the land and applies regardless of ownership changes.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: An owner with a permanent right to cross over adjoining land has:
feedback: An easement gives a property owner rights over anothers property for a purpose.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: An easement is:
option_b: an encumbrance, but not a lien.
option_c: both a lien and an encumbrance.
feedback: Easements are encumbrances, but not liens.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Permission to use property by giving an easement creates:
feedback: Easements are encumbrances that affect property rights.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Condemnation of private property for public use is under:
feedback: Eminent domain allows governments to take private property for public good with compensation.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Oscar, Andrew & Douglas are joint tenants. Douglas conveys to Joe. Afterward, Oscar & Andrew:
option_c: continue as joint tenants with Douglas.
feedback: Douglass conveyance breaks joint tenancy for his share. Joe becomes tenant in common. Oscar & Andrew remain joint tenants of 2/3.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Personal property includes all EXCEPT:
feedback: Fixtures are attached and considered real property, not personal property.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The process under eminent domain by which property is taken is:
feedback: Condemnation is the process governments use to exercise eminent domain.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If dominant & servient tenements merge under one owner, what happens?
feedback: You cannot hold an easement over your own property; the easement ends.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The state wants farmland for a highway. Does it have the right?
feedback: Eminent domain is the right; condemnation is the process.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The right of eminent domain refers to:
option_a: The government's right to acquire or authorize others to acquire title to property for public use
option_b: An institution or individual acquiring land by grant from the government
option_c: An organization's right to condemn property pending an improvement that is for the good of the.community
option_d: The right of every American citizen to own property
feedback: Eminent domain is the power of a state, provincial, or national government to take private property for public use.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The right of eminent domain refers to:
option_a: orgs right to condemn property for community
option_b: governments right to acquire property for public use
option_d: every citizens right to own property
feedback: Eminent domain is the governments right to acquire property for public use.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Stephen leased for six months and stayed without consent after. He now holds an:
feedback: Estate at sufferance = holding over after lease expiration without landlord consent.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Susan lets Oscar store a camper in her yard temporarily without rent. This is a:
feedback: Permission for temporary use is a license.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If a condo owner defaults on a mortgage, the remaining owners:
feedback: Default affects only the unit owner, not others.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The sudden removal of land when a stream changes its channel is:
feedback: Avulsion = sudden change; accretion = gradual.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of these is TRUE concerning a property held in joint tenancy?
option_a: A maximum of two people can own the real estate
option_b: There is always the right of survivorship
option_c: The fractional interests of the owners can be different
option_d: Additional owners may be added later
feedback: Joint Tenancy is a type of concurrent estate in which co-owners have a right of survivorship, meaning that if one owner dies, that owner's interest in the property will pass to the surviving owner or owners by operation of law.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Under which of the following types of liens can both the real property and the personal property of the debtor be sold to pay the debt?
feedback: Most liens are against a specific property, such as a primary residence. Thus, a contractor seeking payment for a new deck cannot have a homeowner's car attached in settlement. A judgment lien, however, is a decision directed by the courts and can apply to whatever assets it deems appropriate.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Under which of the following types of liens can both the real property and the personal property of the debtor be sold to pay the debt?
feedback: Most liens are against a specific property. A judgment lien, however, is a decision directed by the courts and can apply to whatever assets of the defendant's that it deems appropriate.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Under the terms of a net lease, a commercial tenant usually would be responsible for paying:
feedback: A net lease requires the tenant to pay taxes, insurance, and maintenance costs in addition to rent. Common in commercial real estate.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A partition suit is used for which of the following?
option_a: to force a division of property without all the owners' consent
option_c: to allow construction of party walls
option_d: to change a tenancy by entireties to some other form of ownership
feedback: Partition suits are typically pursued when a co-owner of a property wants to sell their share and the other owners are opposed. Since it is a legal action involving the courts, it is an expense with often unsatisfactory results.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A partition suit is used for which of the following?
option_a: To allow construction of party walls
option_b: To force a division of property without all the owners consents
option_d: To change a tenancy by entireties to some other form of ownership
feedback: Partition suits are pursued when a co-owner wants to sell their share and others are opposed. Courts may order division or sale.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Not a necessary element for forming a contract:
feedback: Performance occurs after formation; elements needed: offer, acceptance, consideration, legal purpose, competent parties.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Real property includes which of the following?
option_a: Appurtenances, rights that belong to the land, and all personal property.
option_b: Appurtenances, rights that belong to the land, and harvested crops.
option_c: Appurtenances, rights belonging to the land, and trade fixtures.
option_d: Appurtenances, rights that belong to the land, and attached personal property.
feedback: Real estate consists of land, buildings, natural resources, and appurtenances. Attached personal property (fixtures) becomes real property.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Stephen leases store space to Rose for a restaurant, and Rose installs ovens, booths, counters, and equipment. When do these items become real property?
option_b: When the lease expires, if the items are not taken by the tenant
option_c: When Rose defaults on rental payments
feedback: Trade fixtures remain tenants property but if left behind at lease end, they become part of the real property.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The form of tenancy that expires on a specific date is a:
feedback: A tenancy for years is for a fixed period, with automatic termination on the end date.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If four persons hold title as tenants in common, they:
option_a: would be unable to determine which portion of the land is theirs.
option_b: would have the right of survivorship.
option_c: must each own a one quarter interest.
option_d: could not transfer their interest without consent of the others.
feedback: Tenants in common share undivided interest in the entire property. They cannot claim specific physical portions.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is/are considered personal property?
feedback: Fixtures (furnace, tub, fireplace) stay with property. Patio furniture is movable, so its personal property.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: After a mechanic has filed a lien, he may enforce it by:
option_a: turning the debt over to collections.
option_d: filing a lawsuit within a specified time frame.
feedback: Mechanics liens must be enforced by lawsuit within state deadlines.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A portion of Joe's building was inadvertently built on Ginny's land. This is called an:
feedback: Encroachment = unauthorized intrusion of a structure onto anothers land.

subject: B4 Disclosure
question_text: The landlord of an apartment building neglected to repair the building’s plumbing system. As a result, the apartments did not receive water, as provided by the leases. If a tenant’s unit becomes uninhabitable, which of the following would MOST likely result?
feedback: Constructive eviction is a term used in the law of real property to describe a circumstance in which a landlord either does something or fails to do something that he or she has a legal duty to provide (e.g. the landlord refuses to provide heat or water to the apartment), rendering the property uninhabitable.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The word "improvement" refers to all of the following EXCEPT:
feedback: Trade fixtures are tenants removable items, not permanent improvements.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Of the following, which could be a lease on real property?
feedback: Estate for years = leasehold for a fixed term.

subject: B4 Disclosure
question_text: A licensee showed an owner-occupied property that had window screens, custom venetian blinds, and a wall bed to a buyer. The owner accepted the buyer’s offer with no exclusions. What may the seller remove before closing?
option_a: Only the wall bed, because it is real property
option_b: Only the venetian blinds, as personal property
option_d: All of the identified items, because they are trade fixtures
feedback: If these attached items were not addressed as "exclusions", moving the items would be in contradiction to the purchase agreement.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: To create a joint tenancy, there must be unities of:
option_b: grantees, ownership, claim of right, possession.
feedback: Joint tenancy requires the four unities: possession, time, interest, and title.

subject: B4 Disclosure
question_text: A broker is listing her neighbors’ home. The commission should be determined by which of the following?
option_a: Rates approved by the local Board of Realtors
option_c: The size of the broker’s firm
option_d: Rates approved by the Real Estate Commissioner
feedback: Transaction commissions on the sale of a home should be determined by negotiation between the seller and the listing broker.

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Condo owner and apartment lessee differ, but legally both hold:
feedback: Both owners and lessees hold estates in real property (freehold vs. leasehold).

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Vivian erects a fence. Materials are converted to real estate by method of:
feedback: Fixtures are created through attachment (MARIA test: Method, Adaptability, Relationship, Intention, Agreement).

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following best describes the concept of "legal title" to real estate?"
option_a: Ownership of the bundle of rights to real estate
option_b: The right of a buyer or lender to obtain ownership under certain circumstances
option_d: Absolute proof of ownership of real estate
feedback: Owning title to real property commonly connotes owning the complete bundle of rights that attach to the property, including the right to possession. More accurately, someone who possesses all ownership interests owns legal title to the property. Legal title is distinct from equitable title, which is the interest or right to obtain legal title to a property in accordance with a sale or mortgage contract between the legal owner and a buyer or creditor. During the contractual period of time when ownership of legal title is contingent upon the contract, the buyer or lender owns equitable title to the property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A person claims ownership of a parcel of real estate to a prospective buyer, stating that she has lived on the property for five years and nobody has ever bothered her. The claimant also shows the buyer a copy of the deed. The legal basis of this claim is referred to as
feedback: In proving real estate ownership, a person provides actual notice by producing direct evidence, such as by showing a valid will

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Constructive notice of ownership of a parcel of real estate is primarily demonstrated through
option_a: direct inspection to see who is in possession
feedback: The foremost method of imparting constructive notice is by recordation of ownership documents in public records, specifically, title records

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: An owner transfers title to a property to a buyer in exchange for a consideration. This is an example of
feedback: Voluntary alienation is an unforced transfer of title by sale or gift from an owner to another party

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: For a deed to convey title, it is necessary for the deed to be
feedback: Execution of a valid deed in itself does not convey title. It is necessary for the deed to be delivered to and accepted by the grantee for title to pass

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The only required clause in a deed of conveyance is one that
option_a: states restrictions and limitations to the estate being conveyed
option_b: states the parties and the type of estate being conveyed
option_c: states that the grantor has done nothing to impair title to the property being conveyed
option_d: states the grantor's intention, names the parties, describes the property, and indicates a consideration
feedback: Granting clause, or premises clause - the only required clause; contains the conveyance intentions; names the parties; describes the property; indicates nominal consideration

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The purpose of a covenant clause in a deed of conveyance is to
option_a: state the grantor's assurance or warrant to the grantee that a certain condition or fact concerning the property is true
option_b: state the grantee's promise to use the property in a prescribed manner
option_c: warrant that the grantor has never encumbered title
option_d: describe the consideration that the grantee promises to give in return for title
feedback: Covenant clauses present the grantor's assurances to the grantee

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The type of statutory deed that contains the most complete protection for the grantee is a
feedback: The general warranty deed, or warranty deed for short, is the most commonly used deed. It contains the fullest possible assurances of good title and protection for the grantee

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A person wishes to convey any and all interests in a property to another without making any assurances as to encumbrances, liens, or any other title defects on the property. This party would most likely use which of the following types of deed?
feedback: A quitclaim deed. A quitclaim deed transfers real and potential interests in a property, whether an interest is known to exist or not.  The grantor makes no claim to any interest in the property being conveyed and offers no warrants to protect the grantee

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A one-time tax levied on a property for purposes of recording a transaction is called
feedback: State law usually requires payment of a documentary stamp tax on a conveyance of real property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If a person dies with no legal heirs or relations and has left no valid will, what happens to real property owned by that person?
option_a: It is taken by the state according to the process called escheat
option_b: It is reconveyed to the previous owner in the chain of title
option_c: It is taken by the title insurance company according to the process called involuntary alienation
option_d: It is conveyed to the highest bidder at a public auction
feedback: It is taken by the state according to the process called escheat, or reverts, to the state or county after all claims and debts have been validated and settled

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Just prior to passing away, a person tells two witnesses that she would like her estate to pass to her husband. One witness records the statement and signs his name. This is an example of
feedback: An unenforceable nuncupative will, made orally, and written down by a witness; generally not valid for the transfer of real property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If a person having several heirs dies intestate, the property will
option_a: pass to heirs by the laws of descent and distribution
option_c: pass to the surviving spouse through elective share
option_d: pass to the surviving heirs according to the provisions of the will
feedback: If the decedent died without a valid will, the estate passes to lawful heirs according to the state's laws of descent and distribution, or succession

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A property owner can avert the danger of losing title by adverse possession by
option_a: recording proof of ownership in county title records
option_b: inspecting the property and evicting any trespassers found
option_d: filing a claim of right with the county recorder
feedback: An owner can avert the danger of involuntary alienation by adverse possession by periodically inspecting the property within statutory deadlines and evicting any trespassers found. The owner may also sue to quiet title, which would eliminate the threat of the adverse possessor's claim to legal title

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A hermit secretly lives in a cave on a 200-acre property. After twenty years, the person makes a claim of ownership to the property. This claim will likely be
option_b: upheld because of the length of possession
option_c: declined through the doctrine of prior appropriation
feedback: State laws may allow a real property owner to lose legal title to an adverse possessor. An adverse possessor is someone who enters, occupies, and uses another's property without the knowledge or consent of the owner, or with the knowledge of an owner who fails to take any action over a statutory period of time. To claim legal title, the adverse possessor must have notorious possession, which is possession without concealment

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The fundamental purpose of recording instruments that affect real property is to
option_c: give constructive notice of one's rights and interests in the property
option_d: assemble all relevant documents in a single place
feedback: State laws require the recording of all documents that affect rights and interests in real estate in the public real estate records of the county where the property is located. These public records, or title records, contain a history of every parcel of real estate in the county, including names of previous owners, liens, easements, and other encumbrances that have been recorded. Title records protect the public by giving all concerned parties constructive notice of the condition of a property's legal title: who owns the property, who maintains claims and encumbrances against the property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What is "chain of title?"
option_a: The list of all parties who have ever owned real estate
option_b: The bundle of rights linked to the recorded title to a parcel
option_c: A chronology of successive owners of record of a parcel of real estate
option_d: Involuntary conveyance of title by statutory rules of descent
feedback: Chain of title refers to the succession of owners of record dating back to the original grant of title from the state to a private party. It is thus more than a mere list; it is a chronological list as reflected in title records

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The Torrens System differs from other title recording systems in that
option_a: title is conveyed only when conveyance is registered on the title certificate
option_b: the Torrens System requires prior approval of a transaction before it can be registered
option_c: the Torrens System is not recognized by title insurance companies
option_d: it allows unrecorded encumbrances to cloud title
feedback: The Torrens system differs from other title recording systems in that title passes only when the conveyance has been duly registered on the title certificate itself

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: To be marketable, title must be
option_b: free of undisclosed defects and encumbrances
feedback: Marketable title is, by definition, one that is free and clear of undesirable and unknown encumbrances, claims, clouds, or other defects. Attorney's abstracts and title certificates do not guarantee that a title is marketable

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: With the exception of a Torrens certificate, the best evidence of marketable title is
feedback: A title insurance policy is commonly accepted as the best evidence of marketable title. A title insurance policy indemnifies the policy holder against losses arising from defects in the insured title

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A property owner who possesses all ownership interest owns
feedback: Owning title to real property commonly connotes owning the complete bundle of rights that attach to the property, including the right to possession.  In turn, someone who possesses all ownership interests owns legal title to the property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A mortgage loan transaction gives the lender the right to execute a strict foreclosure, which transfers legal title to the lender in the event of a default. With this contractual right, which type of title does the lender hold?
feedback: Equitable title is the interest or right to obtain legal title to a property in accordance with a sale or mortgage contract between the legal owner and a buyer or creditor. During the contractual period of time, when ownership of legal title is contingent upon the contract, the buyer or lender owns equitable title to the property.  With this contractual right, the lender has equitable title to the property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The term "notice" is synonymous with
feedback: A person who has received actual notice has actual knowledge of something. Receiving actual notice means learning of something through direct experience or communication

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: When the transfer of title to real estate uses a written instrument, the transfer is called
feedback: The use of a written instrument to transfer title to real estate is called a conveyance

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Involuntary alienation occurs primarily by the process of
feedback: Involuntary alienation is a transfer of title to real property without the owner's consent. Involuntary alienation occurs primarily by the processes of descent and distribution, escheat, foreclosure, eminent domain, adverse possession, and estoppel

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A deed is a legal instrument used by an owner.  The owner in a conveyance is also known as the
feedback: A deed is a legal instrument used by an owner, the grantor, to transfer title to real estate voluntarily to another party, the grantee

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following statements applies if a grantee loses the deed?
option_a: The grantor can reclaim the property
option_b: There is no effect on the grantees title to the estate
option_c: Whoever finds the deed will automatically own the property
option_d: The grantee will lose the title to the estate by escheat
feedback: If the grantee loses the deed, there is no effect on the grantee's title to the estate. The grantor, for example, cannot reclaim the estate on the grounds that the grantee has lost the deed after it was delivered and accepted

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The habendum clause
option_a: recites restrictions and limitations to the estate being conveyed
option_b: assures the grantor owns the estate to be conveyed
option_d: describes the type of estate being conveyed
feedback: The habendum clause describes the type of estate being conveyed (fee simple, life, etc.)

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following statements is true about quitclaim deeds?
option_a: The quitclaim deed is typically used to clear title rather than convey it
option_b: The quitclaim deed covenant claims that the title will be defended in court
option_c: The quitclaim deed is the same as a special warranty deed
option_d: The quitclaim deed contains the warrant of seisin
feedback: The quitclaim is typically used to clear title rather than convey it. Where there is a possibility that prior errors in deeds or other recorded documents might cloud (encumber) the title, the relevant parties execute a quitclaim deed to convey "any and all" interest to the grantee

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A valid last will and testament for the transfer of real property will generally take which of the following forms?
feedback: A will can take on one of the following forms:  witnessed, holographic (in the testators handwriting, dated and signed), approved or nuncupative

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: To claim legal title, the adverse possessor must have
feedback: To claim legal title, the adverse possessor must have notorious possession, which is possession without concealment

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following prevents a person from claiming a right or interest that is inconsistent with the person's previous statements or acts?
feedback: Estoppel prevents a person from claiming a right or interest that is inconsistent with the person's previous statements or acts. As a basis for involuntary alienation, the doctrine of estoppel can prevent an owner from re-claiming a property that was transferred under false pretenses

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: To demonstrate marketable title to a buyer, a seller must show that the
option_c: property is not located on a floodplain
feedback: To demonstrate marketable title to a buyer, a seller must show that the title is free of defects, such as a judgment lien

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is a summary of the condition of title as of the date of the certificate, based on a search of public records by a title analyst?
feedback: A title certificate is a summary of the condition of title as of the date of the certificate, based on a search of public records by an abstractor or title analyst. The certificate does not guarantee clear title against defects, unrecorded encumbrances or encroachments

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which two terms are often used interchangeably?
option_c: Contract for deed and abstract title
feedback: In practice, the terms "title" and "legal title" are often used interchangeably

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: A living owner makes a private grant utilizing which of the following?
feedback: A living owner makes a private grant by means of a deed of conveyance, or deed. A private grant that occurs when the owner dies is a transfer by will

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Unless the deed contains special provisions, the deed must be signed by the
feedback: The deed must be signed by the grantor, but need not be signed by the grantee unless the deed contains special provisions requiring the grantee's acceptance. Grantors may give power of attorney to other parties, authorizing them to execute deeds on their behalf

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following is the only required conveyance clause?
feedback: Conveyance clauses and covenant, or warrant, clauses set forth all the necessary provisions of the conveyance. Granting clauses, or premises clause are the only required clause. The granting clause contains the conveyance intentions; names the parties; describes the property; indicates nominal consideration

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The covenant in a deed that assures that the grantee will not be disturbed by third party title disputes is known as the
feedback: The warrant of quiet enjoyment assures that the grantee will not be disturbed by third party title disputes. another party, the grantee

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The covenant in a deed that assures that the grantor owns the estate to be conveyed, and has the right to do so is known as the
feedback: The warrant of seisin assures that the grantor owns the estate to be conveyed, and has the right to do so

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The overall general warranty covenant is
option_b: "I own and will defend against my acts only."
option_c: "I may or may not own, and I won't defend."
feedback: The general warranty deed, or warranty deed for short, is the most commonly used deed. It contains the fullest possible assurances of good title and protection for the grantee. The overall general warranty covenant is: "I own and will defend."

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The overall special warranty covenant is
option_b: "I own and will defend against my acts only."
option_c: "I may or may not own, and I won't defend."
feedback: In a special warranty deed, the grantor warrants only against title defects or encumbrances not noted on the deed that may have occurred during the grantor's period of ownership or trusteeship. The overall special warranty covenant is: "I own and will defend against my acts only."

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: If a party responsible for encumbering title refuses to quitclaim the interest, the owner may file which of the following?
feedback: If a party responsible for encumbering title refuses to quitclaim the interest, the owner may file a quiet title suit. This requires the lienor to prove the validity of an interest. If the defendant is unable to do so, the court removes the cloud by decree

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: An owner can avert the danger of involuntary alienation by taking which of the following action?
option_c: Registering the property with the recorder of deeds
feedback: An owner can avert the danger of involuntary alienation by adverse possession by periodically inspecting the property within statutory deadlines and evicting any trespassers found. The owner may also sue to quiet title, which would eliminate the threat of the adverse possessor's claim to legal title

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Notorious possession and hostile possession give _______________ to the public
feedback: Notorious possession and hostile possession give constructive notice to the public, including the legal owner, that a party other than the legal owner is occupying and claiming to own the property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: To claim the legal title, the adverse possessor must
option_a: be able to show a color of title as a reason for the possession
option_b: prove ownership with a quit claim deed
option_c: have a written signed lease agreement allowing for the use of the property
option_d: register the property in the Torrens system
feedback: A claim of right is based on the adverse possessor's occupying and maintaining the property as if he or she were the legal owner. Color of title results when a grantee has obtained defective title, or received title by defective means, but occupies the property as if he or she were the legal owner. A court may hold that a claim of right or a claim of colored title is a valid reason for the possession

subject: A5 Real Estate Math
question_text: The lease used when the tenant agrees to pay a portion or all of the operating expenses is known as a
feedback: Under a net lease, the tenant pays rent plus some or all of the operating expenses attributable to the rented space. This arrangement is commonly used in office and industrial leases

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following contains the history of every parcel of real estate in the county, including names of previous owners, liens, easements, and other encumbrances?
feedback: The public records, or title records, contain a history of every parcel of real estate in the county, including names of previous owners, liens, easements, and other encumbrances that have been recorded

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: All of the following are evidence of title EXCEPT
option_c: an attorneys opinion of the abstract
feedback: Estoppel prevents a person from claiming a right or interest that is inconsistent with the person's previous statements or acts. It is not evidence of title

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Someone who possesses all ownership interests in real property owns
option_a: equitable title to the real property
option_b: legal title to the real property
option_c: equitable and legal title to the real property
option_d: statutory title to the real property
feedback: Someone who possesses all ownership interests owns legal title to the property. Legal title is distinct from equitable title, which is the interest or right to obtain legal title to a property in accordance with a sale or mortgage contract between the legal owner and a buyer or creditor

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The question of who owns title to a property is generally a function of
option_a: who has a bill of sale for the property
option_b: who has physical possession of the property
option_c: who has the best evidence of ownership
option_d: who has a deed for the property
feedback: Ownership of legal title is a function of evidence. A court will generally rule that the person who has the preponderance of evidence of ownership is the owner of the property

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following best describes actual notice?
option_a: It is notice published in a newspaper
option_b: It is knowledge one could have or should have obtained
option_c: It is notice explicitly stated in a legal document
option_d: It is knowledge received or imparted through direct experience
feedback: Actual Notice of ownership is completed by providing direct evidence of ownership, such as a valid will showing an inheritance of the property, or a deed, or by an examination of county records or by simply visiting the property to see who is in possession

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Which of the following best describes constructive notice?
option_a: It is notice published in a newspaper
option_b: It is knowledge one could have or should have obtained
option_c: It is notice explicitly stated in a legal document
option_d: It is knowledge received or imparted through direct experience
feedback: Constructive notice, or legal notice, is knowledge of a fact that a person could have or should have obtained. The foremost method of imparting constructive notice is by recordation of ownership documents in public records, specifically, title records

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Title can be voluntarily transferred by
option_d: sale contract, deed, and warrant of seizin
feedback: A living owner makes a private grant by means of a deed of conveyance, or deed. A private grant that occurs when the owner dies is a transfer by will

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The owner of a property creates a deed, leaving the name of the grantee blank, and puts it in a drawer, where it is forgotten for several years.  One day, a visitor finds the deed, fills in his name as grantee, and attempts to record it.  However, the owner will probably have no trouble disputing the validity of the deed because
option_a: the grantee was not competent at the time of delivery
option_b: there was no intent to deliver it, and no consideration was given
option_c: the grantee did not sign it at the time of execution
option_d: it was not accompanied by a transfer certificate
feedback: To be valid, a deed must be accompanied by valuable (monetary) or good (love and affection) consideration, and the grantor must declare before a notary or other authorized person that the grantor's identity and signature are genuine, and that the deed execution was a free, voluntary act

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: What is the function of recording a deed?
option_c: It gives constructive notice of ownership
option_d: It removes all prior recorded encumbrances
feedback: Recording is not necessary to make a deed valid. However, it is in the grantee's best interests to do so. Recording the deed gives the public constructive notice of the grantee's ownership

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The only clause that is actually required in a deed is the
feedback: The granting, or premises, clause is the only required clause. It contains the conveyance intentions; names the parties; describes the property; and indicates a nominal consideration

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: The type of deed that offers the grantee the fullest protection against claims to the title is the
feedback: The general warranty deed, or warranty deed for short, contains the fullest possible assurances of good title and protection for the grantee. The deed is technically a bargain and sale deed in which the grantor promises to defend against any and all claims to the title. The overall general warranty covenant is: "I own and will defend."

subject: A2 Real Property
question_text: Why would a property owner file a quiet title suit?
option_a: To force the grantor to defend the title against a third party claim
option_b: To terminate a co-ownership estate when one co-owner is unwilling
option_c: To keep the owner's name out of the title records
option_d: To have an encumbrance removed if the lienholder cannot prove its validity
feedback: If a party responsible for encumbering title refuses to quitclaim the interest, the owner may file a quiet title suit. This requires the lienor to prove the validity of an interest. If the defendant is unable to do so, the court removes the cloud by decree

subject: A2 Real Property
