question_text: Jones put his house on the market. Brown made a written purchase offer that Jones accepted in writing. When is the contract valid?
option_b: When Brown is notified of the acceptance
option_c: When the broker gets his fee
option_d: As soon as the signatures are notarized
feedback: Acceptance must be communicated to the offeror to create a valid contract.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A buyer paid $2,800 for an option to purchase a property within 90 days for $450,000. Within a month, the buyer made an offer to buy the property immediately for $425,000. Which of the following is true in this situation?
option_a: The buyer is in default of the option agreement
option_b: Both parties are in violation of contract law
option_d: The owner may accept the offer for $425,000
feedback: The owner can accept or reject a new offer; negotiation is allowed.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The buyer gives the owner a $2,500 payment and a written agreement stating that, on June 1 of the following year, the buyer will purchase the property for $475,000 cash or the owner may keep the money. This transaction is known as:
feedback: An option gives the buyer the right to purchase at a later date for a specified price.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The buyer has been held to be in default on a contract of sale. If buyer and seller had not agreed on liquidated damages, the seller could do which of the following?
option_a: Sue the buyer for compensatory damages
option_b: Require the buyer to find a substitute purchaser
option_c: Obtain a court order preventing the buyer from purchasing another property
option_d: Arrange for criminal proceedings against the buyer
feedback: Seller can sue for actual losses if buyer defaults without liquidated damages clause.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: When a contract contains provisions outlining what money penalties will be levied against the party that refuses to perform, the amount specified is known as:
feedback: Once the contract has agreed on whatever amount will serve as liquidated damages in case of nonperformance, no greater amount can be sought in the courts.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A prospect for the lease of a commercial property feels the need for adversarial representation and hires a broker to negotiate the lease on his behalf. What is the name of the contract entered into between the prospect and the broker?
feedback: A contract wherein the broker represents a buyer or tenant is called a buyer broker agreement or an exclusive right to represent agreement.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: What is an "executed contract?"
option_a: A contract in which all parties have fulfilled their promises, and performed the contract
option_b: A contract that is dead in the water
option_c: A contract that has been terminated by both the listing agent and the home owner
option_d: A contract terminated by either party
feedback: An executed contract is one in which all parties have fulfilled their promises in the original contract, and thus performed the contract.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following would usually occur in a sale-and-leaseback transaction?
option_a: The seller gets a return on the purchase in the form of rental payments
option_b: The rent that the seller pays is not income-tax deductible
option_c: The buyer keeps capital in inventories rather than in realty
option_d: The property is sold on the condition that the new owner lease it back to the seller at the time title passes
feedback: A sale-leaseback is usually used for creating cash flow. The owner sells the property and leases it back while continuing to use it.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Micah agrees to represent Dan in the sale of a property. The listing contract states that Micah will receive a fee of $25,000 from Dan at closing, regardless of the sales price. Is this a valid form of compensation?
option_a: No. The seller should pay on a basis of a stated percentage of contract price
option_b: Yes. Compensation can be determined in any number of ways, as long as the seller and broker agree
option_c: No. A flat fee exposes the seller to being cheated
option_d: Yes. The broker is supposed to charge what the traffic will bear
feedback: Compensation is negotiated between broker and seller and may be stated in many ways including percentage, flat fee, or by service.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: When part of an agreement is changed, or an agreement is replaced by a new one, it is called a:
feedback: Novation describes replacing an obligation with a new obligation or replacing a party to an agreement with a new party.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: When zoning changes occur, but owners are allowed to continue with non-conforming uses, this is a:
feedback: A grandfather clause allows the continuation of a previously lawful use even after zoning changes.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The basic purpose of a listing agreement is to authorize the broker to:
feedback: A listing agreement authorizes the broker to find a buyer for the owner's property in exchange for a commission.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: In which of the following situations could a broker receive no commission?
option_a: The broker proves that he is the procuring cause of the buyer in a net listing
option_b: The broker proves that he is the procuring cause of the buyer in an exclusive right to sell listing
option_c: The broker proves that he is the procuring cause of the buyer in an open listing
option_d: The broker proves that he is the procuring cause of the buyer in an exclusive agency listing between himself and the seller
feedback: In a net listing, if the broker brings in an offer at or below the agreed price, no commission is earned.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The act of replacing an existing contract with a new one.
feedback: Novation is the act of replacing an existing contract with a new one.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A family signs a lease to rent a house from January 1st to June 30th. This is considered a(n):
feedback: An estate for years is a lease for a fixed period with a definite termination date.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A buyer has entered into an agreement with more than one buyer's agent at the same time. She only owes compensation to one of the brokers if she uses their services. This arrangement is known as a(n):
feedback: An open buyer agency agreement is similar to an open listing seller agreement.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: One week after entering into an exclusive authorization and right to sell listing, the owner dies. His designated administrator decides not to sell the property. In this case:
option_a: the owner's heirs will be liable for the commission
option_b: the owner's estate will be liable for the commission
option_c: the owner's death terminated the listing contract
option_d: the administrator must sell for the full 90-day listing period
feedback: Death of the seller automatically terminates an exclusive right to sell listing.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Mr. Seller signs an open listing on his home with five different brokers. In this case:
option_a: the owner must pay the first broker to take the listing no matter who sells it
option_b: the owner must pay a full commission to all five brokers when it sells
option_c: each broker has an opportunity to earn the entire commission
option_d: the brokers will split the commission five ways regardless of who sells it
feedback: In an open listing, the first broker to secure a ready, willing, and able buyer earns the commission.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Under a lease, the leasehold interest lies in the:
feedback: A leasehold interest is the right to exclusive possession and use of real property for a fixed period of time held by the lessee (tenant).

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Under a lease, the leasehold interest lies in the:
feedback: A leasehold interest is the right to possession and use of property for a fixed period held by the lessee.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following requires real estate listings to be in writing?
feedback: The Statute of Frauds requires contracts for the sale of land, including listings, to be in writing.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: All of the following listings require that the broker be the "procuring cause" of the sale if he/she is to be entitled to a commission, EXCEPT:
option_c: there are no exceptions, all listings require proven procuring cause
option_d: an exclusive right to sell listing
feedback: Under an exclusive right to sell listing, the broker earns commission regardless of procuring cause.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Mr. Owner enters into an exclusive listing with Broker X. One week later, Broker X brings in a full-price offer, but Mr. Owner refuses the offer. Seeking an action of specific performance would be an option for:
option_a: both the buyer and the broker
option_d: neither the buyer nor the broker
feedback: Specific performance cannot be sought because the offer was refused; no contract exists.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Broker Jones takes an exclusive listing on a property owned by three different people. Broker Jones takes the listing contract to each of their places of business to get it signed. Broker Jones must:
option_b: give one copy to any of the three owners
option_c: give a copy to each owner when he/she signs or get them together in one place to sign at the same time
option_d: give a copy to the first owner who signs only
feedback: Broker must provide a copy to each owner or have all sign together at the same time.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A tenant signs a lease that stipulates that the lessee pays the property tax. This lease is probably a:
feedback: Net leases require the tenant to pay rent plus operating expenses such as taxes, insurance, utilities, and repairs.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: When creating a contract, consideration may be:
feedback: Consideration may be money, services, or a promise; it is something of value that induces a party to enter into a contract.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Salesperson Beth presented an offer to purchase from her buyer to the listing agent broker Smith. The offer included a $1,000.00 promissory note from the buyer as the earnest money deposit. Broker Smith should do which one of the following upon receipt of the offer?
option_a: Tell Beth that a promissory note could be acceptable for the deposit and present the offer to his seller
option_b: Tell Beth he won't present the offer as written to the seller
option_c: Tell Beth the note is not good enough because it did not come with a deed
option_d: Tell Beth that he cannot accept the offer without cash or a check for the deposit
feedback: A listing agent must present all written offers. A promissory note can be accepted if the seller agrees.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: During the term of an exclusive authorization and right to sell listing, the broker has his license revoked by the Real Estate Commission. To prove that he is entitled to a commission, he must prove all of the following, except:
option_a: that he was licensed at the time the commission was earned
option_b: that the listing was a legally binding agreement
option_c: that he was the procuring cause of the sale
option_d: that the buyer and seller agreed to the sale during the listing term
feedback: Under an exclusive right to sell listing, procuring cause does not need to be proven for commission entitlement.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: You enter into a contract with a person, not knowing they had been declared incompetent. The incompetence of one of the parties to this agreement would make the contract:
feedback: A contract with an incompetent party is generally voidable by the incompetent party.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A listing agreement is a(n):
option_c: contract to find a buyer for a property
feedback: A listing agreement is an employment contract, can be unilateral or bilateral, and authorizes the broker to find a buyer.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A tenant's five-year lease has expired but they continue to live on the premises and pay their rent monthly. If the landlord hasn't asked them to leave and continues to accept rent, this is considered a(n):
feedback: The lease continues from period-to-period; this is a periodic tenancy.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The Statute of Frauds outlines which contracts must be in writing to be enforceable through court action. Which of the following contracts would be enforceable?
option_a: An oral agreement to pay a broker a commission for the negotiation of an exchange of two businesses
option_b: A verbal agreement to secure a loan of $1,500
option_c: A verbal lease for six months
option_d: An exclusive listing taken orally by a broker to sell a single-family residence
feedback: Leases under one year do not require writing under the Statute of Frauds, so a verbal lease for six months is enforceable.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following is NOT essential to a contract?
feedback: Performance is not required for a valid contract; the essentials are capable parties, lawful object, offer and acceptance, and consideration.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Failure to perform any of the terms or conditions of a contract is called a(n):
feedback: A breach of contract is the violation of any terms or conditions without legal excuse, allowing remedies like damages, rescission, or specific performance.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A broker has a combination of a listing and an option on a property. She exercises the option to buy without disclosing to the seller that she has found a buyer at a higher price. The broker:
option_b: is attempting to make a secret profit.
feedback: In this situation, the broker exercised an option to buy, already knowing that she had a buyer on the hook that would pay her more, resulting in "secret profit." Secret profit refers to a broker making an undisclosed profit at the seller's expense. Brokers cannot conceal offers from buyers until after they have exercised the option- full and fair disclosure must be given to the seller. The common law duty of an agent to make no secret profit has been a fixture of modern jurisprudence, which means that engaging in conduct that creates secret profit can result in litigation.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Bob signs a contract under duress. By whom can the contract be voided?
feedback: Duress is a threat whereby a person is forced to do some act against their will. The person who was forced to perform under a threat has the right to void the agreement or let it stand.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A broker is going to take a listing on a church that is owned by an unincorporated association. In order to determine who has the proper authority to sign documents and represent the group, the broker should do which of the following?
option_a: Contact the head of the group who originally built the building
option_b: Contact the group members and arrange to have a meeting of the majority for a vote
option_d: Check the charter or bylaws of the group
feedback: Both incorporated and unincorporated associations are governed by bylaws or charters. The president of a corporation, by virtue of his/her office, does not have authority to act for the organization. The minister of a church does not have authority unless conferred by the bylaws.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following is NOT essential to all contracts?
feedback: The 4 essentials of a valid contract are capable parties, mutual consent, offer & acceptance and lawful object. Certain other contracts are required to be in writing (like real estate), but writing isn't essential to ALL contracts.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following is TRUE concerning promissory notes?
option_a: They are used as security for trust deeds
option_b: They are recorded at the county recorder's office
option_c: They are always used when real estate is sold
option_d: They are the evidence of the debt
feedback: The promissory note is the evidence of the debt. The trust deed is the security of the debt.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: An exclusive agency listing between a broker and owner incorrectly states 6 dollars rather than 6 percent as the commission payable to the broker for producing a buyer. If the owner sells the property himself, the broker is entitled to:
option_b: an amount to be determined by the real estate commission.
feedback: Because this is an exclusive agency listing, the owner may sell the property themselves without being liable for a commission.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A broker accepts an exclusive authorization and right to sell listing from a corporation. During the listing term, all of the officers of the corporation die. In this case:
option_b: the listing remains in full effect.
option_c: the listing must be acknowledged by the newly appointed officers.
option_d: the listing must be signed by the corporation's board of directors.
feedback: One of the main characteristics of a corporation is its perpetual existence- that is, corporations never die and only cease to exist when properly dissolved. Someone in the corporation's chain of command would have the authority to carry through with a sale, so the listing is unaffected.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A lease contains an assignment clause requiring the consent of the landlord. If the lessee assigns the lease without approval, the assignment is:
feedback: An assignment of a lease in violation of an anti-assignment restriction does not make the lease void, but does make it voidable at the lessor's discretion. When a lease has an assignment clause requiring the consent of the landlord, the landlord may not unreasonably or arbitrarily withhold consent, but consent must be given before the assignment becomes enforceable.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A tenant verbally agrees to lease a house for six months. The following month, the tenant notifies the owner that he has found a more desirable unit and will be moving out. The contract is:
option_d: invalid, since it was not in writing.
feedback: Oral leases for periods of less than one year are generally valid and enforceable.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A month-to-month lease is considered a(n):
feedback: A periodic tenancy is a lease that continues from period to period until one of the parties gives notice of termination. A periodic tenancy has no definite termination date.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: An agent must submit all new offers to the owner until:
option_b: escrow opens on a potential sale.
option_c: the broker decides there have been enough offers.
option_d: the agent decides the seller isn't interested.
feedback: Offers must be presented even after the seller has accepted an offer. It is a violation of an agent's fiduciary duties to willfully fail to present any written purchase offer prior to the closing of the sale, unless the agent is expressly instructed by the owner not to present any more offers (or an offer is patently frivolous). Depending on the terms of the agreement a seller may not be able to accept the offer during the escrow period.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A broker enters into an open listing with a seller, but fails to give a copy of the listing contract to the owner and also fails to include a termination date. The broker then presents an offer which is accepted. Which of the following statements is most applicable?
option_a: The broker is entitled to nothing
option_b: The exclusion of a termination date nullifies the contract
option_c: The exclusion of a termination date does not matter, so the broker is entitled to the stated commission
option_d: The broker is entitled to specific performance
feedback: Unlike an exclusive listing, an open listing need not contain a definite termination date. The listing terminates after a reasonable time, usually whatever is customary in the community. All other concurrent open listings are automatically cancelled by the sale. Also, it isn't necessary for the broker to give the owner a copy of the open listing at the time of signing.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Under an exclusive agency listing, a broker advertises a home for sale. The owner finds a buyer and sells the home prior to the expiration of the listing. The broker is entitled to:
feedback: Since the listing was an Exclusive Agency Listing, the seller may find their own buyer without paying a commission. This is different than an Exclusive Authorization and Right-to-Sell Listing, in which the listing broker is guaranteed a commission regardless of who procures the buyer.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A contract based on an illegal consideration is:
feedback: A contract must be legal in its formation and operation. Both its consideration and its object must be lawful, or the contract is void.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A contract based on an illegal consideration is:
feedback: A contract must be legal in its formation and operation. Both its consideration and its object must be lawful, or the contract is void.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The following are required to be in writing EXCEPT the:
feedback: The Statute of Frauds generally requires that all contracts for the sale of land or any interest therein be in writing. An RV (recreational vehicle) isn't considered land or real property, so selling it would not require a bill of sale to be in writing for the sale to be valid.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A broker has listed Mr. Applegate's property under an exclusive listing. He also has an option to purchase the property within 30 days. If after 27 days, the broker decides to buy the property, he must:
option_a: disclose all material facts known to him and to the seller.
option_b: All of the answer choices are correct.
option_c: inform the seller of any additional offers on the property.
option_d: disclose any profit he might make.
feedback: When a broker has listed a property under an exclusive listing agreement and holds an option to purchase that property, specific ethical and legal duties come into play if the broker decides to exercise their option to buy the property. While not directly related to the broker purchasing the property, in many jurisdictions, ethical rules require that a broker present all offers to the seller up until the point of sale. The broker must disclose all material facts that could affect the seller's decision about the sale or the value of the property. Transparency is key in maintaining trust and ensuring fairness in the transaction. Disclosure of any profit he might make is a critical component of treating the seller fairly, especially if the broker could gain financially from the transaction in ways not readily apparent to the seller. The need for these disclosures is rooted in the broker's fiduciary responsibility to the seller. This responsibility ensures that the seller makes an informed decision about the sale of the property, especially when the buyer is the broker themselves.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The transferee in an assignment of a lease is called the:
feedback: The assignor gives the assignment and remains secondarily liable under the lease. The assignee (transferee) takes over the assignor's interest and is primarily liable to pay rent to the original lessor. Remember the OR-EE rule- the suffix "or" is used to denote the person who performs an action, while the suffix "ee" is used to denote the recipient of that action.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Jack leases a single-family dwelling property from Mr. Michaels. After the lease agreement is signed and Jack moves in, Mr. Michaels insists that Jack sign an addendum to the lease agreeing to make substantial capital improvements to the property. Mr. Michaels' requirement could make the lease contract:
feedback: A lessor cannot change or add to the lease contract after it is signed, and a situation like this would put Jack under duress. If Mr. Michaels insists on the

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A lease contract is best described as
option_a: a temporary transfer of legal title
option_b: an instrument of conveyance of limited title
option_c: a conveyance of a possessory interest
option_d: a title conveyance in exchange for rent
feedback: A lease is both an instrument of conveyance and a contract between principal parties to uphold certain covenants and obligations. As a conveyance, a lease conveys an interest, called the leasehold estate, but does not convey legal title to the property

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: When a tenant acquires a leasehold estate through a lease, what does the property owner acquire?
feedback: In conveying the leasehold estate, the landlord acquires a leased fee estate, which entails the rights to: receive rent; re-possess the property following the lease term; and monitor the tenant's obligations to maintain the premises

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: When an owner leases her property, she temporarily relinquishes the right to
feedback: The legal essence of a valid lease is that it conveys an exclusive right to use and occupy a property for a limited period of time in exchange for rent and the return of the property after the lease term is over

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following happens when a leased property is sold?
option_a: The buyer acquires title subject to the lease
option_c: The lease expires within thirty days unless renewed
option_d: A new lease is automatically executed
feedback: The landlord may sell, assign, or mortgage the leased fee interest. However, transferring and encumbering the leased property do not extinguish the obligations and covenants of a lease. Buyers and creditors, therefore, must take their respective interests subject to the terms of the lease

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A landlord and tenant complete a one-year lease.  The following week, the landlord dies.  Which of the following is true?
option_a: The lease is cancelled and must be re-written
option_b: The tenant must continue to abide by all lease terms
option_c: The tenant may annul the lease at his or her option
option_d: The landlord's heirs may cancel the lease at their option
feedback: A valid lease creates obligations that survive the death of the landlord or tenant, with certain exceptions

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: If a lease does not state a specific ending date, when does it terminate?
option_a: Immediately, since it is an invalid lease
option_c: When either party gives proper notice
feedback: In the absence of an explicit term with beginning and ending date, a court will generally construe the lease to be a tenancy at will, cancelable upon proper notice

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: What is one important difference between a sublease and a lease assignment?
option_a: In an assignment, responsibility for the original lease is transferred completely to the assignee
option_b: In a sublease, the original tenant retains primary responsibility for performance of the original lease contract
option_c: A sublease does not convey any of the leasehold interest
option_d: A sublease conveys the entire leasehold interest
feedback: Subletting (subleasing) is the transfer by a tenant, the sublessor, of a portion of the leasehold interest to another party, the sublessee, through the execution of a sublease. The sublease spells out all of the rights and obligations of the sublessor and sublessee, including the payment of rent to the sublessor. The sublessor remains primarily liable for the original lease with the landlord. The subtenant is liable only to the sublessor

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following lease types conveys rights other than the rights to exclusive use and occupancy of the entire property?
feedback: The practice of leasing property rights other than the rights to exclusive occupancy and possession occurs most commonly in the leasing of water rights, air rights, and mineral rights

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following summarizes the general terms of a ground lease?
option_a: The landlord sells the ground to another, then leases it back
option_b: A tenant buys the landlord's ground, then leases the improvements
option_c: The landlord leases the ground floor of the building to a commercial tenant
option_d: The tenant leases the ground from the landlord and owns the improvements
feedback: The tenant leases the ground from the landlord and owns the improvements. This lease is for ground, only and the landlord does not own the building or other improvements that are on the land

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Among the usual remedies available to the injured party if tenant or landlord defaults on the terms of a lease is the right to
option_a: sue to compel performance of the contract
option_b: enter a lien against the other's estate
option_c: lock the other party out of the premises
option_d: apply the security deposit to legal expenses
feedback: In the event of a default, the damaged party may pursue court action, including suing for specific performance. A successful suit for specific performance compels the defaulting party to perform the contract obligation that was breached. For example, if a landlord fails to replace carpeting as promised in the lease, the tenant can sue to obtain the landlord's specific performance of installing a new carpet

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A lease automatically terminates under which of the following circumstances?
option_a: The tenant fails to pay rent
option_c: The tenant goes out of business
feedback: A foreclosure extinguishes all prior interests in a property, including a leasehold

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: One of the aims of the Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act is to
option_a: create a standard residential lease for use nationwide
option_b: force states to enact tenants' rights laws
option_c: establish uniform rental rates for each type of property
option_d: discourage the use of unfair and overly complex leases
feedback: The Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (URLTA) aims to equalize and standardize rights of landlord and tenant, protect tenants from unethical practices, and prevent unfair, complex leases and their enforceability

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A primary theme of the Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act is that both landlord and tenant
option_a: waive their rights to sue for damages
option_b: bargain with each other in good faith
option_c: give sixty days' notice of intent to terminate the lease
option_d: observe the Realtor's Code of Ethics
feedback: Under URLTA, a landlord and tenant must both bargain in good faith

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A lease is both an
option_a: instrument of conveyance and a contract
option_b: instrument of conveyance and a deed
feedback: A lease is both an instrument of conveyance and a contract between principal parties to uphold certain covenants and obligations. As a conveyance, a lease conveys an interest, called the leasehold estate, but does not convey legal title to the property

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A tenant wants to have a specific lease-expiration date in his lease.  Which of the following leasehold estates does this tenant want?
feedback: An estate for years has a specific lease term

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: In conveying the leasehold estate to the tenant, the landlord retains a
feedback: In conveying the leasehold estate, the landlord acquires a leased fee estate, which entails the rights to receive rent and re-possess the property following the lease term, and the duty to monitor the tenant's obligations to maintain the premises during the lease term

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Generally, a lease for a period exceeding one year must be in writing to be enforceable because of the
feedback: Generally, a lease for a period exceeding one year cannot be oral but must be in writing to be enforceable because of the Statute of Frauds

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: An oral lease is legally construed to be which of the following?
feedback: An oral lease or rental agreement is legally construed to be a tenancy at will, having no specified term

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: In a sublease, which of the following parties remains primarily liable for the original lease with the landlord?
feedback: The sublessor remains primarily liable for the original lease with the landlord

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following is true regarding option leases?
option_a: The lessor does not have to comply
option_b: The lessee does not have to exercise the option
option_c: The lessee must purchase the property
option_d: The lessee must renew the lease
feedback: Typical options are the right to renew the lease, buy the property, and lease additional adjacent space.  A tenant does not have to exercise an option, but the landlord must comply if the tenant does exercise it

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which one of the following lease agreements is typically used for retail properties?
feedback: A percentage lease allows the landlord to share in the income generated from the use of the property. A tenant pays percentage rent, or an amount of rent equal to a percentage of the tenant's periodic gross sales. Percentage leases are used only for retail properties

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Residential leases differ from commercial of leases in that
option_a: the lease term is shorter, typically one or two years
option_b: the lease terms are longer, ranging up to 24 years
option_c: the lease clauses are typically negotiable
option_d: the lease typically requires tenant improvements to meet particular forms of usage
feedback: Residential leases differ from commercial and other types of lease in that the lease terms are shorter, typically one or two years.  Typical commercial leases are for five years or more

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following statement describes an important feature of a commercial lease?
option_a: The lease is primarily used to lease raw land
option_b: The lease clauses are fairly standard from one property to the next
option_c: The lease typically prohibits tenant improvements
option_d: The lease is usually long term ranging from 5-15 years
feedback: long term, ranging from 5-15 years, require tenant improvements to meet particular usage needs, negotiable lease clauses due to the financial magnitude of the transaction, default can have serious financial consequences; therefore, lease clauses must express all points of agreement and be very precise

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: In the event of a default, the damaged party may pursue court action, including suing for all of the following EXCEPT
feedback: Although condemnation may terminate a lease by the taking of a property by eminent domain, it has nothing to do with a breached lease and therefore is not a remedy for default

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A successful suit for specific performance compels the defaulting party to perform the contract obligation that was breached. For example,
option_a: if a tenant defaults, the landlord can claim all or portions of the deposit
option_b: if a landlord defaults in repairing the water heater, the tenant can purchase a new heater and charge the landlord for the repair
option_c: if a landlord prohibits pets and a tenant violates this requirement, the landlord can charge the tenant a monthly fine
option_d: if a landlord fails to replace carpeting as promised in the lease, the tenant can sue to obtain the landlord's specific performance of installing a new carpet
feedback: A successful suit for specific performance compels the defaulting party to perform the contract obligation that was breached. For example, if a landlord fails to replace carpeting as promised in the lease, the tenant can sue to obtain the landlord's specific performance of installing a new carpet

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: In a tenancy for years, a lease may terminate for which of the following causes?
feedback: A foreclosure extinguishes all prior interests in a property and will terminate a lease agreement

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Identify the model law enacted as a blueprint for state laws to regulate leasing and management practices of landlords with residential properties
option_b: The Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
option_c: The Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (URLTA)
feedback: The Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (URLTA) is a model law enacted as a blueprint for state laws to regulate leasing and management practices of landlords with residential properties

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Under the Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (URLTA), a tenant is obligated to do which of the following?
option_a: bargain in good faith with the landlord
option_c: provide the landlord with a signed lease agreement
feedback: The tenant's obligations. Under URLTA, includes the following: bargain in good faith, maintain the condition of the leased premises, abide by (legitimate) rules and regulations of the building, refrain from abusing or causing destruction to the property, limit uses to those approved, avoid unduly disturbing other tenants

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following types of leasehold estate lacks a specific term?
feedback: Three of the four principal types of leasehold estate are: the estate for years, which has a specific lease term; the estate from period-to-period, where the lease term of a specific period automatically renews; and the estate at will, which has no specified lease term. The fourth principal type, the estate at sufferance, is a tenancy without consent that therefore also has no specific term

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A landlord generally has the right to enter the leased premises
option_c: only at the time rent is due
option_d: only thirty days prior to lease expiration
feedback: A tenant has the sole right to occupy and use the premises without interference from outside parties, including the landlord. The landlord may enter the premises for specified purposes such as inspections, but the interference must be reasonable and limited. In addition, the landlord can do nothing outside of the lease's express provisions that would impair the tenant's enjoyment of income deriving from use of the premises

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Among a tenant's primary obligations in a lease is
option_a: compliance with the rules and regulations of the building
option_b: payment for any alterations to the leased space
option_c: recording the lease in title records
option_d: satisfying the municipality's requirements for an occupancy permit
feedback: The lease defines the tenant's obligations, which principally are to pay the rent on time; maintain the property's condition; and comply with the rules and regulations of the building

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Among a landlord's primary obligations in a lease is
option_a: insuring the tenant against loss of personal property
option_b: providing required building support and services
option_c: guaranteeing that a fair rent is being charged
option_d: insuring the property for the value of the leasehold
feedback: The lease defines the landlord's obligations, which principally are to provide the necessary building support and services, and maintain the condition of the property. It is the tenant's responsibility to obtain insurance and to determine the fairness of the rent

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: While a one-year lease is in effect, the tenant dies of a sudden illness.  In this situation,
option_b: the tenant's estate has the option of canceling the contract
option_c: the landlord can record a lien against the leased fee interest
option_d: the tenant's estate is still obligated under the lease
feedback: A valid lease creates obligations that survive the death of the landlord or tenant, with certain exceptions. A tenant's estate remains liable for payment of rent if the tenant dies; the landlord's estate remains bound to provide occupancy despite the landlord's death

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Three college friends rent a house together, and all three sign the lease.  After a month, the friends argue and two of them move out.  Which of the following is true of the rent obligation?
option_a: The remaining tenant is responsible for the full rent obligation
option_b: The remaining tenant is responsible for one third of the rent obligation
option_c: No one is responsible for the rent obligation.  The landlord loses
option_d: The departing tenants have no further rent obligation
feedback: Multiple tenants who sign a single lease are jointly and severally responsible for fulfilling lease obligations. Thus, if one renter abandons an apartment, the other renters remain liable for rent

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A tenant transfers a portion of the leasehold interest to another party.  The document that accomplishes this transfer is a(n)
feedback: Subletting (subleasing) is the transfer by a tenant, the sublessor, of a portion of the leasehold interest to another party, the sublessee, through the execution of a sublease. The sublease spells out all of the rights and obligations of the sublessor and sublessee, including the payment of rent to the sublessor. The sublessor remains primarily liable for the original lease with the landlord. The subtenant is liable only to the sublessor

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The type of lease in which the tenant pays all of the operating expenses of the property is a(n)
feedback: A net lease requires a tenant to pay for utilities, internal repairs, and a proportionate share of taxes, insurance, and operating expenses in addition to rent. In effect, the landlord "passes through" actual property expenses to the tenant rather than charging a higher rent level. Net leases vary as to exactly what expenses the tenant is responsible for. The purest form of net lease requires tenants to cover all expenses, including major repairs and property taxes

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The type of lease in which the landlord pays all of the operating expenses of the property is a(n)
feedback: A gross lease, or full service lease, requires the landlord to pay the property's operating expenses, including utilities, repairs, and maintenance, while the tenant pays only rent. Rent levels under a gross lease are higher than under a net lease, since the landlord recoups expense outlays in the form of added rent

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The type of lease in which the tenant pays rent based on income generated at the leased premises is a(n)
feedback: A percentage lease allows the landlord to share in the income generated from the use of the property. A tenant pays percentage rent, or an amount of rent equal to a percentage of the tenant's periodic gross sales

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following circumstances is the most likely scenario for a ground lease?
option_a: A PUD developer wants to acquire a necessary parcel that separates two parcels she already owns
option_b: A farmer wants to expand his productive acreage without buying more land
option_c: A fast food company wants to place a restaurant in an existing building without buying either land or improvement
option_d: A railroad wants to build a bridge over a privately owned access road
feedback: Ground leases are primarily used when an owner wishes to lease raw land to an agricultural or mining interest; when unimproved property is to be developed and the owner wants to retain ownership of the land; when the developer or future users of the property do not want to own the land; or when the owner of an improved property wishes to sell an interest in the improvements while retaining ownership of the underlying land

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The basic feature that distinguishes a rights lease from other types of leases is
option_a: the long lease period that is typical
option_b: the reversion of all rights to the owner at the end of the lease term
option_d: the absence of exclusive occupancy and possession from the rights leased
feedback: The practice of leasing property rights other than the rights to exclusive occupancy and possession occurs most commonly in the leasing of water rights, air rights, and mineral rights

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: By suing for specific performance, a leasehold tenant might
option_a: force a landlord to provide improvements that were promised but not delivered
option_b: change the obligation to pay rent as it is stated in the lease
option_d: cancel the lease and receive damages for the landlord's default
feedback: A successful suit for specific performance compels the defaulting party to perform the contract obligation that was breached. For example, if a landlord fails to replace carpeting as promised in the lease, the tenant can sue to obtain the landlord's specific performance of installing a new carpet

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following situations describes constructive eviction?
option_a: A court order expels a tenant because of tenant default
option_b: A tenant is forced out of the premises because the landlord has declared bankruptcy
option_c: A tenant vacates the premises and declares the lease canceled because the landlord has made the premises uninhabitable
option_d: A tenant changes the locks overnight and does not allow the landlord access to the premises
feedback: In an instance where the landlord's negligence or disruptive action has rendered the property unoccupiable, a tenant may vacate the premises and declare that the lease is cancelled by default

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: What is the difference between a tenant's vacating leased premises and abandoning the premises?
option_a: Vacating the premises terminates the tenant's lease obligations
option_b: Abandonment implies that the tenant shows no intention of fulfilling the lease obligations
option_c: Vacating the premises terminates the landlord's lease obligations
option_d: Vacating the premises is automatically a tenant default
feedback: If a tenant abandons a leased property and demonstrates no intention of fulfilling the obligations of the lease, the landlord may retake possession and pursue legal recourses for default. It is important to note that vacating leased premises does not in itself constitute abandonment, and certainly not if the tenant continues to abide by the lease obligations

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A periodic leasehold or a tenancy at will can usually be terminated by
option_c: a sublease, with the lessor's approval
feedback: In the absence of an explicit term with beginning and ending date, a court will generally construe the lease to be a tenancy at will, cancelable upon proper notice

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Under the Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act, if a lease does not state a clear expiration date, the lease is regarded as
feedback: URLTA sets standards for improving oral, vague, or unbalanced lease agreements. In the case of an unclear lease expiration date, the lease is generally deemed to be a periodic tenancy

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: If a tenant is seeking a remedy for an alleged landlord default and the landlord disputes the claim, the Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act allows the tenant to
option_b: abate the rental payments while the dispute is settled
option_c: make the rental payments to a court impound pending resolution
option_d: deny the landlord access to the premises
feedback: Tenants generally are not released of liability for rent during a dispute. Rents, however, may be paid to a court impound pending judgment

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act generally does not apply to
option_a: a residence that is under a contract for deed
option_c: a unit in an apartment building that has fewer than ten units
option_d: a duplex in which the owner occupies one of the units
feedback: State laws based on URLTA generally do not apply to transient occupancies, such as hotel and motel rentals, proprietary leases in cooperatives, or to occupancy in a residence that is under a contract for deed

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: An important legal feature of a contract is
option_a: it represents a "meeting of the minds."
option_b: it must use precise wording in a document
option_d: it can be created only by an attorney
feedback: A contract is an agreement between two or more parties who, in a "meeting of the minds," have pledged to perform or refrain from performing some act. A valid contract is one that is legally enforceable by virtue of meeting certain requirements of contract law. If a contract does not meet the requirements, it is not valid and the parties to it cannot resort to a court of law to enforce its provisions

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: According to contract law, every valid contract is  also
feedback: In terms of validity and enforceability, a court may construe the legal status of a contract in one of four ways:  •valid  •valid but unenforceable  •void  • voidable

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The guardian for a mentally incompetent party enters into an oral contract with another party to buy a trade fixture. This contract
option_c: must be in writing to be valid
feedback: A contract involving a minor as a party may be either void or voidable. If the contracting party is representing another person or business entity, the representative must have the legal authority to contract

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A prospective homebuyer submits a signed offer to buy a house with the condition that the seller pays financing points at closing. The seller disagrees, crosses out the points clause, then signs and returns the document to the buyer. At this point, assuming all other contract validity items are in order, the status of the offer is
option_a: an accepted offer, therefore a valid contract
feedback: By changing any of the terms of an offer, the offeree creates a counteroffer, and the original offer is void

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: As part of a construction contract between a contractor and a buyer, the contractor promises to complete construction by November 20. This promise can be construed as
option_a: competency on behalf of the contractor
feedback: Valuable consideration can be something of tangible value, such as money, or something a party promises to do or not do

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: An unscrupulous investor completes a contract with a buyer to sell a property the investor does not own. The sale contract for this transaction
feedback: The content, promise, or intent of a contract must be lawful. A contract that proposes an illegal act is void

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A homeowner encourages an agent to aggressively persuade a buyer to purchase his house by overinflating historical appreciation rates. The agent and the seller agree that 25% annual appreciation would work, even though this figure is four times actual rates. The pitch succeeds, and the seller accepts the buyer's resulting offer. This contract is
feedback: The parties must create the contract in good faith as a free and voluntary act. A contract is thus voidable if one party acted under duress, coercion, fraud, or misrepresentation

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The statute of limitations requires that parties to a contract who have been damaged or who question the contract's provisions
option_a: must act within a statutory period
option_b: must select a specific, limited course of action for recouping their losses
option_c: must arbitrate prior to taking court action
option_d: must wait a statutory period before they may take legal action
feedback: The statute of limitations restricts the time period for which an injured party in a contract has the right to rescind or disaffirm the contract. A party to a voidable contract must act within the statutory period

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The purpose of the Statute of Frauds is to
option_b: require certain conveyance-related contracts to be in writing
option_c: nullify oral leases and listing agreements
option_d: eliminate fraud in real estate contracts
feedback: The Statute of Frauds requires that certain contracts must be in writing to be enforceable. Real estate contracts that convey an interest in real property fall in this category, with the exception that a lease of one year's duration or less may be oral

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A seller immediately accepts a buyer's offer but waits eight days before returning the accepted document to the buyer. Meanwhile, the offer has expired. Which of the following is true?
option_a: The buyer is bound to the contract since it was accepted immediately
option_b: The buyer has no obligations to the seller whatsoever
option_c: The buyer may not rescind the expired offer
option_d: The seller may sue for specific performance
feedback: If an offer contains an expiration date and the phrase "time is of the essence," the offer expires at exactly the time specified

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A buyer agrees to all terms of a seller's offer except price. The buyer lowers the price by $1,000, signs the form, and mails it back to the seller. At this point, the seller's offer
feedback: By changing any of the terms of an offer, the offeree creates a counteroffer, and the original offer is void

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A buyer submits an offer to a seller. Two hours later, the buyer finds a better house, calls the first seller, and withdraws the offer. Which of the following is true?
option_a: The buyer may not revoke the offer in such a short period of time
option_b: The first seller may sue the buyer for specific performance
option_c: If the seller accepted the offer, the buyer must perform
option_d: The original offer is legally extinguished
feedback: An offer may be revoked, or withdrawn, at any time before the offeree has communicated acceptance. The revocation extinguishes the offer and the offeree's right to accept it

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Real estate contracts that are not personal service contracts
option_d: are exempt from the statute of frauds
feedback: A real estate contract that is not a personal contract for services can be assigned to another party unless the terms of the agreement specifically prohibit assignment

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following contracts must be in writing to be enforceable?
feedback: A contract may be in writing or it may be an oral, or parol, contract. Certain oral contracts are valid and enforceable, others are not enforceable, even if valid. For example, most states require listing agreements, sales contracts, and leases exceeding one year to be in writing to be enforceable

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A good example of a unilateral contract is
feedback: An option is an example of a unilateral contract: in an option-to-buy, the party offering the option (optionor) promises to sell a property if the optionee decides to exercise the option. While the potential buyer does not have to buy, the owner must sell if the option is exercised

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A contract is discharged whenever
option_d: the parties agree to their respective promises
feedback: A contract terminates when fully performed by the parties

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A contract may be defensibly terminated without damages if
option_a: it is abandoned by one party
option_c: it is deemed to be valid
feedback: An otherwise valid contract can be canceled if it is not possible to perform

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A landlord suddenly terminates a tenant's lease in violation of the lease terms. The tenant takes action to compel the landlord to comply with the violated terms. This is an example of a suit for
feedback: A suit for specific performance is an attempt to force the defaulting party to comply with the terms of the contract

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: To be legally enforceable, a valid contract must include
option_c: the legal description of the property
feedback: Contract validity requirements include competent parties, mutual consent, valuable consideration, legal purpose, voluntary act of good faith

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A property owner signed a listing agreement under duress.  If the seller contests the legitimacy of the signed contract, the contract may be considered
feedback: A voidable contract appears to be valid but is subject to rescission by the party who is deemed to have acted under some kind of disability.  Examples are victims of duress, coercion, or fraud

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which statute restricts the time period for which an injured party in a contract has the right to rescind or disaffirm the contract?
feedback: The purpose of the statute of limitations is to restrict the timeline for which an injured party in a contract has the right to rescind or disaffirm the contract. A party to a voidable contract must act within the statutory period costs

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Contracts to buy, sell, exchange or lease interests in real property must be in writing to be enforceable. This falls under the
feedback: The statute of frauds requires that certain contracts must be in writing to be enforceable.  Real estate contracts fall under this category, with the exception of a one year or less lease term

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A prospective client received a written agreement that included the property price, commission fees, terms, and the designated agent assigned to service the scope of work. This type of contract is known as
feedback: An express contract is one in which all the terms and covenants of the agreement have been stated and agreed to by all parties, whether verbally or in writing

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A buyer agreed to purchase a property for $250,000, and the seller agreed to sell the property and convey the title at closing.  This agreement is known as
feedback: A bilateral contract is one in which both parties promise to perform their respective parts of an agreement in exchange for performance by the other party

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following is an example of an executed contract?
option_b: A counteroffer from the seller in a sales contract
option_c: A sales contract contingent on the loan approval
option_d: A sales contract prior to closing
feedback: An executed contract is one that has been fully performed and fulfilled whereby neither party bears any further obligation

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The aggrieved parties in a breached contract elect rescission as their legal remedy. In this situation the contract is
option_a: canceled, and the breaching party must pay for caused damages
option_b: canceled, and parties are returned to pre-contract conditions
option_c: canceled, and the breaching party retains the earnest money
option_d: canceled, and the aggrieved party files a suit for performance
feedback: This cancels the contract and returns the parties to their pre-contract condition, including refunding any monies exchanged

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Three days before the closing, the seller decided not to sell the property. In this situation, the buyer may
option_c: file a suit for specific performance
feedback: A suit for specific performance is an attempt to force the defaulting party to comply with the terms of the contract. The most common example is in a defaulted sale or lease contract whereby the buyer or seller wants the court to compel the defaulting party to go through with the transaction

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following is an example of revocation?
option_a: The sales contract is canceled by the mutual consent of the parties
option_b: The parties fail to perform contractual obligations
option_c: A new contract is created to replace a canceled contract
option_d: A seller cancels the listing agreement without the broker’s consent
feedback: Revocation is cancellation of the contract by one party without the consent of the other. For example, a seller may revoke a listing to take the property off the market

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: When a contract states the total amount due to a damaged party in the event of a breach, the compensation is known as
feedback: When a contract states the total amount due to a damaged party in the event of a breach, the compensation is known as liquidated damages. If the contract does not specify the amount, the damaged party may sue in court for unliquidated damages

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A suit for _________________ is an attempt to force the defaulting party to comply with the terms of the contract
feedback: A suit for specific performance is an attempt to force the defaulting party to comply with the terms of the contract. Specific performance suits occur when it is difficult to identify damages because of the unique circumstances of the real property in question

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A forfeiture requires the breaching party to give up something, according to the terms of the contract. For example,
option_a: The broker may have to return the commission earned if the sales transaction fails to close
option_b: A buyer who defaults on a sales contract may have to forfeit the earnest money deposit
option_c: The seller may have to pay discount points on behalf of the buyer’s loan approval
option_d: The sales contract was canceled by mutual agreement of the parties
feedback: A forfeiture requires the breaching party to give up something, according to the terms of the contract. For example, a buyer who defaults on a sales contract may have to forfeit the earnest money deposit

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following conditions terminates a contract?
feedback: Performance terminates a contract. A contract terminates when fully performed by the parties. It may also terminate for: partial performance, if the parties agree, sufficient performance, if a court determines a party has sufficiently performed the contract, even though not to the full extent of every provision

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Any of the following actions or circumstances can terminate an offer EXCEPT
feedback: Any of the following actions or circumstances can terminate an offer: acceptance: the offeree accepts the offer, converting it to a contract, rejection: the offeree rejects the offer, revocation: the offeror withdraws the offer before acceptance, lapse of time: the offer expires, counteroffer: the offeree changes the offer, death or insanity of either party

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Electronic contracting is made possible by which of the following federal laws?
feedback: Electronic contracting is made possible by the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA) and the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-Sign), which are federal laws. UETA, which has been accepted in most states, provides that electronic records and signatures are legal and must be accepted. E-Sign makes contracts, records, and signatures legally enforceable, regardless of medium, even where UETA is not accepted

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: What is rescission?
option_a: The act of withdrawing an offer before it has been accepted
option_b: The act of nullifying a contract
option_c: The act of declaring a contract unenforceable
option_d: The act of modifying the terms of an offer
feedback: Parties to a contract may rescind a contract by mutual consent, or a damaged party may rescind the contract unilaterally. This act of rescission cancels the contract and returns the parties to their pre-contract condition, including the refunding of any monies already transferred

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The valuable consideration necessary to make a contract valid must be
option_c: something specifically offered in exchange for something else
option_d: something of equal value with whatever is received in exchange
feedback: A contract must contain a two-way exchange of valuable consideration as compensation for performance by the other party. The contract is not valid or enforceable if just one party provides consideration. Valuable consideration may be something of tangible value, such as money, or something the party promises to do. It may also be something intangible that a party must give up

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The necessary condition of mutual consent may be found lacking in a contract if
option_a: the two parties did not discuss every possible interpretation of the contract's provisions
option_b: the offer which was accepted is vague
option_c: one party did not have the legal authority to sign the contract
feedback: Mutual consent, also known as offer and acceptance and meeting of the minds, requires that a contract involve a clear and definite offer and an intentional, unqualified acceptance of the offer. In effect, the parties must agree to the terms without equivocation. A court may nullify a contract where the acceptance of terms by either party was partial, accidental, or vague

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A contract that conveys an interest in real estate must
option_a: contain a legal description of the property
option_b: be written on a form approved by the state bar association
option_d: be recorded within three days to be enforceable
feedback: In addition to satisfying the requirements of a valid contract in general, a contract that conveys an interest in real estate must be in writing, contain a legal description of the property, and be signed by one or more of the parties

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A party sells a summer cottage appraised at $300,000 to a stranger for $150,000.  The seller's family wants to challenge the validity of the sale contract.  The most apparent weakness of the contract that they might be able to attack is the
option_b: insufficiency of the consideration exchanged for the property
option_c: lack of consent on the part of the seller's family
option_d: lack of good faith on the part of the buyer
feedback: Grossly insufficient consideration, such as $50,000 for a $2 million property, may invalidate a contract on the grounds that the agreement is a gift rather than a contract.

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: How much time does a seller have to accept a buyer's offer?
option_a: Forty-eight hours from the time of the offeror's signing of the offer
option_b: Twenty-four hours from the time of the offer's delivery to the seller
option_c: As long as the seller wants
option_d: A "reasonable" time, or until the expiration date on the offer
feedback: If an offer contains an expiration date and the phrase "time is of the essence," the offer expires at exactly the time specified. In the absence of a stated time period, the offeree has a "reasonable" time to accept an offer

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A buyer submits an offer to a seller and then dies in a car accident.  Before learning of the buyer's death, the seller accepts the offer. Which of the following is true?
option_a: The seller can force the buyer's estate to go through with the purchase
option_b: The buyer's death terminated the offer
option_c: The seller must make a new offer with the same terms to the buyer's heirs
option_d: The buyer's heirs have the option of enforcing the contract
feedback: Any of the following actions or circumstances can terminate an offer: acceptance; rejection; revocation; lapse of time; counteroffer; or death or insanity of either party

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following contracts can be assigned to another party?
option_c: A contract for the sale of a house
option_d: An employment contract between a broker and a salesperson
feedback: A real estate contract that is not a personal contract for services can be assigned to another party unless the terms of the agreement specifically prohibit assignment

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: An implied contract may be deemed to exist if
option_a: the parties do not disavow an express contract that has expired
option_b: the parties act is if there is a contract
option_c: an offering party does not receive written notice that the offer has been rejected
option_d: the parties promise to perform their part of the agreement if the other party performs
feedback: An implied contract is an unstated or unintentional agreement that may be deemed to exist when the actions of any of the parties suggest the existence of an agreement

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following is an executory contract?
option_d: An option to buy after it is exercised
feedback: An executory contract is one in which performance is yet to be completed. A sales contract prior to closing is executory: while the parties have agreed to buy and sell, the buyer has yet to pay the seller and the seller has yet to deed the property to the buyer

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A bilateral contract is one in which
option_a: both parties promise to do something in exchange for the other party's performance
option_c: both parties agree to perform a service
option_d: one party promises to do something if the other party performs first
feedback: A bilateral contract is one in which both parties promise to perform their respective parts of an agreement in exchange for performance by the other party. An example of a bilateral contract is an exclusive listing: the broker promises to exercise due diligence in the efforts to sell a property, and the seller promises to compensate the broker when and if the property sells

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A homeseller signs a listing agreement with a broker and the next week decides not to sell and revokes the listing.  Which of the following is true?
option_a: The seller has no contractual obligations to the broker
option_b: The contract remains in full force until the expiration date
option_c: The broker may have a claim for damages
option_d: The seller cannot sign a listing agreement with another broker
feedback: A damaged party may sue for money damages in civil court

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A breach of contract is
option_a: a termination of the contract by the mutual consent of the parties
option_b: financial damage suffered by a party because another party has nullified a contract provision
option_c: a lawsuit to force a party to discharge the contract
option_d: the failure of a party to perform according to the terms of the contract
feedback: A breach of contract is a failure to perform according to the terms of the agreement

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: In a suit for damages by reason of default, the damaged party may claim liquidated damages if
option_a: the contract stated a specific amount due to a damaged party
option_b: the contract stated that a defaulting party could not profit from the contract
option_c: the defaulting party owns property that can be foreclosed to pay the damages
option_d: the contract stated that a damaged party could take legal action to enforce the contract
feedback: When a contract states the total amount due to a damaged party in the event of a breach, the compensation is known as liquidated damages. If the contract does not specify the amount, the damaged party may sue in court for unliquidated damages

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Several buyers are competing for the last available home in a desirable new subdivision. One buyer calls the owner-developer directly on the phone and offers $10,000 over and above the listed price. The developer accepts the offer. At this point,
option_a: the parties have a valid, enforceable sale contract on the home
option_b: the parties have completed a verbal, executory contract
option_c: the parties may not cancel their contract
option_d: the developer could not entertain other offers on the property
feedback: A sale contract is executory: the signatories have yet to perform their respective obligations and promises. A contract for the sale of real estate is enforceable only if it is in writing

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: An owner completes a contract to sell her property. Before closing, the seller runs into financial trouble and assigns the contract to her principal creditor. The buyer cries foul, fearing the property will be lost. Which of the following is true?
option_a: The buyer can sue the assignee to disallow the illegal assignment
option_b: The buyer can take legal action against the assignor
option_c: The assignor has completed a legal action
feedback: Either party to a sale transaction can assign the sale contract to another party, subject to the provisions and conditions contained in the agreement

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: During the executory period of a sale contract, the buyer acquires an equitable title interest in the property. This means that
option_a: the buyer can potentially force the seller to transfer ownership
option_b: both parties own the property equally
option_c: if contract contingencies are not met, the buyer takes legal title
option_d: the buyer owns equity in the subject property to the extent of the funds deposited in escrow
feedback: A sale contract gives the buyer an interest in the property that is called equitable title, or ownership in equity. If the seller defaults and the buyer can show good faith performance, the buyer can sue for specific performance, that is, to compel the seller to transfer legal title upon payment of the contract price

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: The purpose of an escrow account is to
option_a: entrust deposit monies to an impartial fiduciary
option_b: enable the principals to access the funds in escrow without interference from the other party
option_c: ensure that the broker receives her commission
option_d: prevent the buyer from withdrawing the offer
feedback: The escrow holder acts as an impartial fiduciary for buyer and seller. If the buyer performs under the sale contract, the deposit is applied to the purchase price

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A sale contract contains an open-ended financing contingency: if the buyer cannot obtain financing, the deal is off. Six months later, the buyer still cannot secure financing. Which of the following is true?
option_a: The seller may cancel the contract, since it can be ruled invalid
option_b: The buyer can continue indefinitely to seek financing, and the seller's property must remain off the market
option_c: The seller must return the buyer's deposit
option_d: The seller can force a lender to commit to a loan under fair financing laws
feedback: A contingency that is too broad, vague, or excessive in duration may invalidate the entire contract on the grounds of insufficiency of mutual agreement. To avoid problems, the statement of a contingency should be explicit and clear, have an expiration date, and expressly require diligence in the effort to fulfill the requirement

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: In the event of a buyer's default, a provision for liquidated damages in a sale contract enables a seller to
option_a: sue the buyer for the anticipated down payment
option_b: force the buyer to quitclaim equitable title
option_c: sue the buyer for all liquid assets lost as a result of the default
option_d: claim the deposit as relief for the buyer's failure to perform
feedback: If a buyer fails to perform under the terms of a sale contract, the breach entitles the seller to legal recourse for damages. The usual remedy is forfeiture of the buyer's deposit as liquidated damages, provided the deposit is not grossly in excess of the seller's actual damages

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: Which of the following best characterizes a conventional sale contract?
feedback: A sale contract is executory: the signatories have yet to perform their respective obligations and promises. Upon closing, the sale contract is fully performed and no longer exists as a binding agreement.  To be enforceable, a sale contract must:  •be validly created (mutual consent, consideration, legal purpose, competent parties, voluntary act)  •be in writing  • identify the principal parties (bilateral)  •clearly identify the property, preferably by legal description  •contain a purchase price  •be signed by the principal parties

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A due-on-sale clause in a sale contract puts parties on notice that
option_a: the full price of the property is due the seller at closing
option_b: any loans surviving closing become immediately payable
option_c: all of the seller's debts must be retired before or upon closing
option_d: third-party loans surviving closing may be accelerated by the lender
feedback: The mortgage agreement may or may not state that the seller's loan will be accelerated by the lender and called due on sale. The due-on-sale clause in the sale contract merely stipulates that the buyer and seller recognize the possibility that loans that survive the closing may be called due

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A sale contract may specifically deal with tax withholding responsibility if the seller is a foreigner. What is this responsibility?
option_a: The buyer must withhold 15% of the purchase price at closing for the seller's capital gain tax payment
option_b: The buyer must withhold 15% of the purchase price at closing for the buyer's capital gain tax payment
option_c: The seller must withhold 15% of the buyer's funds as a deposit on the buyer's capital gain tax
option_d: The seller must withhold 15% of the sale price to pay the seller's capital gain tax
feedback: The seller acknowledges that the buyer must withhold 15% of the purchase price at closing if the seller is a foreign person or entity and forward the withheld amount to the Internal Revenue Service. Certain limitations and exemptions apply

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: An important legal characteristic of an option-to-buy agreement is that
option_a: the potential buyer, the optionee, is obligated to buy the property once the option agreement is completed
option_b: the optionor must perform if the optionee takes the option, but the optionee is under no obligation to do so
option_c: the contract can be executed at no cost to the optionee
feedback: An option-to-buy is an enforceable contract in which a potential seller, the optionor, grants a potential buyer, the optionee, the right to purchase a property before a stated time for a stated price and terms. In exchange for the right of option, the optionee pays the optionor valuable consideration

subject: B1 Contracts
question_text: A tenant has an option-to-purchase agreement with the landlord that expires on June 30. On July 1, the tenant frantically calls the landlord to exercise the option, offering the apology that she was busy with a death in the family. Which of the following is true?
option_a: Since options contain grace periods, the landlord must sell
option_b: The tenant loses the right to buy, but can claim the money paid for the option from the landlord
